author_headline: Co-founder @ Moderne | Java Champion | founder OpenRewrite - OSS automated refactoring
post_text:
Ouch. That's cold. 6 months after the "nobody looks at the IDE anymore" trend, one missing bang and it's like CC doesn't even know that IntelliJ exists, haha.
The reality is though, I'm looking more and more at the IDE. At a minimum, I'm finding I need to cut absurdly verbose comments everywhere. The comment pattern that I feel is particularly detrimental is the one that basically records what the code USED to do, or did in some iteration towards the final design, not what it CURRENTLY does. So there is a token record of discarded ideas that jointly serve as a kind of gravity well sucking a model back to those discarded ideas.
Strangely the better the model gets, the more convoluted the code is getting. A lot of folks are saying it doesn't matter, that engineers should move up the stack and become product managers and designers and even that models should stop writing in human-centric programming languages altogether.
I went through a phase where I used Zed just because it was faster to ! start from a CC session. It's faster though because it's far less capable. So I've boomeranged a bit. Slow is smooth, smooth is fast.
… more

variants.comment:
The "gravity well" framing is sharp — comments that record discarded paths are genuinely worse than no comments, because they look like intent to the model.

My read on it though is slightly different: the problem isn't the comments per se, it's that the model has no way to distinguish "this comment describes current intent" from "this comment describes a rejected iteration." Both look like context. Pruning the bad ones manually is the symptom fix; the structural issue is that code — with all its accumulated commentary — is still the source of truth the model reads. If the spec were the source of truth and code were regenerable from it, the gravity well disappears: you just regenerate from clean specs instead of inheriting the scar tissue.

What's your take on whether IDE-side hygiene can keep up with the velocity at which an agentic session accumulates that cruft? It feels like a treadmill that gets faster the more capable the model becomes.
variants.rationale: Agrees with the gravity-well framing then reframes: the structural issue is code-as-source-of-truth, not comment hygiene.

variants.comment:
The observation that better models produce more convoluted code is counterintuitive but tracks with what I've been seeing too.

The framing I'd put on it: the more capable the model, the more faithfully it reproduces everything in context — including the discarded paths recorded in those comments. So you get a higher-fidelity rendering of all the noise, not just the signal. The model isn't confused; it's doing exactly what it's supposed to do with the context it has.

Which makes me wonder: is the right response to clean the context, or to reconsider what gets treated as context at all? At some point, keeping code (plus its accumulated commentary) as the primary artifact the model reads might be the category error — not the comment hygiene.
variants.rationale: Picks up "better model = more convoluted code" and asks whether code-as-context itself is the category error.

variants.comment:
The boomerang from Zed back to IntelliJ makes sense — faster to start doesn't mean faster to finish.

Curious about the OpenRewrite angle here though: is there a version of this where automated refactoring becomes part of CI hygiene for AI-native teams? Not hand-editing stale comments in the IDE one by one, but treating "comments that describe what the code used to do rather than what it currently does" as a detectable pattern — and pruning them on merge, the same way you'd run a linter. Feels like it could be written as a recipe. Has anyone in the OpenRewrite ecosystem tried formalizing that pattern?
variants.rationale: Engages Jonathan on his own turf (OpenRewrite) — could stale-comment pruning be a CI recipe?

slack_summary: Argues AI-generated code accumulates "gravity well" comments recording discarded ideas — and that better models paradoxically produce more convoluted code; he's back to leaning on the IDE.

author_headline: Doer of things. (Prev: Google, IDX, Firebase, Fabric (acq by Google), Twitter, Crashlytics (acq by Twitter), Zynga, Thoughtworks, Intel) | Engineer | Investor
post_text:
I ran an experiment trying to show LLMs are bad at writing formal specs.
I was wrong (mostly). But it revealed where the real bottleneck is.
When I asked current models to write formal specifications in Dafny for small functions, the specs caught bugs about as well as generated test suites did, once they verified at all.
The catch is "once they verified." Only about half the spec attempts did. And that was a bare setup: single shot, no tools, no chance to read the verifier error and try again. So it's a floor, not a ceiling.
The interesting part is which tasks failed. Not the algorithmically hard ones. The ones where the model first had to formalize a concept before it could even state the property.
So I ran a cleaner test. Same requirement, two setups:
→ Model invents the domain from scratch (what an account is, what conservation of money means), then writes the spec
→ I hand it a small verified library that already defines those things, and it just writes the spec on top
Two results, both directional (small samples):
→ Giving the model the formalized domain raised correct specs from ~0.67 to ~0.77. On a money-ledger conservation task specifically: 0.25 to 0.92.
→ Using a newer model did not help. Across three recent Claude versions, the from-scratch number stayed flat at ~0.67.
This lines up with VeriSoftBench, where frontier models solve roughly a third of proofs on actual software repositories, versus ~90% on math backed by Mathlib.
Mathematics has Mathlib. The whole domain, formalized once, reusable.
Software doesn't. Neither does finance, healthcare, or law.
If that's the real bottleneck, the company that wins at vertical formal verification isn't the one waiting for a bigger model. It's the one that builds and owns the formalized domain.
At Unbound, we believe that the model becomes the cheap layer on top. Whoever owns the domain formalization owns the hard, durable part.
Caveats: small samples, one model vendor, three bugs in my own scoring before I trusted the numbers. This is a hypothesis I now hold more firmly, not a proof.
Full writeup with methodology, per-domain breakdown, and the scoring mistakes in the article. Thanks for the review Ranjan Rajagopalan.
Links in first comment
… more

variants.comment:
The 0.25 → 0.92 jump on the money-ledger task when you hand the model a pre-verified domain library is the most striking number in this post.

One thing I'm curious about: who builds the first verified domain library for a new vertical? Bootstrapping it from scratch seems to put you back in the same problem — the model has to formalize from scratch to produce the initial library, which is exactly the failure mode you measured. Is the answer "a small team of humans formalizes it manually once," or is there a way to get the model to help with that first step without running into the same wall?
variants.rationale: Targets the cold-start gap: who builds the first verified domain library, since bootstrapping hits the same failure mode?

variants.comment:
The Mathlib parallel is the right frame — math solved the bottleneck by formalizing the domain once, reusably, and it unlocked a step change in proof automation.

But Mathlib took decades and a dedicated community to reach critical mass, and mathematical axioms don't ship product updates. Software domains change quarterly — sometimes weekly. What's the theory for keeping the formalized domain in sync when the underlying business logic shifts? A stale or incomplete domain library for finance might produce specs that verify cleanly against the wrong model of the world, which seems worse than a failed verification you can catch.
variants.rationale: Accepts the Mathlib analogy but exposes domain mutability — software domains change; math axioms don't.

variants.comment:
The flat ~0.67 across three Claude versions is the most honest number here — it cleanly rules out "wait for a bigger model" as a strategy.

One thing I'd push back on slightly: the framing assumes the missing layer is formalized domain types (what an account is). My read, from working on behavior-driven specs, is that the harder gap is formalized behavior — what the system must do in each state, not just what the data looks like. Domain types help a lot, and your numbers show it. But without formalized behavioral postconditions as the source of truth, you can still end up with specs that verify against a correct-looking domain model while missing the actual invariants the business cares about. Curious whether your verified library covered behavioral postconditions or mainly data structure definitions.
variants.rationale: Petro's True BDD thesis: formalized behavior (postconditions), not just domain types, is the durable source of truth.

slack_summary: Ran an experiment on LLMs writing formal specs (Dafny): handing the model a pre-verified domain library lifted correctness dramatically, and bigger models didn't help — the durable moat is owning the formalized domain.

3

variants.comment:
The framing that data foundation is the real blocker — not the model — matches what we've seen in every domain where AI gets deployed against legacy systems.

Curious what you've found is the actual shape of "not AI-ready" in health plans specifically. Is it mostly a data quality problem (missing values, inconsistent coding), a schema problem (no shared ontology across payers and providers), or an access/permissions problem (the data exists but nobody can legally move it)? My guess is the hardest cases aren't any one of these — they're all three at once, which means "fix the data foundation" is actually three separate workstreams with different stakeholders. What's your experience?
variants.rationale: Sharpens the vague "not AI-ready" claim into quality vs schema vs access — three workstreams with different stakeholders.

variants.comment:
The point that AI-ready data is quietly becoming the biggest AI investment holds up — every serious AI deployment eventually hits the data wall.

The question I keep coming back to: is "AI-ready data" actually an infrastructure problem, or is it a power problem? In healthcare, the entities that generate the richest data (providers, payers, PBMs) are often not the entities that benefit most from sharing it. You can build the cleanest data layer in the world, but if the incentive to expose it doesn't exist, the pipeline stays empty. What's your read — are the orgs you work with stuck on the technical side, or is the political/contractual side the harder unlock?
variants.rationale: Reframes AI-ready data as a power/incentive problem, not just infrastructure — who owns the data vs who benefits from sharing.

variants.comment:
"Data foundation before AI" is right in the general case, and the healthcare graveyard is full of pilots that died on dirty data.

That said — some of the clearest healthcare AI wins I've seen written up have come from severely constraining the problem scope rather than cleaning the underlying data first. Prior auth triage on a single payer's claim schema. Discharge summary extraction from one hospital's Epic instance. In both cases, the scope was narrow enough that "good enough" data was actually good enough. The data foundation thesis holds at scale — but I'd push back on it as a prerequisite for any value. Sometimes the right move is to find the one workflow where your data is already clean and prove the model there, then use that win to fund the data cleanup. Curious if you've seen that pattern work, or if you'd argue it creates more technical debt than it's worth.
variants.rationale: Counters "data foundation as prerequisite for any value" — narrow-scope wins on already-clean data can fund the cleanup.

slack_summary: Argues the real blocker for healthcare AI isn't model capability but the data foundation underneath — "AI-Ready Data" is the emerging competitive advantage (newsletter promo).

author_headline: Making AI Multiplayer | Co-Founder of SpecStory
post_text:
The people who actually ship with agents do three unglamorous things well: they understand the harness they're driving, they set up the repo so the agent doesn't wander, and they never accept "done" without verification.

I'm teaching one free lesson on each this month, with two people I admire and learn from. In August we put all three together in my in-depth course.

Here's the run of show: All free live and hands-on:

⚡ July 14 · Build a Definition of Done for Claude Code and Codex
Your agent says "done," and it's a guess. Build a tiny harness that keeps it working until a real check passes. This is the verification pillar.
Register: https://lnkd.in/eWUpnrwB

⚡ July 20 · Build an Environment-Aware AI Agent from Scratch, with John Berryman (author of Prompt Engineering for LLMs, early GitHub Copilot)
The "agent harness" is just a loop you can build yourself. Once you see it, you can shape it. This is understanding the thing you steer.
Register: https://lnkd.in/eJCTF7Sv

⚡ July 27 · Make Your Repo Agent-Ready: Rules, Docs, Reviews, with Dan Gerlanc (past CTO of .txt, makers of Outlines)
Most agent failures are the repo's fault, not the model's. Encode your conventions, keep your docs in sync, and set up adversarial reviews. This is the setup pillar.
Register: https://lnkd.in/eCBi8x-K

This is the spine of Hardcore Agentic Engineering, my new Cohorted course I'm running with Sean Johnson and Jake Levirne on Maven starting in August: https://lnkd.in/eYEbE2S2

If you've been meaning to get serious about shipping with agents, this is the on-ramp. They're free, and they fill up.

variants.comment:
The "repo's fault" framing for the July 27 session landed — most teams I talk to assume agent failures are model failures and don't look at what they handed the agent to work in.

Curious what the clearest signal is that a repo is causing the problem vs the harness or the prompt. Is it usually missing conventions (nothing in CLAUDE.md, no style guide), stale docs that contradict the actual codebase, or something else? Trying to understand what the actual diagnostic looks like before a team reaches Dan's session.
variants.rationale: The "repo’s fault" claim is the most contrarian, least-examined pillar — a specific diagnostic question opens the conversation without disputing the thesis.

variants.comment:
The verification pillar is the one I keep coming back to. "Your agent says done and it's a guess" — exactly right. But the next question is: verified against what oracle?

Tests pass — great. But if the tests were generated by the same agent that wrote the code, you're running in a circle. The harness only closes the loop if there's something external it checks against: a spec, a written acceptance criterion, a human-approved definition. Curious how the July 14 session addresses that — does it start from existing tests, or does it walk through what you write the harness against first?
variants.rationale: The verification pillar has an unexamined blindspot (the oracle problem when tests are AI-generated) — a provocative, on-topic question useful to attendees.

variants.comment:
The three pillars hold up. I'd add that all three get harder when the upstream spec is vague — if the agent doesn't have a clear, agreed definition of what "done" actually means for the feature (not just for the task), verification catches code errors but misses intent errors. The harness stops the wandering, but it doesn't know where you were trying to go.

Not a critique of the course structure — the three pillars are real and learnable. Just noticing that teams who struggle with all three at once often have a spec problem upstream that makes each pillar wobble independently.
variants.rationale: Extends the three-pillar model by surfacing the upstream spec layer as a prerequisite — positions Petro’s thesis without pitching True BDD by name.

slack_summary: Announcing three free hands-on lessons (Jul 14/20/27) on shipping with agents — verification, building a harness, and making your repo agent-ready — as the on-ramp to his Maven course.

author_headline: CTO at CircleCI
post_text:
For decades, we treated CI as the first place code got validated.
Write code → Push it → Let CI tell you what broke
That workflow made sense when humans were writing code.
Today, we're asking delivery systems built for human-paced development to validate machine-paced development.
The data suggests the teams that adapt first are pulling away. The top 5% now ship 9x more validated code than the median team, up from 8x last quarter. The difference isn't that they're generating dramatically more code. It's that they know much sooner whether a change is ready to move forward.
Every time an agent waits several minutes for CI to report a lint error or a failing unit test, you've created unnecessary work. The agent reloads context, reprocesses information, and tries again. Those retries add up quickly. In our modeling, a 50-developer team operating at agentic pace can spend roughly $900,000 a year just getting code through validation.
And most of that cost comes from finding routine problems too late.
The days of using CI to discover lint errors is behind us. It's now where we prove that software is ready for production.

variants.comment:
The 9x vs median framing lands well — it reframes the agentic advantage as a feedback latency story, not a code generation speed story, which is the more honest diagnosis.

The $900K figure is described as modeling. Curious what the key inputs are: is the bulk of that cost coming from token spend on context reload (re-priming the agent after each retry), wall-clock developer wait time, or compute on the CI side itself? The answer probably changes which layer of the stack is worth fixing first.
variants.rationale: The $900K modeling figure is the most interrogatable claim — asking what drives it is a genuine technical question that adds value without being hostile.

variants.comment:
The reframe from "CI = discovery" to "CI = proof" is the right one. Catching lint errors in CI when an agent is looping is roughly the equivalent of doing quality control at the shipping dock.

The part I'd push on: if CI is now the production-readiness gate, something upstream has to absorb what CI used to catch. Pre-commit hooks and local test runners partially fill it, but they're still synchronous blockers in the agent loop. Is the actual answer a new class of tooling, or is this an argument that agents need to run their own validation before they push — and if so, what enforces that they do?
variants.rationale: The post identifies the problem clearly but stops short of naming what replaces CI’s current role — pushing on that gap is the most useful contribution.

slack_summary: Argues that at agentic pace, CI shifts from where you discover bugs to where you prove code is production-ready — with slow feedback loops costing a 50-dev team ~$900K/year.

author_headline: VP Product | Enterprise AI, SaaS Platforms & Technology Leadership | Former CTO Domain Group
post_text:
A couple of years ago, I pushed every product team in my portfolio to build an AI feature.  I wanted people to engage with the technology.
Something interesting happened... The more we built, the more obvious it became that AI wasn't behaving like a feature at all.  It was behaving like a capability that could touch every workflow, every process, and every job to be done.
That led me to a simple conclusion:
Your customers didn't buy your software to use your software.  They bought it to get a job done.
The winners in the next phase of AI won't be the companies with the most AI features.  They'll be the companies that understand their customers' work deeply enough to remove it.
I've put together some thoughts on agents, orchestration, context, cognitive surrender, and why I think the future of software may be less software.
I'd be interested to hear whether you're seeing similar things.
#ProductStrategy #ProductManagement #AgenticAI #FutureOfWork #Innovation #Leadership

variants.comment:
The framing of AI as capability rather than feature lands exactly right — and it took most teams an embarrassing amount of time to arrive there.

I'd push back gently on "cognitive surrender" though. Surrender implies giving something up passively. What I see happening in the teams that actually get value from agents is something more deliberate: they're moving cognitive ownership upward — from code to spec, from implementation to intent. The job stays human; the execution becomes derived. That's not surrender, it's a redistribution of where the thinking lives. Whether that redistribution holds up when the agent misunderstands the job is a separate and harder question.
variants.rationale: Counters Cohen's 'cognitive surrender' framing with Petro's cognitive-ownership-moves-up thesis.

variants.comment:
The distinction between "AI as feature" and "AI as capability" is the right one to make.

I saw the JTBD version of this failure years before AI made it faster: a client of mine spent $200K building a mobile app for people struggling with weight — gamified healthy eating, real technical quality. Shut it down two months after launch. A year later, a competitor launched the same core idea with slightly different execution and got to millions of users. The difference wasn't the software; it was that the competitor had set up a feedback loop between the job to be done and what they were actually building. My client built the feature. The competitor built the outcome. AI doesn't fix this gap — it just lets you produce the wrong thing three times faster. The companies that win will be the ones that understand the job before they touch the agent, not the ones that prompt hardest.
variants.rationale: ReSelf client JTBD failure story mirrors Cohen's feature-vs-outcome thesis.

variants.comment:
Agreed that the winners will understand customers' work deeply enough to remove it — that's a sharper framing than most of the "AI-native" talk I see.

One tension your post leaves open: who holds that deep understanding once agents are doing most of the work? The risk I keep thinking about isn't "less software" — it's less institutional knowledge about why the job exists the way it does. If the agents execute and the humans mostly review outputs, the understanding can quietly hollow out. And then the next time the job changes, nobody in the room knows it well enough to tell the agent what to do differently. Curious whether you see orchestration design as part of the answer here, or whether this is more of an organizational question than a technical one.
variants.rationale: Surfaces institutional-knowledge atrophy blindspot Cohen's framing leaves open.

slack_summary: Argues AI is a capability not a feature — winners understand customer jobs deeply enough to remove work, so future software may be less software.

author_headline: Founder/CTO | PhD in AI/ML | 2x Exits (Sysomos, Aislelabs) | Built & Scaled Teams to 100+ | Hands-On, Customer-Facing Engineering Leader
post_text:
Software engineer == editor-in-chief of machine-written code?
🔥 Unpopular opinion from someone shipping with coding agents daily: the hard part of engineering leadership got harder, not easier.
🤖 Agents write the code. But taste — what to build, what "good" looks like, when to say no — doesn't compress. If anything, cheap code means more of it gets written, and the cost of weak technical judgment compounds faster.
📰 The role of a senior engineer is quietly becoming: editor-in-chief of machine-written code.
Agree or overblown? 👇

variants.comment:
The "cost of weak technical judgment compounds faster" — that part I'd sign without hesitation.

Where I'd push back is the "editor-in-chief of machine-written code" framing itself. It still positions code as the artifact that matters. If anything, the trajectory points somewhere more radical: the senior engineer becomes the person responsible for the spec holding together under regeneration, not for the code that comes out of it. When rebuilding gets cheap enough, editing the generated code is the wrong layer to be working at — you go back and change what drove the generation. The edit happens upstream of the code.

The role is changing, I agree. But if the frame stays "editor of code" — even machine-written code — we're probably still anchoring to the artifact that's becoming disposable.
variants.rationale: True BDD thesis (code is regenerable, spec is source of truth) counters the 'editor of code' frame.

variants.comment:
The "taste doesn't compress" framing is exactly right, and I don't think it's overblown at all.

One thing I keep coming back to: if cheap code means more of it gets written and the cost of weak judgment compounds faster, what happens when the weak judgment now lives in the spec or the prompt rather than in the code? Code review used to be the mechanism that surfaced bad technical calls — someone read the diff and caught the mistake before it shipped. When the source of truth shifts upstream to behavioral specs and agent instructions, that review mechanism no longer sits in front of the bad judgment. It sits downstream, in the generated output, which most teams are reviewing less carefully precisely because an agent wrote it.

What replaces code review as the safeguard against weak judgment in a spec-driven team?
variants.rationale: Extends the thesis: where does the safeguard against weak judgment live once code review isn't the gate?

variants.strategy_label: Ask a clarifying question rooted in the topic
variants.comment:
Agree that the hard part got harder — shipping with coding agents daily makes this obvious pretty fast.

Curious which judgment you're pointing at specifically when you say "weak technical judgment compounds faster." Is it judgment about what to build — the product direction, what to say no to — or judgment about how to instruct the agent: spec quality, architectural constraints, behavioral definitions? I ask because those two compound through completely different feedback loops. Bad product judgment used to show up in a product that nobody wanted, months later. Bad spec judgment shows up in a generated codebase that looks fine until you try to change something — and by then you've got layers of AI-produced code built on top of a shaky foundation. The skill gap and the fix look pretty different depending on which one you mean.
variants.rationale: Teases apart product-direction judgment vs spec/instruction judgment — Petro's expertise.

slack_summary: Argues coding agents made engineering leadership harder — taste and technical judgment don't compress, so senior engineers become editors-in-chief of machine-written code.

post_text:
Software engineer == editor-in-chief of machine-written code?
🔥 Unpopular opinion from someone shipping with coding agents daily: the hard part of engineering leadership got harder, not easier.
🤖 Agents write the code. But taste — what to build, what "good" looks like, when to say no — doesn't compress. If anything, cheap code means more of it gets written, and the cost of weak technical judgment compounds faster.
📰 The role of a senior engineer is quietly becoming: editor-in-chief of machine-written code.
Agree or overblown? 👇
… more
25
7
​

variants.comment: The "editor-in-chief" framing resonates — taste really doesn't compress. But I'd push on one thing: the metaphor positions the senior engineer downstream of the code, cleaning up after the agent. What I keep noticing is the real judgment point is upstream — in the spec, not the review. If the behavior description is sharp, the agent produces something worth editing. If it isn't, you're not editing, you're rewriting with extra steps. So the new skill might not be editorial so much as spec authorship — and that's a different muscle than most senior engineers have ever had to develop.
variants.rationale: Agrees with the core (taste doesn't compress) but redirects the metaphor — senior judgment is upstream in the spec, not downstream in code review, matching Petro's TrueBDD thesis.

variants.comment: The point about compounding judgment costs is the sharpest thing in here — agreed completely. One thing I keep sitting with: if "what to build" and "what good looks like" are the non-compressible parts, and agents can now produce 10x more code per day, does the math work out to something like "one senior engineer per ten junior-plus-agent pairs"? Or does the judgment demand scale differently — because the editor-in-chief model assumes senior engineers stay useful in proportion to volume, and I'm not convinced that holds linearly.
variants.rationale: Pulls out an unexamined assumption — whether judgment scales linearly with code volume — a sharp non-hostile provocation for a founder/CTO.

variants.comment: The framing of "editor-in-chief" is apt, and the point about compounding weak judgment is something I haven't seen articulated this cleanly. Curious what the practical form of "taste" looks like for you when output volume explodes — are you catching failure modes earlier in the spec or requirements stage, or primarily at the code review stage? I ask because building with coding agents daily, I've found the most expensive mistakes happen before the agent writes a line, not after.
variants.rationale: Acknowledges the framing, then asks where taste actually fires — upstream spec vs downstream review — grounding Petro's practitioner credibility.

slack_summary: Argues senior engineers are becoming editor-in-chief of machine-written code — agents write code but taste and judgment don't compress.

author_headline: Founder & CEO, Dapple | Building the Enterprise OS Cloud | The Operating System for Enterprise AI
post_text:
Every infrastructure cycle has a defining abstraction.
Mainframes abstracted hardware.
VMware abstracted servers.
AWS abstracted data centers.
AI needs its own abstraction layer.
Buying GPUs is the easy part.
Operating AI across clouds, private infrastructure, sovereign environments, and enterprise security requirements is where the complexity begins.
That's the layer we're building at Dapple.
Not another GPU cloud.
The operating layer for enterprise AI.
Here's a clip from AI in the AM where we unpack why this matters.
… more
9
​
1

variants.comment:
The history-of-abstractions framing is clean — and the sovereignty piece is the one I hadn't seen foregrounded before in this conversation.

Genuine question: when you operate across sovereign environments with different data residency rules, does the abstraction layer need to be stateless by design, or does state management end up being where the real complexity lives? Curious whether you see that as a configuration problem or an architectural one.
variants.rationale: Surfaces the stateless-vs-stateful design question as a concrete unaddressed variable in Dapple's multi-sovereign thesis, requiring a real technical answer.

variants.comment:
Agreed that the operating layer is where complexity begins — buying GPUs is genuinely the easy part.

Here's what I keep wondering: each prior abstraction (VMware, AWS) eventually became a lock-in vector — the thing that was supposed to free you from the layer below locked you into the layer above. What's the design choice you're making at Dapple that stops the enterprise OS from becoming the new infrastructure dependency they can't escape in five years?
variants.rationale: Uses the abstraction-becomes-lock-in pattern from infra history to push Dapple's thesis into a sharper, under-examined place without being hostile.

variants.comment:
The operating-layer framing maps onto something I ran into building BankAI — a self-hosted GenAI system for a German bank, pre-ChatGPT.

The GPU procurement was easy. What consumed 80% of the engineering effort was the surrounding scaffolding: air-gapped deployment, data residency, audit logging that satisfied German banking regulators, integration with identity systems the bank had run for 15 years. None of that was model work. It was exactly the operating layer you're describing. When you're selling into enterprise security requirements, the model is almost a detail compared to everything wrapped around it.
variants.rationale: Anchors acknowledgment in a real BankAI experience showing the operating-layer complexity from inside enterprise AI deployment, validating the thesis with specifics.

slack_summary: Every infra cycle has a defining abstraction; AI needs its own operating layer across clouds, sovereign, and enterprise security — what Dapple is building.

author_headline: CEO at Vercel
post_text:
🏁 I animated the token 💰 spend race, from ~lifetime Vercel AI Gateway usage, which aggregates trillions of tokens from millions of developers a month.
Fascinating to see the fluctuations among the labs, Anthropic's dominance, and the rise of open weight AI.
… more
299
25
13

variants.comment:
Anthropic's dominance in the spend race is the number that stands out most to me here — especially given how aggressive the pricing competition has been over the last year.

What's your read on what's actually driving it? From where I sit, watching teams choose models for production work, it feels less like raw benchmark scores and more like trust in reliability and consistency across long-context tasks. But I'm curious whether the Gateway data gives you any signal on that — or is spend share mostly a function of which model the dev reached for first and never switched off?
variants.rationale: Rauch claims Anthropic's dominance but leaves the driver unexplained — a specific question about trust/reliability vs first-mover lock-in pulls out a genuinely interesting variable.

variants.comment:
The rise of open-weight AI in the spend chart is the part I keep staring at.

The natural end state of that curve is developers running capable-enough models locally or on their own infra — which means gateway traffic doesn't grow proportionally with overall token consumption. At what quality threshold do you think open-weight models start pulling meaningful volume away from the hosted providers in your data? Or is the gateway still mostly where open-weight traffic lands too, just routed differently?
variants.rationale: The open-weight rise is the sharpest blindspot in the post — pushing on what it means for gateway volume is a provocative framing directly relevant to Vercel's business.

variants.comment:
The fluctuations among labs are the interesting part to me — a year ago the conventional wisdom was that the top models would converge on quality and price would decide everything. The spend data you're showing suggests differentiation is stickier than that.

My hypothesis: developers don't switch models mid-project even when a cheaper alternative pulls ahead on benchmarks, because the switching cost (re-tuning prompts, different failure modes, different context handling) is high enough that the benchmark delta has to be significant before it's worth it. Does the Gateway data give you any signal on how long incumbency lasts after a new model ships?
variants.rationale: Contrasts the year-ago commoditization narrative against what the spend data shows, proposes a switching-cost hypothesis, invites Rauch to confirm or correct.

slack_summary: Animated the token-spend race across AI labs from Vercel AI Gateway data — highlights Anthropic's dominance and the rise of open-weight models.

post_text:
For decades, we treated CI as the first place code got validated.
Write code → Push it → Let CI tell you what broke
That workflow made sense when humans were writing code.
Today, we're asking delivery systems built for human-paced development to validate machine-paced development.
The data suggests the teams that adapt first are pulling away. The top 5% now ship 9x more validated code than the median team, up from 8x last quarter. The difference isn't that they're generating dramatically more code. It's that they know much sooner whether a change is ready to move forward.
Every time an agent waits several minutes for CI to report a lint error or a failing unit test, you've created unnecessary work. The agent reloads context, reprocesses information, and tries again. Those retries add up quickly. In our modeling, a 50-developer team operating at agentic pace can spend roughly $900,000 a year just getting code through validation.

variants.comment:
The framing of CI shifting from "discovery" to "proof" is sharp — that distinction reframes the whole toolchain conversation.

But if routine checks leave CI, where do they live? Pre-commit hooks and local linters have existed for decades and most teams underuse them. The gap you're describing isn't really about CI's timing — it's that validation responsibility never got pushed early enough in the first place, even for human developers. Agentic pace just makes the cost of that gap legible in dollars.

What's your read: is this a CI redesign problem, or a "validation should have always happened earlier" problem that agents are just finally forcing teams to take seriously?
variants.rationale: Questions whether the real fix is CI itself or where validation responsibility sits — a sharper reframe

variants.strategy_label: Clarifying question rooted in the topic
variants.comment:
The $900K estimate for a 50-dev team operating at agentic pace cuts through the hand-wavy "agents are expensive" conversation with actual numbers.

Curious what assumptions drive that model most — is it primarily context reload cost (tokens plus latency per retry), or is it the wall-clock wait that blocks the agent from parallelizing other work during the CI queue? The two lead to pretty different architecture fixes: one pushes you toward faster, smarter in-process checks; the other toward CI that can report back incrementally rather than after a full run. Wondering which lever your data says moves the needle more.
variants.rationale: Teases out which cost driver dominates the $900K model, pointing to a concrete architecture conversation

variants.comment:
The 9x validated code throughput for top-5% teams, up from 8x last quarter, is a tighter compression than I'd have expected in a single quarter.

Worth noting that the Accelerate research (Forsgren et al.) showed a similar stratification pattern in DORA elite vs low performers — but that gap took years to widen meaningfully, not quarters. If it's moving this fast now, my guess is the separating variable isn't tooling adoption speed at all — it's whether the team restructured their validation architecture first, then adopted agents, vs. layering agents on top of the old CI pipeline and hoping. Would be curious whether your data segments those two paths.
variants.rationale: References Forsgren/DORA to question what the separating variable actually is

author_headline: Co-Founder & CTO Familiar
post_text:
P.S. "No AI generated slosh, think and write please."
It's quite sad that I need to add this postscript to pretty much every email, Slack message or communication that I send these days.
What most people don't realise: writing forces you to think, and thinking forces you to write better. If you can think, you can write. If you can't think, you can't write.
If you're outsourcing writing to AI, you're outsourcing thinking too.
The reason why we'll need UBI in the future is not because AI will replace jobs, it's because most people's brain and ability to think is atrophying at the speed of light right now.
Don't let this happen to you. Make sure you write and think every day.
… more
21
3
1

variants.comment:
The writing-forces-thinking loop is real — I've seen it both ways in my own work.

The part I'd push on slightly: "AI-generated writing" and "outsourced thinking" aren't the same thing, though they often travel together. Someone who prompts AI, reads the output, argues with it, restructures it, and ends up somewhere different from what it produced — they used AI as a drafting layer but kept the thinking. Someone who accepts the first output and hits send — they did outsource. From the outside both look identical.

The atrophy risk you're describing is real, but the cause isn't the tool, it's the posture. Which makes your P.S. understandable, but I suspect the people who actually need it are also the ones who won't notice it matters.
variants.rationale: Separates AI-generated writing from outsourced thinking — the cause is posture, not the tool

variants.comment:
The framing of writing-as-thinking is something I keep coming back to — it's one of the cleaner arguments against the "just use AI for everything" crowd.

The UBI claim is what I'd love to stress-test though. Is there actual evidence of cognitive atrophy at scale, or is this pattern-matching from what we see in our own networks? The people who read posts like this and add a P.S. to their emails are, by definition, not the ones who switched their thinking off. I wonder if the atrophy risk is real but unevenly distributed — not a tide that lifts all boats down, but a gap that widens between people who still do the work and people who stopped. Which might be a more uncomfortable conclusion than UBI.
variants.rationale: Stress-tests the UBI/cognitive-atrophy claim — the weakest part of a strong post

variants.strategy_label: Ask a clarifying question rooted in the topic
variants.comment:
The observation about needing that P.S. on every communication resonates — something has clearly shifted in what people consider acceptable to send without editing.

Curious what the actual signal is for you: is it the writing quality itself that's dropped, or is it something more subtle — that the ideas behind the writing got shallower? Because those are different problems. Bad writing from AI is visible and fixable. Shallow thinking dressed in fluent prose is harder to catch, and that version of outsourcing might be the one worth actually worrying about.
variants.rationale: Distinguishes surface writing quality from depth of ideas — a variable he hasn't addressed

slack_summary: Argues that outsourcing writing to AI means outsourcing thinking, and warns cognitive atrophy — not job loss — is the real reason we'll need UBI.

author_headline: Founder & CEO, Dapple | Building the Enterprise OS Cloud | The Operating System for Enterprise AI
post_text:
Every infrastructure cycle has a defining abstraction.
Mainframes abstracted hardware.
VMware abstracted servers.
AWS abstracted data centers.
AI needs its own abstraction layer.
Buying GPUs is the easy part.
Operating AI across clouds, private infrastructure, sovereign environments, and enterprise security requirements is where the complexity begins.
That's the layer we're building at Dapple.
Not another GPU cloud.
The operating layer for enterprise AI.
Here's a clip from AI in the AM where we unpack why this matters.
… more
10

1

variants.comment:
The "buying GPUs is the easy part" line lands because it's exactly what we discovered the hard way.

When building BankAI — a self-hosted GenAI for a German bank, pre-ChatGPT — we thought the hard part was the model. It wasn't. The model was maybe 20% of the effort. The other 80% was: data never leaves the sovereign boundary, here's the audit trail for every inference, the model has to run inside an air-gapped environment, and by the way, five different security teams need to sign off before it touches anything real. We were building that operating layer by accident, without a name for it.

The "operating layer" framing is exactly right — and the market will probably need several attempts to get it right, the same way VMware took years to become the default answer to server sprawl.
variants.rationale: BankAI was exactly this operating layer built by accident — sovereign, air-gapped, pre-ChatGPT

variants.comment:
The infrastructure abstraction pattern — Mainframes, VMware, AWS — is a clean frame and the analogy holds.

One thing it raises for me: each previous abstraction had a clear unit of what it was abstracting — hardware slots, VMs, data-center capacity. What's the unit here? In an enterprise, "one AI workload" is surprisingly fuzzy — an agent run, a model serving endpoint, a pipeline with five chained calls and a human-in-the-loop checkpoint. VMware's value became obvious once "a VM" was a clear thing to isolate and move around.

What's the moment when "an AI workload" becomes well-defined enough that the operating layer snaps into place — is that a solved problem at Dapple, or still an open question?
variants.rationale: Asks what the primitive unit of an AI workload is — a real open question for the abstraction analogy

variants.comment:
The sovereign environments and enterprise security piece is the most honest part of this — those two constraints alone rule out most generic GPU cloud offerings.

Curious how Dapple handles the genuinely air-gapped case: no outbound network, models on hardware the client owns, attestation chains fully under client control. That's a real segment — regulated financials, defense-adjacent, some healthcare — and it's where most "enterprise AI operating layers" hit a wall because their control plane still needs a SaaS callback somewhere. Is that in scope, or does Dapple's architecture assume at least some connectivity back to a hosted control plane?
variants.rationale: Targets the air-gapped/sovereign claim — where most enterprise AI control planes silently break

slack_summary: Argues every infrastructure cycle has a defining abstraction and AI needs its own operating layer — buying GPUs is easy; operating AI across sovereign/enterprise environments is the hard part Dapple is building.

author_headline: Helping teams capture the decisions AI coding agents forget
post_text:
"F*** your skills" is the joke. "And mine too" is the point.
We stopped shipping production software without tests. But we're still deploying production-grade behavior into agents with little more than lucky runs, screenshots, and folklore.
If we're going to ship reusable agent behavior - skills, instructions, tools, and workflow habits - we need to test it like code: tasks, rubrics, trajectories, comparisons, regressions, and promotion evidence.
That's what `runme eval` makes easy: local evals, clear evidence, and safer promotion. Link's in the comments.
… more
13
1

variants.comment:
The parallel to production software without tests is the right one to make.

One question I can't shake: who writes the rubrics? If the same coding agent that produced the skill also drafts the eval tasks and rubrics, you get the same blind spots in both. The agent will naturally write rubrics that test the behavior it knows how to produce — not the behavior the spec actually requires. It's the same problem we've been wrestling with on the validation side: when the same LLM generates both the thing and the check, passing isn't evidence of correctness, it's evidence of consistency.

Curious whether runme eval has a story for rubric authorship — whether it assumes a human writes them, or whether there's a way to break the circularity when you're running fully automated.
variants.rationale: Surfaces the rubric-authorship circularity — if the agent writes both skill and rubric, passing means consistency not correctness

variants.strategy_label: Disagree and argue your position (soft counter)
variants.comment:
Solid framing — "lucky runs, screenshots, and folklore" is exactly the state of most agent pipelines I've seen, including ones shipping to real users.

The thing that keeps me uncomfortable is what happens when the eval passes but the spec was wrong. Evals verify that behavior is consistent and reproducible — which is necessary but not sufficient. You can have a perfectly green eval suite for an agent that consistently does the wrong thing, because the rubric was written against the observed behavior rather than the intended behavior. We had something like this at the Bank.ai side: our German document validation passed internal checks reliably, and we only discovered a class of edge cases when a client's real documents surfaced patterns our rubrics never covered.

Does runme eval have a concept of a behavioral contract that lives above the rubric level — something that can survive a model upgrade without needing a full rubric rewrite?
variants.rationale: Evals verify consistency not correctness; grounded in a concrete Bank.ai edge-case discovery

variants.comment:
The analogy holds, and I wish more people in the agent space were making it explicitly.

Here is the part that worries me though: with production software, tests validate behavior against a spec that exists independently of the code. The code can be wrong; the spec is the judge. With agent skills, if the rubric is written by the same person (or agent) who wrote the skill, the rubric can encode the same misunderstanding. You end up with a green eval for behavior that shouldn't have been shipped in the first place — which is worse than folklore, because at least folklore doesn't give you false confidence.

The fix we reached in document generation was keeping a separate behavioral contract — written before the implementation, reviewed by domain people who didn't write the skill. Evals then ran against that contract, not against what the implementation happened to do. Slower, but it's the only way to break the circularity. Curious whether that distinction shows up in how runme eval is designed.
variants.rationale: Bank.ai fix — a separate behavioral contract written before implementation, reviewed by non-authors

slack_summary: Argues we stopped shipping software without tests but still deploy agent behavior on lucky runs and folklore — reusable agent behavior should be tested like code, which is what runme eval enables.

post_text:
We analyzed a sample of 1.2 million anonymized Claude Cowork sessions and found that more than 90% fell outside software development. Most of it is everyday knowledge work.
The biggest share is what we'd call the work around the work: drafting a status update, building a slide deck, or condensing research into a single report. These tasks move projects forward and keep businesses running, but are rarely a person's core responsibility.
People are handing that work to Claude Cowork and spending the recovered time on the parts of the job that need their judgment. A hiring manager might use it for interview scheduling and feedback summaries, freeing up time for conversations with candidates.
Read the full analysis: https://lnkd.in/dG9yP6_F
… more
929
42
45

variants.comment:
The 90%-outside-software figure is the kind of data point that reframes a product's positioning entirely — most AI tooling has been built with devs in mind, so this distribution is genuinely surprising.

Curious whether your data surfaces a meaningful split within that knowledge-work bucket: how much is mechanical overhead (reformatting, summarizing, scheduling) versus lighter judgment work (synthesizing, deciding, proposing)? That distinction matters for understanding where the recovered time actually lands, and whether it's being reinvested or just absorbed by the next queue.
variants.rationale: Asks for the mechanical-vs-judgment split inside the knowledge-work bucket — where the recovered time actually lands

variants.comment:
The "work around the work" framing is clean and I think accurate for where most people are right now.

But that label assumes the boundary between admin overhead and judgment work is reasonably stable. If AI keeps getting better at scheduling, summarizing, and structuring — at what point does it start absorbing tasks we currently call judgment? The hiring manager example is interesting: feedback summaries feel like overhead today, but evaluating candidates from those summaries is judgment. How far is that line going to hold before the two categories start collapsing into each other?
variants.rationale: Pushes on the assumed stable admin/judgment boundary that AI capability growth is actively eroding

variants.comment:
The 90% non-dev finding matches what we saw when we automated document generation for a German bank between 2018 and 2023 — the vast majority of the load was exactly this kind of knowledge overhead: drafting loan agreements, validating investment contracts, reformatting regulatory filings. People were genuinely relieved to hand it off.

What changed at the bank wasn't headcount. The bottleneck shifted upstream to the judgment calls — which terms to propose, which clients to flag, which exceptions needed a human read. The work didn't shrink; it relocated. Curious whether your session data shows any signal of that pattern at scale, or whether most users are still in the early "recovery" phase where they haven't yet hit the new bottleneck.
variants.rationale: BankAI knowledge-overhead automation showed the bottleneck relocates upstream to judgment, not shrinks

slack_summary: Analysis of 1.2M anonymized Claude Cowork sessions found 90%+ fell outside software development — mostly 'the work around the work' (status updates, decks, research), freeing people for judgment-heavy tasks.

author_headline: Co-founder @ Moderne | Java Champion | founder OpenRewrite - OSS automated refactoring
post_text:
Ouch. That's cold. 6 months after the "nobody looks at the IDE anymore" trend, one missing bang and it's like CC doesn't even know that IntelliJ exists, haha.
The reality is though, I'm looking more and more at the IDE. At a minimum, I'm finding I need to cut absurdly verbose comments everywhere. The comment pattern that I feel is particularly detrimental is the one that basically records what the code USED to do, or did in some iteration towards the final design, not what it CURRENTLY does. So there is a token record of discarded ideas that jointly serve as a kind of gravity well sucking a model back to those discarded ideas.
Strangely the better the model gets, the more convoluted the code is getting. A lot of folks are saying it doesn't matter, that engineers should move up the stack and become product managers and designers and even that models should stop writing in human-centric programming languages altogether.
I went through a phase where I used Zed just because it was faster to ! start from a CC session. It's faster though because it's far less capable. So I've boomeranged a bit. Slow is smooth, smooth is fast.

variants.comment:
The "gravity well" framing is precise — stale comments don't just confuse, they actively pull the model toward discarded states.

What strikes me is that this is a context problem, not a comment problem. The model reads whatever you give it, and if the codebase is the primary context, it inherits every scar of every iteration. The question I keep sitting with: what if the model's primary context wasn't the code at all? If behavior specs were the source of truth and code was regenerable on demand, stale comments become irrelevant — there's nothing for them to haunt. The "slow is smooth" logic you ended with might apply one level up too: slow down on keeping code as the canonical artifact, and the rest gets smoother.
variants.rationale: Lifts the conversation to whether code-as-context is itself the problem, adjacent to True BDD without pitching.

variants.comment:
The paradox you name — better model, more convoluted code — is something I'm watching too, and I think the direction of causality matters here.

My read: the model isn't getting worse as it improves. It's getting better at exploring the full possibility space the context gives it. And if that context is a codebase with residual comments from three design iterations, "better" means more thorough exploration of dead ends. The model is doing exactly what it should — it's just that code+comments is a lossy, accumulative format that encodes history alongside intent, and models can't always tell the difference.

Curious whether your "cut the verbose comments" heuristic is solving it, or just delaying the same problem until the next iteration cycle adds new sediment.
variants.rationale: Reframes the better-model-worse-code paradox as a context-format problem, ends with a challenging question.

variants.strategy_label: Clarifying question rooted in the topic
variants.comment:
The Zed phase resonates — I went through something similar evaluating lighter tools purely on session startup time, then boomeranged for the same reason you did.

One thing I'm curious about: when you say you're "looking more and more at the IDE," what does your context curation workflow actually look like before handing off to CC? Are you manually pruning files from context, or relying on CC to figure out what's relevant? Because the gravity well you describe feels like it compounds fast when the model is also the one deciding what to read.
variants.rationale: Surfaces the unaddressed context-curation workflow variable, invites a concrete answer.

slack_summary: On returning to the IDE in the agent era: stale/verbose code comments act as a gravity well pulling AI models back toward discarded design iterations.

author_headline: Helping teams capture the decisions AI coding agents forget
post_text:
"F*** your skills" is the joke. "And mine too" is the point.
We stopped shipping production software without tests. But we're still deploying production-grade behavior into agents with little more than lucky runs, screenshots, and folklore.
If we're going to ship reusable agent behavior - skills, instructions, tools, and workflow habits - we need to test it like code: tasks, rubrics, trajectories, comparisons, regressions, and promotion evidence.
That's what `runme eval` makes easy: local evals, clear evidence, and safer promotion. Link's in the comments.

variants.comment:
The "folklore" framing is the right diagnosis — most teams couldn't tell you whether the agent behavior they shipped last month still holds after the last model update.

One thing I keep running into: if the same LLM that generates the behavior also authors the rubric, the rubric inherits the same blind spots. The agent passes its own test not because it's correct but because both sides share the same interpretation of the task. It's the classic "student writes the exam" problem, and I don't see it going away just by adding more evals. Does runme eval address the rubric-authorship question at all, or is that still left to the human?
variants.rationale: Sharpens the post into the rubric-authorship open problem Petro has written about publicly.

variants.comment:
The analogy to shipping production software without tests is a good one — "lucky runs and folklore" describes the state of things pretty accurately.

Curious about the trajectory comparison piece specifically. When the agent's intermediate steps are non-deterministic between runs — same input, legitimately different paths to the same output — how does runme eval decide whether a trajectory changed for a bad reason or just varied within acceptable range? That boundary seems like the hardest part to define without collapsing back into "does the final output look right."
variants.rationale: Targets the trajectory-comparison vocabulary with a design question requiring real technical depth.

variants.comment:
The problem statement is exactly right — we treat agent behavior as something you observe rather than something you specify, and that's exactly where the trouble starts.

Where I'd push back slightly: testing against rubrics only helps if the rubric is itself a stable artifact. In my experience the rubric tends to drift the same way the behavior does — someone tweaks the instructions, the rubric implicitly changes, and nobody notices until a regression shows up that the old rubric wouldn't have caught anyway. The testing layer needs a source of truth above it, not just below it. Otherwise you're measuring against a moving target and calling it evidence.
variants.rationale: Argues the rubric itself needs a source of truth above it, building on Petro behavior-spec thesis.

slack_summary: Argues we ship reusable agent behavior on lucky runs and folklore and should test it like code — with rubrics, trajectories, and regressions.

author_headline: CEO, HuLoop | Board Member | AI-Powered Work Optimization
post_text:
Why do so many AI initiatives fail?
It's usually not because of the AI. It's because organizations try to layer AI on top of broken work.
In the latest edition of The Work Intelligence Loop, I share key insights from my conversation with Giorgio Zampirolo and Daria Rudnik on why successful AI adoption starts with fixing work, establishing clear ownership, and bringing employees along on the journey.
One takeaway that really stood out: The organizations seeing the greatest ROI from AI aren't the ones with the most AI tools. They're the ones that redesigned how work gets done first.
If you're leading AI, automation, digital transformation, or operations, I think you'll find this conversation valuable.
Listen to Episode 8 here: https://lnkd.in/gKgzxnMb
I'd love to hear your perspective: What's the biggest obstacle to successful AI adoption in your organization?
#ArtificialIntelligence #AI #FutureOfWork #DigitalTransformation #WorkOrchestration #IntelligentAutomation #AIGovernance #Leadership #BusinessTransformation #Innovation #ChangeManagement #TheWorkIntelligenceLoop

variants.comment:
The organizations seeing the greatest ROI aren't the ones with the most AI tools — that framing cuts through a lot of noise.

What I'm curious about: when you say "redesigned how work gets done first," what did that actually look like in the cases you saw? Were these orgs doing formal process mapping, or something less structured — more like a team deciding which decisions stay human and which don't? The reason I ask is that "fix the work first" can mean very different things depending on whether the broken part is process, ownership, or just unclear requirements — and the interventions are pretty different.
variants.rationale: The post leaves what redesign work actually means unaddressed — the real question practitioners face.

variants.comment:
The "layering AI on broken work" diagnosis is right, and I'd add one split that changes the fix.

There's broken work where the process is chaotic or unclear — and there's broken work where the process is actually fine but the requirements aren't captured anywhere a machine can use. We had clients at Speed & Function who had clear, functional workflows, and still couldn't get AI to help them reliably — because nobody had ever written down what "correct" looks like in a way that isn't stuck in someone's head. The AI adoption failure in those cases wasn't a process problem, it was a specification problem. Both land under "fix the work first," but the interventions are pretty different.
variants.rationale: Adds a process-vs-specification split grounded in Petro Speed & Function client experience.

variants.comment:
Redesigning the work before adding AI is the right call. The uncomfortable question is: how many orgs actually know what their work process is before they experiment?

In my experience, a surprising number of teams only discover their real workflow — the one that actually runs, not the one in the process doc — by watching where the AI fails. So there's a version of "layer AI on broken work, watch it break badly, then redesign" that is actually a reasonable path to discovering what needs fixing. Not the most efficient path. But probably more common than the "fix first, then add AI" narrative suggests.
variants.rationale: Challenges the assumption orgs know their real workflow before adding AI.

slack_summary: Argues most AI initiatives fail not because of the AI but because orgs layer it on broken work; biggest ROI comes from redesigning how work gets done first.

author_headline: AI-Native ATS | YCW25 Co-Founder & CTO Spott
post_text:
Claude Fable 5 changed how our engineers work.
And it's exactly why we won't be paying for it, for now.
Anthropic makes it pay-as-you-go only later this week. At $10 in / $50 out per million tokens (x2 Opus), running it raw isn't an option. One heavy prompt ate our weekly limit.
The price forced a workflow: use a cheaper model to plan and scope, then hand the expensive model only the tightly-scoped execution step.
The result: 50-80% fewer Fable tokens, same capability. The workflow matters more than which model sits in the senior seat. Put Opus there and day to day, we barely feel the difference.
Fable is a step up at finding intricate bugs and understanding complex implementations. But not the step change that justifies double.
The model goes. The workflow stays.

variants.comment:
We ran into almost the same thing. Fable wasn't the step-change on every task we expected — and when we dug in, we realized that what Fable is genuinely better at (intricate bug tracing, understanding complex implementation context) is a specific, separable step. Everything leading up to that — scoping, decomposition, deciding what's even worth running through the expensive model — cheaper models handle just as well.

The uncomfortable implication for me: if a workflow upgrade delivers 50-80% of the cost savings, that's not a Fable problem. That's a sign we were running all our expensive models without real task decomposition before. The constraint revealed the architecture we should have built anyway.
variants.rationale: Petro team had the same Fable-isn-t-breakthrough experience; adds practitioner confirmation and the implication the workflow gap predated Fable.

variants.comment:
The forced-workflow-by-price-constraint is a real pattern — I've seen it produce better systems than intentional design, because the constraint kills optionality before you get attached to it.

One thing I keep turning over: this workflow is optimized for today's price gap between a frontier and a mid-tier model. The planning/scoping step works because the mid-tier is genuinely cheaper AND good enough for decomposition.

What's your failure mode if the next model is strong at both steps at a cost close to what your scoping model costs today? Does the two-stage workflow survive, or does it collapse back into "just use the one model" and you rebuild the process again? I'm not sure the workflow is durable — it might just be the current cost curve wearing the disguise of a design principle.
variants.rationale: Challenges the durability of the workflow-vs-model framing — does the design principle survive the next price drop?

variants.comment:
The 50-80% token reduction from workflow design alone — before any model swap — is the part that stands out.

Curious what the handoff looks like between the scoping step and the execution step in practice. Is the scope document a free-form plan, a structured prompt template, something else? The reason I ask: in my experience the interface between the planning step and the execution step is where almost all the quality variance lives — the expensive model is only as good as the tightness of what it receives. If that interface is fuzzy, you get the token savings but also absorb a lot of the quality loss there rather than at the model level.
variants.rationale: Gets at the omitted operational detail — the planning-to-execution interface is where quality variance lives.

slack_summary: Fable 5's price forced a two-stage workflow (cheap model to plan, expensive for tightly-scoped execution), cutting tokens 50-80% at same capability — workflow over model.

author_headline: CEO at Vercel
post_text:
🏁 I animated the token 💰 spend race, from ~lifetime Vercel AI Gateway usage, which aggregates trillions of tokens from millions of developers a month.
Fascinating to see the fluctuations among the labs, Anthropic's dominance, and the rise of open weight AI.

variants.comment:
The open-weight rise is the part of this that surprises me most — it doesn't get discussed as much as the frontier lab race does.

Curious about the shape of that growth: is it concentrated in a handful of models (Llama, Mistral), or spread across many? And does the usage pattern look more like cost-sensitive production workloads or experimental side projects? The answer would tell a lot about whether this is a lasting rebalancing or a current-generation blip.
variants.rationale: Pushes for structure behind the open-weight trend — concentration vs spread, production vs experimental.

variants.comment:
Anthropic's dominance in a developer-facing gateway is a strong signal — developers tend to stick with whatever holds up under real production load.

The thing I'd sit with here is whether "developer mindshare on a gateway" and "long-term enterprise contract wins" are actually measuring the same thing. Developers choose what ships fastest; procurement teams choose what they can defend to a CISO. If those two populations diverge, what looks like dominance today becomes a more fragmented picture in 18 months. Has the Vercel data shown any movement in the enterprise vs. self-serve split?
variants.rationale: Questions whether developer gateway dominance actually tracks enterprise wins.

variants.comment:
The fluctuations among labs are genuinely hard to predict — 18 months ago the working assumption was that GPT-4-class models would hold most of the developer usage.

What actually shifted in that window, from where I sit, isn't just model quality — it's API reliability and price-per-token, both of which moved a lot. My read on Anthropic's share: Claude 3 Opus was expensive enough to push teams toward alternatives, then Haiku made the economics land, and usage followed. Would be curious whether the Vercel data shows any correlation between pricing inflection points and market-share moves across labs.
variants.rationale: Proposes a pricing-driven hypothesis for Anthropic share, inviting confirm/correct on Vercel data.

slack_summary: Shares an animated token spend race from Vercel AI Gateway usage — fluctuations among labs, Anthropic's dominance, and the rise of open-weight AI.

author_headline: Data-driven manager with 9+ years in product development within B2B/B2C, MBA alumni
post_text:
Вітаю, продовжуємо зростати, тож знову шукаю продакта в свою команду.
Мені потрібна людина, яка:
- Вміє і любить спілкуватися з користувачами, розуміє їхні потреби
