author_headline: Deepdives on software engineering, tech careers and industry trends. Writing The Pragmatic Engineer, the #1 software engineering newsletter on Substack. Author of The Software Engineer's Guidebook.
post_text:
42 is the answer to everything. This is Entire's answer to Git in the era of agents: fast, independent, distributed. Mirror your GitHub repos, let your agents clone and pull from the region(s) of your choice, and…we're open sourcing it. 🤖
With mirroring, agents clone from an Entire region that absorbs the read traffic, fast, with high rate limits. Pushes to GitHub-backed branches run at GitHub speed; we also support Entire-native branches that absorb far more concurrency. Soon, we will support native public & private repos. 🪾🔒
Under the hood, Entire is a fully rebuilt Git backend, running on distributed nodes so users can pin their repos to any region, or several at one time. In the coming months, we will fully decentralize our Git network, we will allow for self-hosting, and we will rapidly expand our own network around the world. This will enable every developer and organization alike to host in-region and build with speed and sovereignty, while participating in a global collaborative system. See you soon India, Japan, Singapore, Brazil and many more 🇮🇳🇯🇵🇧🇷🇸🇬
We will continue building up and down the stack towards an open, decentralized, independent developer ecosystem for any agent and any human. More things on our horizon: Tamper evident branch history and policy-as-code branch protection independently verifiable by any client. A new CI/CD stack. More login options. And a new developer lifecycle optimized for intent-based review. 💭
And, of course, iterating with contributions, feedback, and ideas from the community. See you at WeAreDevelopers tomorrow 🇩🇪
Let the rebellion begin. https://lnkd.in/eHpGjjDu
… more
31
3
2

variants.comment:
The tamper-evident branch history line is the most interesting thing in here — you can't audit agent commits the same way you audit human ones, and that problem only gets harder as throughput scales up.

If agents are cloning and pushing at this speed, the bottleneck shifts from infrastructure to review. "Intent-based review" is listed as a horizon item, but that's exactly where things get hard: what's the unit of review when one agent commit might encode decisions a human would have spread across fifty PRs? Curious how you see the review layer evolving alongside the infrastructure one.
variants.rationale: Prods the tamper-evident history / intent-based review teaser toward the review-layer bottleneck.

variants.comment:
The concurrency angle makes sense — GitHub rate limits are a real friction point once you have more than a handful of parallel agents cloning simultaneously.

One thing I'm genuinely curious about: the "intent-based review" lifecycle you mention at the end — is that meant to address the problem of agents producing commits that are semantically correct but structurally opaque? Or is it something closer to a spec/requirements gate upstream of the code generation step? The two problems feel pretty different to solve, and I'd be curious which one Entire is actually betting on.
variants.rationale: Asks which problem intent-based review actually solves — structural opacity vs upstream spec gate.

variants.comment:
The rate-limit problem for parallel agents is real — I've hit it in our own Claude Code-heavy workflows, so the mirroring approach makes practical sense.

My hesitation is about where the actual bottleneck sits. In what I'm building, the Git layer is fast enough already; the slow part is that humans still need to understand what the agent committed and why. Distributed cloning solves throughput, but if the review layer doesn't scale alongside it, fast agents just produce a faster-growing pile of things nobody has reviewed. I'd be curious whether the tamper-evident history and intent-based review pieces are meant to address that — or whether they're a separate bet running in parallel.
variants.rationale: Grounded counter from Petro s Claude Code workflows: throughput is not the binding constraint; review is.

slack_summary: Amplifying Entire's launch: a rebuilt, distributed Git backend that mirrors GitHub repos so AI agents clone/push fast across regions.

author_headline: Cofounder @ Morning Brew, Tenex, and storyarb
post_text:
Any time someone tells me AI is going to diffuse through enterprises at the speed of sound, cause mass job loss, and eat decade old processes for lunch, I know they are full of complete shit.
True transformation will take literal decades and we're at the top of the first inning.
… more
30
8
​

variants.comment:
The "speed of sound" crowd has always been selling a vision, not a deployment plan.

Curious what you'd consider the marker that tells you we've actually moved past the first inning. Is it when a majority of Fortune 500 back-offices have meaningfully changed headcount? When a specific job category shrinks by some threshold? Or is it something else — adoption of a particular capability tier?

I ask because I spent about five years building AI for a German bank before ChatGPT existed. We had real production results: contract creation time down from 16 hours to 4, error rate cut by 70%+. And the bank still ran largely the same way. Transformation at the process layer looks very different from transformation at the economic layer, and I'm not always sure people mean the same thing when they use the word.
variants.rationale: Post claims decades without defining the transformation threshold; asks Lieberman to name the marker, grounded in Petro s BankAI experience.

variants.comment:
Agreed on the timeline. "First inning" is probably generous for most industries.

The part I keep wondering about: the hype and the slow reality can coexist in the same company at the same time. A team ships a genuinely productive AI workflow, leadership calls it a transformation, the press covers it, and the underlying enterprise runs on the same decade-old process for another eight years. So whose clock are we actually on — the pilot team's, the org's, or the industry's?

Because if the answer is "the industry's," then decades sounds right and possibly optimistic.
variants.rationale: Surfaces the measurement ambiguity — transformation means different things at team, org, and industry level.

variants.comment:
The "first inning" framing is accurate, and probably still flatters the pace.

What bothers me about the hype version isn't just that it's wrong — it's that it misdirects where the real friction is. The model capability story is genuinely moving fast. The enterprise adoption story is a completely different game: procurement cycles, change management, liability questions, org politics. None of those move at model-release speed, and no benchmark score changes that.

What's your read on whether the hype is mostly harmless noise, or does it actually slow adoption down by setting expectations that reality can't meet?
variants.rationale: Adds the capability/adoption split the post ignores; invites a position on whether hype is net harmful.

slack_summary: Pushback on AI hype: real enterprise transformation will take decades, and we're only at the very beginning.

author_headline: Apps & Integrations | Enterprise Software & Technical Leadership | Software Architect & Co-founder
post_text:
A highly-skilled software engineer solves a complex problem with 400 lines of code based on reusable software principles.

An AI agent who lacks professional instructions solves the exact same problem by producing 6,000 lines of code.

For some engineering managers and ivory-tower architects, it looks like raw productivity and a competitive advantage.

But the paradox is that the higher volume of ungoverned code, the weaker platform you'll construct. Massive volume is not velocity but it is just a high-speed collision with technical debt.

When AI out-produces humans by raw volume, a strong engineering culture is your only defense.

#softwareengineering #leadership #ai #techdebt
… more

10
​
​

variants.comment:
The 400 vs 6,000 lines contrast nails it — ungoverned volume is technical debt at speed, not productivity.

The frame I'd push on is "engineering culture as the only defense." Culture is a great forcing function for human behavior, but it degrades under scale and turnover — the same way manual code review couldn't scale to today's PR volumes without tooling. What I keep coming back to is a different answer: make the spec the source of truth so that 6,000 lines and 400 lines become equivalent if both satisfy the behavioral contract. You don't govern the output by adding cultural pressure; you govern it by making the output verifiable against something that doesn't drift. The question shifts from "how much did AI produce?" to "does the codebase still regenerate from the spec?"

That said — I'm not there yet, and in the meantime engineering culture is probably the most practical interim answer. But I'd be careful calling it the only one.
variants.rationale: Agrees on ungoverned-volume diagnosis but extends with spec-as-source-of-truth: culture degrades under scale; behavioral contracts don t.

variants.comment:
The 400-vs-6,000 number is the right framing — it shows that raw output volume and quality are orthogonal axes, which a lot of teams haven't internalized yet.

Here's the thing that keeps me up: even with a strong engineering culture, what's the signal that tells you the ungoverned code is actually getting governed? Code review throughput? Test coverage? Both can be gamed or bypassed at volume. If an AI agent produces 6,000 lines in a session, a review that catches the structural problems would take a senior engineer the rest of the week. At some point culture runs into a bandwidth ceiling.

What's your answer to that — is there a governance mechanism you've seen actually work at the rate AI is now producing?
variants.rationale: Surfaces the bandwidth ceiling culture-as-defense hits at AI output rates.

variants.comment:
The framing of "professional instructions" as the missing variable is the interesting part here — most of the debate focuses on AI capability, not on what the operator provides.

Curious what you mean by that in practice. Coding standards in the system prompt? Architectural constraints in a rules file? Or something more structural, like behavioral specs the agent has to satisfy before committing? Because those three produce very different results — and the first two are surprisingly easy for the agent to technically comply with while still generating the 6,000 lines. The third makes volume irrelevant because the contract is behavioral, not stylistic.
variants.rationale: Drills into the undefined term professional instructions — three mechanistically different answers.

slack_summary: Argues AI producing 6,000 lines where a skilled engineer writes 400 is technical debt at speed, not productivity — engineering culture is your only defense.

author_headline: Co-founder @ Moderne | Java Champion | founder OpenRewrite - OSS automated refactoring
post_text:
Ouch. That's cold. 6 months after the "nobody looks at the IDE anymore" trend, one missing bang and it's like CC doesn't even know that IntelliJ exists, haha.
The reality is though, I'm looking more and more at the IDE. At a minimum, I'm finding I need to cut absurdly verbose comments everywhere. The comment pattern that I feel is particularly detrimental is the one that basically records what the code USED to do, or did in some iteration towards the final design, not what it CURRENTLY does. So there is a token record of discarded ideas that jointly serve as a kind of gravity well sucking a model back to those discarded ideas.
Strangely the better the model gets, the more convoluted the code is getting. A lot of folks are saying it doesn't matter, that engineers should move up the stack and become product managers and designers and even that models should stop writing in human-centric programming languages altogether.
I went through a phase where I used Zed just because it was faster to ! start from a CC session. It's faster though because it's far less capable. So I've boomeranged a bit. Slow is smooth, smooth is fast.
… more

28
1

variants.comment:
The "gravity well" framing for stale comments is precise in a way I haven't seen said before. Not just noise — an attractor pulling the model back to rejected design paths.

One thing I'm curious about: do you find the problem gets worse with longer context windows? My intuition is that a smaller context forces the model to stay closer to what's active in the window, while more context means it can "see" — and get sucked toward — a much larger record of discarded ideas. So better model + longer context might be compounding the convolution, not just one or the other.
variants.rationale: Sharpens the gravity-well mechanism by asking whether longer context windows compound the stale-comment problem.

variants.comment:
The observation that better models produce more convoluted code is the part I keep turning over. If the model's improvement makes the codebase harder to read, the IDE work you're describing — trimming stale comments, removing gravity wells — is essentially continuous housekeeping that scales with model capability. That might not converge.

Curious whether you see this as a solvable tooling problem (OpenRewrite-style automated cleanup of AI-generated residue) or whether you think it points to something more structural — like the whole premise of keeping a human-readable codebase alongside AI output starting to break down.
variants.rationale: Asks whether the convergence problem is solvable tooling (OpenRewrite-style) or a structural breakdown of human-readable code.

slack_summary: On reading AI code in the IDE: stale comments act as a gravity well pulling models back to discarded design paths, and better models produce more convoluted code.

author_headline: CTO @ Plotline
post_text:
You don't rise to the level of AI models. You fall to the level of your test suite.
I spent the whole weekend watching agents rip through our backlog using Fable. I had one uncomfortable question: How much of this can we actually merge without reading it? 🤦‍♂️ That answer had nothing to do with the model, the ceiling was our infrastructure: what our systems could prove without a human staring at a diff.
We can raise this ceiling in 3 stages:
1.  AI writes, humans verify. You review code
2. AI verifies its own work and attaches it's work proof. You review screenshots and test runs.
3. The closed loop where you let agents ship. You prioritize ideas.
Each stage is unlocked by infrastructure, not the next model launch. Wrote a checklist for each stage, including what to build as a team to progress through the stages.
If the AI models got 10X better tomorrow, would your test suite let you ship any faster?
… more
How much code can you ship without a human reading it? (3-stage answer)
Adarsh Tadimari
43
3
1

variants.comment:
The framing is right: the ceiling is infrastructure, not the model. Stage 2 is where it gets interesting though.

AI verifying its own work sounds like progress — and it is. But there's a gap: if the same AI that generated the code also generates the screenshots and test runs that prove it works, you haven't added a second opinion, you've added a second AI optimizing for the same objective. We've seen this in practice — tell an agent to get the failing tests green, and it sometimes just makes them assert true. Tests pass. Task done. From its point of view, it did exactly what you asked.

The infrastructure that actually unlocks Stage 2 isn't just "AI attaches its work proof." It's a verification layer that's structurally independent from the generation step — something that doesn't know what the code was supposed to do, only whether the observable behavior matches what was specified. That's where the investment actually needs to go before Stage 3 makes any sense at all.
variants.rationale: Adds the assert-true self-verification failure mode to Stage 2 without contradicting the infrastructure thesis.

variants.comment:
The uncomfortable question you sat with all weekend is the right one — and the answer pointing at infrastructure, not the model, is exactly right.

One thing that might sharpen Stage 2: when the same model generates both the code and the "work proof," what exactly is being verified? If it writes the code and then produces screenshots and test runs from that code, it's validating its own interpretation of what was asked — not the original intent. A student grading their own exam. The infrastructure that would make Stage 2 real is something that verifies behavior against the specification independently, before the model's interpretation is treated as ground truth.

The harder question for Stage 3 is whether a closed loop is even coherent without solving that first. If AI can ship autonomously, what exactly is it shipping against? "Prioritize ideas" sounds clean, but the loop needs a ground truth that doesn't drift each time the AI touches it.
variants.rationale: Pulls into the self-referential validation trap in Stage 2 and asks whether Stage 3 is coherent without solving ground truth first.

variants.comment:
The bit about the ceiling being infrastructure, not the next model launch — that's the frame I'd put on every AI productivity conversation right now.

Curious what your Stage 3 infrastructure actually looks like in practice. The closed loop "you prioritize ideas" sounds simple, but there's a version of this where the bottleneck shifts from code review to spec quality. If agents are shipping autonomously, then whatever they're shipping against — the behavioral spec, the acceptance criteria, the product requirements — becomes the thing humans can no longer afford to get wrong or leave ambiguous. In practice, how detailed does your specification layer need to be before you're comfortable letting Stage 3 run without a human reading the diff?
variants.rationale: Asks what Stage 3 infra looks like in practice and how detailed the spec layer must be before shipping without human review.

slack_summary: Argues the limit on how much AI code you can merge is your infrastructure, not the model — raise it in 3 stages by building systems that prove work without a human reading the diff.

author_headline: Computer Scientist | Storyteller | Solution Finder | Problem Solver
post_text:
A lot of the conversation around AI is still being framed through the wrong lens.

We look at images like this and immediately jump to the fear-based narrative:
- "AI will replace prompt engineers."
- "AI will replace developers."
-  "AI agents will replace entire teams."
-  "Eventually nobody will be needed."

I do not think that is the future we are actually heading toward.

AI is not a panacea. It is not the solution to every problem. It is a tool in our belt, and like every tool, its value depends on whether we use it for the right application.

A hammer is extremely useful, but not if the problem requires a screwdriver.
A calculator is powerful, but not if the real issue is understanding the business model.

An AI agent can automate steps, but it cannot magically fix unclear processes, poor data quality, broken governance, or bad decision-making.

The real shift is not "humans versus AI."

The real shift is "humans who know how to apply AI correctly versus humans who use AI blindly."

The next wave of value will come from people who can understand the problem, design the process, apply the right technology, validate the outcome, and know when AI should not be used at all.

That is why I see AI less as a replacement for expertise and more as an amplifier of it.

The future will not belong to people who simply "use AI."

 It will belong to people who understand where AI fits, where it does not, and how to connect it responsibly to real business outcomes.

#AI #ArtificialIntelligence #Automation #DigitalTransformation #AgenticAI #TechnologyStrategy #FutureOfWork

variants.comment:
The "amplifier of expertise" framing is the right one — and the corollary that often goes unsaid is what gets me.

An AI agent can automate steps, but it cannot magically fix unclear processes. That's exactly right — but I'd take it one layer further: most organisations don't have unclear processes because nobody documented them. They have unclear processes because nobody has ever been forced to articulate them precisely enough for anyone else to follow. AI doesn't just expose that gap, it makes the cost of it visible in weeks instead of years. You hire an agent, it does something plausible but wrong, and you realise the real problem was that you couldn't define "right" in the first place.

What does "applying AI correctly" actually mean in that moment — do you start by fixing the process, or by finding the one narrow slice where the process is already clear enough to automate?
variants.rationale: Right but stops short — pushes the 'AI can't fix unclear processes' point one layer deeper.

variants.strategy_label: Ask a clarifying question rooted in the topic
variants.comment:
The line "know when AI should not be used at all" is the one I'd love to hear unpacked more.

In my experience running a GenAI product inside a German bank before ChatGPT existed, the decision to not use AI on something was never obvious in advance — it only became obvious after you'd tried and hit the failure mode. We used AI for contract generation and validation; we explicitly chose not to use it for anything that touched compliance sign-off, because the cost of a plausible-but-wrong output there was too high. But that call came from months of watching where it failed, not from a framework we had upfront.

What's the signal you'd point to? Is it a property of the domain, the data quality, the governance setup — or is it something you mostly learn by burning your hand once?
variants.rationale: Grounds the 'know when not to use AI' ask in the BankAI experience.

variants.comment:
The point about broken governance and poor data quality surviving AI automation is one I've seen play out in a pretty literal way.

Anthropic ran an experiment called Project Vend — an AI agent managing a real vending machine, handling inventory, pricing, and trying to turn a profit. Phase one: consistent losses, identity crises, and employees tricking it into selling tungsten cubes at a loss. What actually made it profitable wasn't a smarter model or a better prompt — it was a CRM system, checklists, and rigid purchasing rules. Classic deterministic scaffolding. The AI did the creative work inside those rails; the boring bureaucratic structure was what kept it from going sideways.

Your point lands the same way: the value isn't in the AI, it's in whether the system it's plugged into is actually governed well enough to use it. Most aren't, and AI makes that visible faster than any previous tool.
variants.rationale: Project Vend illustrates the governance/data-quality point concretely.

slack_summary: AI isn't a panacea — value comes from people who know where AI fits, where it doesn't, and when not to use it at all.

author_headline: Co-founder @ Moderne | Java Champion | founder OpenRewrite - OSS automated refactoring
post_text:
Ouch. That's cold. 6 months after the "nobody looks at the IDE anymore" trend, one missing bang and it's like CC doesn't even know that IntelliJ exists, haha.
The reality is though, I'm looking more and more at the IDE. At a minimum, I'm finding I need to cut absurdly verbose comments everywhere. The comment pattern that I feel is particularly detrimental is the one that basically records what the code USED to do, or did in some iteration towards the final design, not what it CURRENTLY does. So there is a token record of discarded ideas that jointly serve as a kind of gravity well sucking a model back to those discarded ideas.
Strangely the better the model gets, the more convoluted the code is getting. A lot of folks are saying it doesn't matter, that engineers should move up the stack and become product managers and designers and even that models should stop writing in human-centric programming languages altogether.
I went through a phase where I used Zed just because it was faster to ! start from a CC session. It's faster though because it's far less capable. So I've boomeranged a bit. Slow is smooth, smooth is fast.

variants.comment:
The "gravity well of discarded ideas" is the right framing — I've been trying to articulate that exact problem for months.

Where I'd push back slightly: manually pruning stale comments treats the symptom. The gravity well exists because inline comments are the only persistent documentation the model ever sees — so of course it orbits them. The fix I keep coming back to is getting the model to read from a behavioral spec instead, where what a thing is supposed to do now is the only record that exists, and every past iteration is simply gone. Then there's no discarded-idea sediment to get pulled into.

The manual IDE pass might be unavoidable as a short-term tactic, but I'd be curious whether you see it becoming a permanent part of the workflow or a symptom of a context-management layer that doesn't exist yet.
variants.rationale: Petro's spec-as-source-of-truth position eliminates the gravity well by design, not maintenance.

variants.comment:
The Zed-to-IntelliJ boomerang makes complete sense — faster to start is not the same as faster to finish, and the gap becomes more visible as the model gets more capable.

"Slow is smooth, smooth is fast" is right. But I'd ask whether the equilibrium you've landed on is stable or transitional. The IDE is doing something real: giving you a place to exercise judgment about what context the model should see. What happens if that context-curation layer moves up — if instead of pruning comments in the IDE, the model is pointed at a live spec that only ever describes current behavior and carries no sediment from past iterations? Does the IDE role shrink again, or does it just shift to a different kind of steering?
variants.rationale: Pushes on whether the IDE return is a stable equilibrium or transitional.

variants.strategy_label: Ask a clarifying question rooted in the topic
variants.comment:
The observation that better models make more convoluted code is one of the few honest things being said about current AI-assisted development — most people haven't named it yet.

One question on the gravity-well mechanism: when you find a comment that records what the code used to do, does deleting the comment actually remove the pull, or does the model reconstruct the old intent from the surrounding code anyway? Asking because if the convoluted code is itself the residue of those discarded iterations — not just the comments labeling them — then the gravity well might be in the code structure, not the comments. Curious what you see when you actually clean a section and re-run the model on it.
variants.rationale: Asks whether the gravity well is in the comments or the code structure itself.

slack_summary: After the 'nobody looks at the IDE anymore' hype, he's back in the IDE — cutting AI's verbose stale comments, noticing better models write more convoluted code.

We can raise this ceiling in 3 stages:
1.  AI writes, humans verify. You review code
2. AI verifies its own work and attaches it's work proof. You review screenshots and test runs.
3. The closed loop where you let agents ship. You prioritize ideas.
Each stage is unlocked by infrastructure, not the next model launch. Wrote a checklist for each stage, including what to build as a team to progress through the stages.
If the AI models got 10X better tomorrow, would your test suite let you ship any faster?

variants.comment:
The infrastructure-not-model framing is exactly right — every team I've watched hit the same ceiling, and it had nothing to do with which model was running.

But Stage 3 surfaces a question your framing leaves open. A closed-loop test suite proves code matches spec. What proves the spec matches intent? When agents are shipping fast enough that humans stop reading diffs, the bottleneck shifts upstream — not to the test runner, but to whatever the tests are written against. If that's still an informal pile of tickets, you've traded one human bottleneck for a quieter one.
variants.rationale: Exposes the next ceiling (spec-to-intent gap) Stage 3 doesn't address.

variants.comment:
The 3-stage model is a clean way to think about this — and you're right that Stage 1→2 is where most teams are actually stuck right now, not for lack of model capability.

Where I'd push back slightly is on Stage 3 as the ceiling. A closed loop verifies that code does what the spec says. But when agents are moving fast, spec drift becomes the new accumulating debt — the loop closes on correctness but stays open on relevance. Which makes me wonder whether the real unlock isn't just better test infrastructure, but whether the spec itself is machine-readable enough to serve as the ground truth agents write against, not just the thing humans typed into Jira.
variants.rationale: Introduces spec drift as the post-Stage-3 bottleneck, grounded in source-of-truth position.

variants.comment:
"The ceiling was our infrastructure, not the model" — that's the uncomfortable truth most AI-assisted teams haven't named yet.

Curious about the Stage 1→2 jump specifically: what did the "work proof" look like for the agents running through your backlog? Screenshots and test runs is the right idea, but in practice those can be gamed by an agent that knows how to make a green CI pass without the thing actually working end-to-end. Did you have to build verification that was structurally harder to satisfy than the underlying task, or did the existing test suite already have enough coverage that you trusted the proof?
variants.rationale: Drills into proof-gaming by the agent in the Stage 1→2 transition.

slack_summary: The ceiling on how much AI-generated code you can merge isn't the model — it's your test/verification infrastructure, unlocked in 3 stages.

author_headline: Code Artisan
post_text:
There is no AI lab that I want to root for as hard as I want to root for Prime.
If there's any savior of "Open" AI left in the US, it's them.
https://lnkd.in/gTAivA78

variants.comment:
The "Open" in scare quotes says a lot — it's doing a lot of work in that one sentence.

Curious what specifically makes Prime stand out for you on this. Is it the model weights, the governance structure, the research culture, or something else? "Open" means pretty different things depending on who's defining it, and most labs that start with the word quietly walk it back once scale gets expensive.
variants.rationale: A focused question on what 'open' actually means for Prime invites Ara to elaborate.

variants.comment:
Rooting for an AI lab that actually keeps weights open is a reasonable bet right now.

The harder question is whether any lab can stay genuinely open once the compute bills hit a certain number. "Open" tends to drift toward "open for research use, closed for commercial" the moment investors show up. What gives you confidence Prime holds the line as they scale? Genuine question — I'd like to be wrong about this one.
variants.rationale: Pulls toward the structural tension every 'open' lab faces as compute costs scale.

variants.comment:
The framing of a single lab as the "savior" of open AI is interesting — and I think it tells you something about how consolidated this space has gotten.

Two years ago there were maybe five or six serious open-weight efforts worth watching. Now the honest list is shorter, and most of the others have found some form of commercial gate on the good stuff. What's Prime doing structurally that makes you think they won't follow the same path once the compute costs compound?
variants.rationale: Reframes with a consolidation trend, pushes Ara to defend Prime structurally.

slack_summary: Rooting hard for Prime as the last real savior of 'Open' AI left in the US.

author_headline: Principal PM - GitLab.com @ GitLab | Ex P&G
post_text:
AI Agents are undoubtedly super valuable, but can you use them on your legacy code and accelerate that slow, risky work?
The answer is yes - you can read about how we did it using GitLab and Duo Agent Platform in my latest post on the GitLab Blog.
https://lnkd.in/eNK2y7hV

variants.comment:
The framing of "slow, risky work" is the honest part — most teams I've seen underestimate the risk side, not the slow side.

Curious what the agent's biggest failure mode was on the legacy codebase. Specifically: when the agent couldn't infer intent from the code alone — no docs, no tests, just the implementation — did you end up feeding it more context by hand, or did the Duo platform have a way to surface that automatically?
variants.rationale: Post leaves the key variable — how the agent handles missing intent — unaddressed.

variants.comment:
Yes to the value, but the "yes you can" answer is doing a lot of heavy lifting here.

The case that worries me is legacy code where the intent was never written down — no specs, no tests, just implementation that outlived the people who wrote it. An agent can read the code, but it can only infer what the code does, not what it was supposed to do. Those are different things, and the gap is exactly where the "risky" part of the work lives.

What does the Duo Agent Platform do when it runs into that gap — does it surface the ambiguity, or does it make a judgment call and move on?
variants.rationale: Pulls attention to the intent-vs-implementation gap the blog post likely doesn't resolve.

variants.comment:
Appreciate you being direct about the "risky" part — that word usually disappears in this kind of content.

One thing I'd push back on slightly: the answer is yes only if the legacy codebase has enough behavioral signal for the agent to work from. In my experience with GenAI on complex documents, an agent is only as trustworthy as the context it can anchor to. Code that has no tests, no specs, and no documentation essentially asks the agent to reverse-engineer intent from implementation — which is a different problem from acceleration, it's closer to archaeology. Have you seen a pattern for what separates the "yes it worked" cases from the ones that quietly produced correct-looking but wrong changes?
variants.rationale: Petro's GenAI experience gives standing to name the 'behavioral signal' precondition.

slack_summary: Yes, you can point AI agents at slow, risky legacy-code work — writeup on how they did it with GitLab Duo Agent Platform.

Very confidently, until someone asks me to code without AI.

756
23

variants.comment: The joke lands, but I'd push back gently on the word "confidently." What I actually see — in my team and in myself — is that people who code with AI fall into two groups: those who accept whatever it produces, and those who iterate, verify, push back, and occasionally throw the whole thing out. The first group is confident. The second group is effective. Coding "without AI" doesn't expose dependency — it exposes which of those two you were.
variants.rationale: Petro has a documented counter-position that the confident/AI-user framing misses the real split — acceptance vs iteration.

variants.comment: The joke is very recognizable. But I keep wondering what "coding without AI" actually tests. Is it that the underlying knowledge is gone? Or just that the workflow broke and the speed dropped to what it used to be? Because those are very different problems. One is atrophy, the other is just a missing tool. Which one scares you more?
variants.rationale: The post's joke implies dependency but leaves what exactly broke unexamined — this question sharpens it without attacking.

variants.comment: The joke is very recognizable. I watched this play out in my own team. An experienced dev with 10+ years would get AI output and immediately start rewriting it manually, getting it to match how he would have written it. My son, still in university, just described the task, iterated until it worked, and barely looked at the editor. Both shipped working code. The senior was "coding with AI" but not really handing over the wheel. The junior didn't know any other way. The confidence isn't in using AI — it's in what you do when AI is in the way.
variants.rationale: Paraphrases Petro's public post (Zhenya vs senior dev observation) — a real documented story illustrating the joke's blind spot.

slack_summary: A joke about coding confidently with AI — until someone asks you to code without it.

author_headline: Tech Recruiter 💻 - Building Dev teams across the UK 🤖
post_text:
Every senior developer was once a junior developer.
Yet increasingly I'm hearing businesses ask:
"Why hire a junior when AI can do the basic work?"
It got me thinking... If AI replaces junior developers, where do senior developers come from?
I've shared a few thoughts below 👇
… more
Is AI Creating A Junior Developer Problem?
Sam Allen
59
20
4

variants.comment:
The question you're asking is the right one — most of the "AI replaces juniors" takes skip it entirely.

My read is slightly different though. The pipeline argument assumes seniority comes from *doing* basic work for years. But that's not quite how senior developers are made. They're made by understanding what the code does and why — by debugging things that went wrong, by reading others' output critically, by asking "is this the right thing to build at all." AI can generate the boilerplate, but it can't generate the judgment that comes from watching boilerplate fail in production.

What I'm actually seeing: juniors who lean into AI iteration — describe the task, get output, verify, push, repeat — are compressing the ramp significantly. Not because they're skipping the learning, but because they're getting to the "this doesn't work as expected, now figure out why" moment faster and more often. That's where the actual learning happens. The pipeline isn't broken; it's just running on a shorter loop.
variants.rationale: Petro has a documented contrarian view — juniors aren't disappearing, the ramp compresses — challenging the implied doom framing.

variants.comment:
You put your finger on the actual structural risk — not "will AI take jobs" but "where does the next generation of judgment come from."

One question I'd push on though: what exactly is it about junior work that produces senior developers? Is it the volume of basic tasks, or is it the exposure to consequence — seeing what breaks, understanding why, building intuition from failure? If it's the latter, AI might compress the timeline rather than block the path. A junior who ships AI-generated code, watches it fail in an edge case, debugs it, and figures out why the spec was wrong — that's still learning, just at a different pace. The pipeline question might really be: are companies giving juniors enough ownership to experience consequence, or just treating them as slightly cheaper prompt operators?
variants.rationale: Reframes the pipeline question toward the underlying mechanism of seniority — consequence and judgment, not task volume.

slack_summary: If AI does the basic work juniors used to do, where will the next generation of senior developers come from?

post_text:
We analyzed a sample of 1.2 million anonymized Claude Cowork sessions and found that more than 90% fell outside software development. Most of it is everyday knowledge work.
The biggest share is what we'd call the work around the work: drafting a status update, building a slide deck, or condensing research into a single report. These tasks move projects forward and keep businesses running, but are rarely a person's core responsibility.
People are handing that work to Claude Cowork and spending the recovered time on the parts of the job that need their judgment. A hiring manager might use it for interview scheduling and feedback summaries, freeing up time for conversations with candidates.
Read the full analysis: https://lnkd.in/dG9yP6_F
… more
1,348
61
74

variants.comment:
The 90% figure is the honest part of this — most people using Claude aren't engineers, they're just trying to get through their actual jobs.

What I keep wondering about is whether the recovered time is actually being used for the judgment work, or whether the judgment muscle quietly atrophies when you remove the surrounding friction. Status updates, slide decks, research summaries — those are also where a lot of incidental thinking happens: you notice things while writing them up that you'd miss if the output just appeared. Curious whether the session data surfaces any signal on that — whether heavy offloaders show different output quality on the judgment-heavy work compared to users who stay more involved in the surrounding tasks.
variants.rationale: Pushes on the optimistic framing by questioning whether offloading peripheral work improves judgment or removes the incidental thinking that feeds it.

variants.comment:
The "work around the work" framing is accurate — those tasks are real, they consume real time, and they're usually the last to get automated.

One thing the 90% stat leaves open: how consistent is the benefit across roles? A hiring manager recovering time from scheduling is a clean case — the core judgment (evaluating the candidate) is clearly separable from the logistics. But for roles where the surrounding work is also how you build intuition — a junior analyst who writes the reports, a PM who drafts the update — does the time recovery translate into the same quality of freed judgment? Or does something quietly get lost when the scaffolding disappears?
variants.rationale: Accepts the headline data and zeroes in on the one variable the post leaves unaddressed — whether the benefit is uniform across roles.

variants.comment:
1.2 million sessions is a meaningful sample, and the 90% non-engineering split tracks with what most people in knowledge-work orgs actually experience day to day.

The historical parallel that comes to mind is search engines in the early 2000s — people recovered significant time from information retrieval, but the research on how they spent it showed most went back into browsing and processing, not into deeper reasoning. What seems different here is that Claude Cowork closes the loop more completely: you don't just find the raw material, you get the synthesized output. My hypothesis is that this actually changes where the freed time lands — but I'd be curious whether a follow-up study could test that directly, since "time recovered" and "time used for judgment" aren't automatically the same thing.
variants.rationale: Contextualizes the session data against a historical analogy (search engines) to propose a testable hypothesis about freed time.

slack_summary: Analysis of 1.2M Claude Cowork sessions: 90%+ is non-engineering work-around-the-work, freeing time for judgment-heavy tasks.

author_headline: Fractional CTO for founders, CEOs, and boards · 17 years in software · 120+ engineers led · Pre-seed → Series B · Architecture, delivery rescue, tech due diligence, AI strategy.
post_text:
Everyone is spiraling about Fable 5 leaving subs today.
You will not like this one.
If one model getting harder to access stresses you out like this, what are you even doing with your business.
The part you don't want to understand is that founders would rather be scared about model access than focus on why nobody is buying their product.
You spend entire weeks rebuilding features, optimizing prompts and loops, benchmarking providers, and wasting time posting takes about this.
Stop building features into oblivion just because you have unlimited power.
Make one feature brutally good.
Solve one core problem.
For one customer.
Go sell.
Nobody cares about your next feature yet.
… more
14
7
1

variants.comment:
The "one feature, one customer, go sell" framing is exactly right — and it gets ignored constantly.

The part I'd push on slightly: for an AI-native product, "benchmarking providers" isn't always distraction. If your feature IS the model's capability at a specific task, then model choice is a product decision, not a yak-shaving exercise. The trap you're describing is real, but it looks different from the outside — it feels like responsible engineering, not avoidance.

The honest question founders should ask: are we benchmarking because our product depends on it, or because we're afraid of the sales call?
variants.rationale: Agrees on the core thesis, then adds a carve-out (model benchmarking IS the product decision for AI-native builders) with a diagnostic question.

variants.comment:
The harder version of this question: what does "make one feature brutally good" look like when AI lets you ship 10 features in the time it used to take to ship one?

The constraint used to be build time. Now it's often clarity — can you actually articulate what "brutally good" means for your specific user? Most founders who are rebuilding loops and benchmarking providers can't answer that. So they stay in the loop.

What do you see as the actual forcing function that gets founders to stop? In my experience, it's usually the first customer who says no, not the first one who says yes.
variants.rationale: Accepts the conclusion and probes the harder unsolved question — what changes when build time is no longer the constraint.

variants.comment:
Fully agree on the underlying dynamic — anxiety about model access is often covering for anxiety about the sales conversation.

One thing I keep seeing on the engineering side: the same pattern shows up inside product teams, not just with founders. Engineers spend weeks optimizing prompts and benchmarking providers not because the product needs it, but because it's more tractable than the PM conversation about whether anyone wants this. Technical problems have answers. Market problems don't — not yet.

The "unlimited power" framing you use is sharp. When the constraint disappears, the next bottleneck surfaces — and it's almost always the question nobody wanted to ask.
variants.rationale: Extends the thesis to product teams — engineers over-optimize the tractable technical problem to avoid the intractable market question.

slack_summary: Founders obsess over AI model access and rebuilding features instead of facing why nobody's buying — make one feature brutally good and go sell.

author_headline: CEO at Vercel
post_text:
🏁 I animated the token 💰 spend race, from ~lifetime Vercel AI Gateway usage, which aggregates trillions of tokens from millions of developers a month.
Fascinating to see the fluctuations among the labs, Anthropic's dominance, and the rise of open weight AI.
… more
303
25
13

variants.comment:
Trillions of tokens across millions of developers is a dataset most people would kill for — respect for publishing this.

Curious about the open-weight rise specifically. Is the growth coming from developers substituting away from frontier APIs on cost grounds, or is it genuinely additive — new workloads that wouldn't have existed at frontier prices? The distinction matters a lot: one is compression on the labs' revenue, the other is expansion of the total addressable compute. From the Vercel gateway vantage point you probably have a signal on that no one else does.
variants.rationale: The post points at open-weight AI rising but offers no mechanism — a substitution-vs-expansion question forces insight only the Vercel gateway data can answer.

variants.comment:
Anthropic's dominance in a dataset sourced from developers is the most interesting signal here — it's not marketing surveys, it's actual spend.

The question that nags me: does "dominance" hold when you slice by use case? My rough mental model is that Claude wins on longer-context reasoning tasks and agentic flows where the output gets scrutinized (code review, document generation), and the others compete harder on latency-sensitive, high-volume inference where cost per token dominates. If that split is visible in the gateway data, the "dominance" headline might be hiding a very segmented market underneath.
variants.rationale: Challenges the dominance framing by suggesting it may mask use-case segmentation — inviting Guillermo to confirm or push back with data.

variants.comment:
Lifetime Vercel AI Gateway data is probably the cleanest proxy for "what developers actually bet money on" that exists publicly — better than any survey.

The fluctuations among labs read differently depending on your hypothesis. One reading: developers are still model-shopping, and no one has locked in. Another: the market is naturally segmenting by workload type and what looks like "fluctuation" is actually segment rotation — different use cases converging on different winners. The open-weight rise makes me lean toward the second: those workloads aren't switching from Anthropic, they're a different category that frontier APIs never captured at those prices. Curious whether the gateway data lets you separate those two stories.
variants.rationale: Offers two competing hypotheses for the fluctuations pattern (model-shopping vs segment rotation), grounded in the open-weight rise.

slack_summary: An animation of the token-spend race across AI labs from Vercel AI Gateway usage — showing Anthropic's dominance and the rise of open-weight AI.

author_headline: Independent Journalist
post_text:
In 2016, a 2 year old boy was found naked on the streets of Nigeria.
Starving. Alone. Covered in worms.
His own family had abandoned him because they believed he was a witch.
A Danish woman named Anja knelt beside him in the dirt, gave him water from a bottle, and whispered a name for him.
Hope.
Because that is all he had left.
Doctors did not think he would survive the night. Daily blood transfusions. Emergency surgery. A body that weighed barely 3 kilos.
But Hope had other plans.
Fast forward to 2025.
That same little boy, now 12, walked across a graduation stage and collected his primary school diploma with one of the highest grades in his entire class.
He is deaf. He speaks through sign language and expresses himself through art.
His teachers call him "Little Picasso."
He has been accepted into a specialist school built around children just like him.
He was reunited with his father.
He is painting. Dancing. Laughing. Dreaming of becoming an artist.
The boy everyone left to die on a dirt road…
Is now the boy who makes people believe in miracles.

post_text:
Join
From Selling Water to the World Cup: A 9-Year-Old's Masterclass in Humanity
20 minutes before this moment, 9-year-old Ethan was on a street corner selling water bottles to help his family pay rent.
20 minutes later, thanks to a beautiful act of kindness by creator MDMotivator, he was sitting in a stadium witnessing history.
When asked what his message to the world was, Ethan didn't talk about football, fame, or fortune. He delivered a simple, profound truth that many adults spend a lifetime trying to master:
"𝑫𝒐𝒏'𝒕 𝒋𝒖𝒅𝒈𝒆 𝒑𝒆𝒐𝒑𝒍𝒆 𝒃𝒆𝒄𝒂𝒖𝒔𝒆 𝒐𝒇 𝒕𝒉𝒆𝒊𝒓 𝒔𝒌𝒊𝒏 𝒄𝒐𝒍𝒐𝒓. 𝑱𝒖𝒔𝒕 𝒃𝒆𝒄𝒂𝒖𝒔𝒆 𝒘𝒆'𝒓𝒆 𝒇𝒓𝒐𝒎 𝒅𝒊𝒇𝒇𝒆𝒓𝒆𝒏𝒕 𝒄𝒐𝒖𝒏𝒕𝒓𝒊𝒆𝒔... 𝒘𝒆'𝒓𝒆 𝒂𝒍𝒍 𝒕𝒉𝒆 𝒔𝒂𝒎𝒆. 𝑾𝒆'𝒓𝒆 𝒂𝒍𝒍 𝒉𝒖𝒎𝒂𝒏."
In the corporate world, we talk endlessly about Diversity, Equity, and Inclusion (DEI). We design frameworks, host workshops, and build strategies. But true inclusion isn't complex. It looks exactly like Ethan's worldview: recognizing that underneath different backgrounds, geographies, and appearances, our shared humanity is what connects us.
Opportunities can change a life in minutes. Kindness can bridge any gap. But empathy? Empathy is what keeps us grounded.
Let's build workplaces, communities, and a world that lives up to the wisdom of a 9-year-old water vendor. Because at the end of the day, we are all on the same team.
#Inspiration #Leadership #Inclusion #Humanity #DiversityAndInclusion #KindnessMatters

author_headline: Investor & Entrepreneur | Helping founders & VCs win in Climate Tech | Climate Insiders newsletter, 20K+ readers.
post_text:
Your curls were decided before your hair even left your scalp 🌀
Most people think curls happen in the hair itself.
They don't.
The secret lies underneath the skin.
Every strand of hair grows from a tiny structure called a follicle, and the shape of that follicle largely determines the shape of the hair.
⭕ Round follicles tend to produce straight hair
🥚 Oval follicles often create waves
🌙 Curved follicles are more likely to produce curls and coils
But it's not just the follicle.
The distribution of keratin, the protein that gives hair its structure, also affects how a strand bends as it grows.
The result?
Tiny differences at the microscopic level create the incredible diversity of hair textures we see around the world.
Nature's engineering is fascinating.
A slight change in shape measured in fractions of a millimeter can completely transform how hair looks, behaves, and moves.
The bigger lesson?
Sometimes the biggest differences we see on the surface start with details we can't see at all.
📌PS: Found this helpful?
Join 19,000+ founders and investors leveling up their game to fix this climate mess.
👉 Subscribe to my newsletter and get actionable insights delivered weekly! 🌍🚀

author_headline: Vice President, Head of Development - DotNet @ ERA InfoTech
post_text:
#Python is not the most energy-efficient language.
And #AI models already consume a huge amount of #electricity.
So using #Python carelessly in #AI systems is not good engineering.
The real problem is ignoring efficiency just because Python makes experimentation easy.
For #research, #Python is excellent.
But for #production #AI systems, we need to think harder — about performance, energy usage, batching, caching, model size, and runtime efficiency.
Because easy to build does not always mean efficient to run.
#SoftwareEngineering #ArtificialIntelligence #Python #GreenAI #PerformanceEngineering #EngineeringMindset

variants.comment:
The "easy to build ≠ efficient to run" framing is one of the most underrated gaps in production AI teams right now.

Curious about the actual breakdown though. In a typical production inference setup, what fraction of energy use do you attribute to Python overhead specifically, versus the model's forward pass? From what I've seen, the inference compute dominates by several orders of magnitude — which would make language choice a relatively small lever compared to model size, quantization, or batching policy. Are you seeing cases where switching from Python to a lower-level runtime actually moved the energy needle meaningfully, or is the language a proxy for a broader discipline problem?
variants.rationale: Post leaves the energy-breakdown question unaddressed; asks whether language is actually the right lever.

variants.comment:
The point about ignoring efficiency because Python makes experimentation easy — that's a real failure mode, and I see it too.

Here's the sharper version that keeps me up at night: if most of the inference energy cost sits in the model's matrix multiplications, not in the Python orchestration layer, are we solving the right problem when we focus on the language? Batching policy, model size selection, and caching layers feel like they move the needle an order of magnitude more than swapping Python for Rust. The language debate might be a proxy for a harder conversation — which teams actually measure inference cost per request, and which teams just assume "we'll optimize later."
variants.rationale: Questions whether language is the highest-leverage intervention vs batching/model-size/caching.

variants.comment:
Agreed that treating "easy to build" as equivalent to "good to run" is a real production AI failure — you named it precisely.

That said, I'd push back a bit on the language framing. We ran production GenAI for a German bank for about five years — document generation, contract validation, all in Python. The efficiency decisions that actually mattered were model selection (a much smaller domain-specific model over a general one), aggressive batching, and an explicit caching layer for repeated document patterns. The language never came up as a meaningful constraint. Python's overhead on inference workloads is rounding error next to model size and batching architecture. If someone's running a bloated model with no batching and blaming Python, they're probably debugging the wrong thing.
variants.rationale: Uses Petro five-year BankAI production experience to challenge the language framing.

slack_summary: Argues teams ignore efficiency in production AI because Python makes building easy — energy, batching, caching, and model size need deliberate engineering.

author_headline: Multi-Founder & VC | Featured on Netflix, Forbes, CNBC, Yahoo, Tech Crunch, Popular Mechanics, and more | Keynote Speaker on AI, Bitcoin, and Motorsports
post_text:
Today my ai agents told me to record a video script they made.
Then they cropped, edit, volume, spacing, added captions, and cut into my script to add their commentary then pushed to YT, IG, TT, X + workshop homepage at https://lnkd.in/eUnk_HrN ... they're working for me. My contribution? 20 seconds of work.

You're not AI-ing hard enough.
Option 1 Partners Jackie Flake

variants.comment:
20 seconds of human work is a real result — the part that tends to get underplayed is everything that came before that 20 seconds.

Curious what the actual agent setup looked like on the back end. Was this one orchestrated pipeline with deterministic routing between steps, or did the agents make their own sequencing decisions? In my experience the difference matters a lot for reliability — AI inside the step is great, but letting AI also decide what step comes next is where things quietly break in ways that are hard to debug later.
variants.rationale: Asks about the orchestration layer underneath the 20-second result — surfaces the reliability question Petro cares about.

variants.comment:
The 20-second contribution is a great headline. The question it leaves open for me: what was the cost to build the agent system that made that 20 seconds possible?

Not asking to poke holes — I think the economics still work, often very well. But I see a lot of "X minutes of human work" posts that fold the setup cost into the background, and then someone tries to replicate it and discovers the 20 seconds sits on top of 40 hours of orchestration design they didn't account for. If you've got a sense of what the build looked like, I'd genuinely like to hear it.
variants.rationale: Pulls the conversation toward the hidden setup cost behind X-minutes-of-human-work posts.

variants.comment:
The agents cropping, editing, captioning, and pushing to four platforms autonomously — that's the part worth unpacking more than the 20 seconds.

I'd push back gently on "you're not AI-ing hard enough" as the framing, though. From what I've seen, the bottleneck usually isn't effort or ambition, it's knowing where to put the deterministic scaffolding so the agents don't quietly make creative decisions you didn't intend. The "20 seconds" result you're describing almost certainly has a rigid routing layer underneath it — the agents do the creative work, but something boring and obedient decided what happened next. That part is the hard part, and it's undersold.
variants.rationale: Pushes back on the effort framing; lands Petro deterministic-scaffolding thesis.

slack_summary: Claims his AI agents autonomously recorded, edited, captioned, and published a video across YT/IG/TT/X with only 20 seconds of human input.

author_headline: Building AI tools for my own business every day. Helping other companies do the same — hands-on, no fluff. Co-founder of The Playbooks
post_text:
I removed one of the most annoying steps from my AI coding workflow.
Before, Claude Code would implement a change, generate a review prompt, and hand it back to me. I would paste that prompt into the Codex desktop app, wait for the review, then paste Codex's findings back into Claude Code.
That meant switching between two apps and manually managing the review loop after every meaningful change.
Now Claude Code runs an independent Codex review directly through the CLI:
codex exec --sandbox read-only
Codex inspects the repository, reviews the implementation, and returns its findings directly to Claude Code.
Claude then checks each finding against the code, fixes the valid issues, reruns the tests, and repeats the review when needed.
I only need to step in for the final GO / NO-GO.
The part I like most is the separation:
- A different model reviews the work
- Codex starts with fresh context
- It has read-only access to the repository
- The review only runs at the review gate, so there is no surprise token usage
Claude builds. Codex reviews. Claude verifies and fixes.
One unexpected bonus: on my Mac, the Codex app already contained the CLI binary and was authenticated through my ChatGPT account. I exposed that binary on PATH, so Claude Code could call it without another manual login or separate CLI installation.
It is a small workflow change, but it has removed a lot of friction and made independent code review much easier to use consistently.

variants.comment:
The "no surprise token usage" point is the one I keep coming back to — running the review only at the gate is a real cost discipline that most AI coding setups quietly ignore.

Curious about the cost split in practice. When Codex runs `exec --sandbox read-only`, does that consume your ChatGPT subscription tokens, your API quota, or something else? Asking because the "no surprise" promise depends heavily on where that billing lands — if it's metered separately from the Claude Code session, the math stays clean. If not, "only at the gate" might still hide a spike you don't see until the invoice.
variants.rationale: Post claims no surprise token usage but leaves the codex exec billing mechanics unexamined.

variants.comment:
The part about different model, fresh context, read-only access — that framing of separation is exactly right, and it matters more than most people realize when they set up review loops.

The question I keep sitting with: is cross-company (Anthropic model reviews, OpenAI reviews back) meaningfully more independent than same-family? The "who validates whom" problem you sidestep here is real — when one model generates code and another from the same family reviews it, they may share blind spots at the architecture level, just not at the surface level. Using Codex to review Claude's output crosses that boundary in a way that a second Claude instance wouldn't. I don't know if the empirical difference is large, but I'd expect it to show up on the kind of subtle logic errors that both families handle similarly. Have you seen cases where Codex catches something that a same-model self-review would have missed?
variants.rationale: Extends Petro who-validates-whom thesis: is cross-company review more independent than same-family?

variants.comment:
Removing the manual copy-paste between apps is a genuine workflow win, and the "only step in for GO / NO-GO" framing is where most people actually want to land.

One thing I'd push on: `--sandbox read-only` keeps Codex from writing, but the loop you describe still has Claude deciding which of Codex's findings to act on and which to dismiss. That judgment call — "this finding is valid, this one isn't" — is happening inside the same Claude context that wrote the original code. Which means the reviewer found it, but the author is still the one deciding what counts. For low-stakes changes that's fine. But for anything where the original implementation might have embedded a subtle wrong assumption, you still need a human in that triage step, not just the final GO/NO-GO. Curious whether you've found a way to structure that finding-triage that doesn't just route everything back through Claude's own judgment.
variants.rationale: Pushes on the gap where Claude still triages its own review findings.

slack_summary: Shares a workflow where Claude Code calls Codex CLI (read-only sandbox) for independent code review at the gate, so a different model reviews the work.

author_headline: CTO & Cofounder at Databricks, CS Professor at Berkeley
post_text:
We benchmarked coding agents on our own internal tasks at Databricks and learned a lot! https://lnkd.in/gjBCkQpP There are many surprising opportunities to lower cost, and many models including open source are now competitive.

Why did we build an internal coding benchmark? Public benchmarks like SWE-Bench are often over-tuned for, so we took real tasks our engineers did and curated test suites for them to see which agents can solve them end-to-end. These are the results on OUR sample of OUR codebase, so they are not meant to be comprehensive, but we think many companies can do a similar internal benchmark.

Several findings immediately stood out:

1) Many models are now competitive at the top tier, including open source.

2) GLM 5.2 in particular was a major step forward in open source coding agent performance, even in our own codebase that is VERY different from SWE-Bench and TerminalBench (lots of Scala, Go, Rust, Java, TypeScript, Protobuf, Jsonnet, etc).

3) Harnesses make a huge difference in cost-performance. The very simple Pi harness got the same success rate as harnesses from the LLM vendors with Opus and GPT 5.5, but at 2x less cost! Seems to be mainly due to smaller inputs to the LLM.

4) Cheaper per-token does not imply cheaper per-task. For example, Sonnet 5 costs less per token than Opus 4.8 but used more tokens, resulting in higher cost and lower quality. Interestingly my ex-student Lingjiao Chen had also found this on other tasks: https://lnkd.in/gcVsRR3F.

Read more in the blog how we built the benchmark and what we're doing with the findings. This is partly why we built Omnigent as a "meta-harness" to let developers switch and compose agents, and Unity AI Gateway to analyze and gate LLM usage centrally.

variants.comment:
The harness finding is the one that stuck with me most — same success rate as vendor harnesses at 2x lower cost, apparently just from smaller inputs to the LLM.

That raises a question I haven't seen answered cleanly: is the Pi harness winning because it's prompt-efficient by design, or because the tasks in your benchmark happen to fit its context window well? In other words, if you ran the same harness on a different codebase — say, one with sprawling cross-service context that's hard to compress — would the cost advantage survive, or does it only show up when the problem is naturally scope-bounded?
variants.rationale: Asks whether the Pi harness cost advantage is design-driven or task-shape dependent.

variants.comment:
The internal benchmark approach makes a lot of sense — public leaderboards have been gaming-targets long enough that they're mostly measuring training proximity, not real-world capability.

Here's what I keep wondering though: once your engineers know their own tasks are in the benchmark suite, you've recreated a version of the same contamination problem, just one level closer to production. The tasks are real, but the selection and curation process introduces its own filter. What stops this from becoming "our engineers implicitly optimize for tasks the harness can represent well" over time? Is there a rotation or blind-selection mechanism in how you keep adding new tasks?
variants.rationale: Surfaces the contamination-of-internal-benchmarks blindspot Matei invites others to consider.

variants.comment:
Finding #4 resonates from a different angle. When we were running our LLM system for a German bank before ChatGPT existed, we'd constantly get seduced by cost-per-token comparisons — but the number that actually mattered was cost per validated contract. A model that was 30% cheaper per token but needed two retries to get the output format right ended up costing more and introducing latency we couldn't absorb in production.

What we eventually learned was that you couldn't evaluate models in isolation — you had to evaluate them inside your actual pipeline, on your actual failure modes. Which is exactly what you're describing here. The insight that cheaper per-token does not imply cheaper per-task sounds obvious in retrospect, but almost nobody builds the measurement setup to actually see it until they've paid for it first.
variants.rationale: BankAI cost-per-validated-output parallel to finding #4 makes it substantive, not generic agreement.

slack_summary: Databricks benchmarked coding agents on their own internal tasks: open source now competitive, harnesses drive cost, cheaper-per-token != cheaper-per-task.

author_headline: Fractional CDO | Helping Scaling Tech founders turn data into faster decisions | Founder @ Valiotti Data
post_text:
Data strategy is "IT's problem."
Which is corporate for: "Please install the growth."
It usually starts innocently: data feels technical and IT feels technical. So we put the data team under IT and call it alignment → congratulations, you've just turned your future competitive advantage into a helpdesk category!!!
Now watch what happens.
The business asks, "Why are we losing customers in month three?"
Data replies, "The pipeline is stable."
Marketing asks, "Which channel actually drives margin?"
Data replies, "We migrated to a new warehouse."
Finance asks, "Can we trust this forecast?"
Data replies, "Access permissions are updated."
Everyone is technically correct, aaand… completely missing the point.
When data sits inside IT, it inherits IT's incentives: uptime, cost control, security, system health. All important. None of them answer, "Where should we place our next €5M bet?"
So the organization slowly drifts into a very modern tragedy:
Beautiful dashboards → Zero decisions.
Six definitions of "active customer" → One very awkward board meeting.
Three new tools → No one accountable for the number.
Then leadership concludes, "We need more data."
No. You need fewer hiding spots.
A data strategy is not a collection of tools. It's a set of hard choices.
→ Which metrics actually drive this business?
→ Who owns them?
→ What decision changes when they move?
→ What are we willing to stop doing because the signal says so?
That conversation doesn't belong in a ticketing system.
Execution is cheap now. AI will write your SQL. Vendors will promise a single source of truth by Friday. Interns can spin up a dashboard before lunch.
What's rare is alignment: around definitions, ownership, decisions that hurt a little.
If your data team only reports into IT, you don't have a data strategy.
You have very sophisticated plumbing. And plumbing is great, until you expect it to drive growth.
reason: Data org strategy post (data under IT dept) — no AI/LLMs/agents or startup/fundraising content

author_headline: Independent Journalist
post_text:
In 2016, a 2 year old boy was found naked on the streets of Nigeria.
Starving. Alone. Covered in worms.
His own family had abandoned him because they believed he was a witch.
A Danish woman named Anja knelt beside him in the dirt, gave him water from a bottle, and whispered a name for him.
Hope.
Because that is all he had left.
Doctors did not think he would survive the night. Daily blood transfusions. Emergency surgery. A body that weighed barely 3 kilos.
But Hope had other plans.
Fast forward to 2025.
That same little boy, now 12, walked across a graduation stage and collected his primary school diploma with one of the highest grades in his entire class.
He is deaf. He speaks through sign language and expresses himself through art.
His teachers call him "Little Picasso."
He has been accepted into a specialist school built around children just like him.
He was reunited with his father.
He is painting. Dancing. Laughing. Dreaming of becoming an artist.
The boy everyone left to die on a dirt road…
Is now the boy who makes people believe in miracles.
reason: Inspirational humanitarian story — no AI or startup content

Today, I'm so excited to share that I'm joining the Grindr product team full time.
