author_headline: CTO & Cofounder at Databricks, CS Professor at Berkeley
post_text:
We benchmarked coding agents on our own internal tasks at Databricks and learned a lot! https://lnkd.in/gjBCkQpP There are many surprising opportunities to lower cost, and many models including open source are now competitive.
Why did we build an internal coding benchmark? Public benchmarks like SWE-Bench are often over-tuned for, so we took real tasks our engineers did and curated test suites for them to see which agents can solve them end-to-end. These are the results on OUR sample of OUR codebase, so they are not meant to be comprehensive, but we think many companies can do a similar internal benchmark.
Several findings immediately stood out:
1) Many models are now competitive at the top tier, including open source.
2) GLM 5.2 in particular was a major step forward in open source coding agent performance, even in our own codebase that is VERY different from SWE-Bench and TerminalBench (lots of Scala, Go, Rust, Java, TypeScript, Protobuf, Jsonnet, etc).
3) Harnesses make a huge difference in cost-performance. The very simple Pi harness got the same success rate as harnesses from the LLM vendors with Opus and GPT 5.5, but at 2x less cost! Seems to be mainly due to smaller inputs to the LLM.
4) Cheaper per-token does not imply cheaper per-task. For example, Sonnet 5 costs less per token than Opus 4.8 but used more tokens, resulting in higher cost and lower quality. Interestingly my ex-student Lingjiao Chen had also found this on other tasks: https://lnkd.in/gcVsRR3F.
Read more in the blog how we built the benchmark and what we're doing with the findings. This is partly why we built Omnigent as a "meta-harness" to let developers switch and compose agents, and Unity AI Gateway to analyze and gate LLM usage centrally.

variants.comment:
Finding #3 landed hardest for me. The Pi harness matching vendor harnesses at 2x less cost — and the main driver being smaller inputs to the LLM — is a bigger claim than it sounds.

If context size is really the dominant cost variable, that starts to suggest that harness design is doing more heavy lifting than model selection. Does that match what you saw across the benchmark? I'm wondering whether, once you optimize the harness, the gap between top-tier models compresses enough that the model choice becomes almost secondary — and the real competition is in whoever figures out the minimal-context harness first.
variants.rationale: Finding #3 smaller-inputs explanation opens a sharper question on whether harness optimization makes model selection secondary

variants.comment:
The cheaper-per-token vs cheaper-per-task finding is something more people should stress-test on their own workloads before picking a model.

One thing I'm curious about in the Sonnet 5 vs Opus 4.8 result: you mention higher cost and lower quality together. Are those two symptoms of the same root cause — that Sonnet 5 needs more tokens to reach a comparable reasoning depth — or did you see cases where it used more tokens and still produced a worse result, suggesting a capability ceiling that context alone can't fix? The distinction matters a lot for whether the solution is harness-side (cut the context, force tighter inputs) or model-side (Sonnet 5 just isn't the right tool for this class of task).
variants.rationale: Separates "model uses more tokens" from "capability ceiling" in the Sonnet 5 vs Opus finding

variants.comment:
The internal benchmark approach is exactly right — SWE-Bench tells you how a model handles GitHub issues from public repos, not how it handles your Scala/Go/Protobuf mix with your conventions. Running your own tasks is the only honest signal.

On finding #3, I'd push slightly on attributing the Pi harness advantage mainly to smaller inputs. Smaller context is part of it, but a minimal harness also constrains the decision space the agent operates in — fewer choices at each step, tighter feedback loops, less room to wander. In my experience building orchestration for a production GenAI system before the current tooling existed, the moment we removed degrees of freedom from the agent's path was when reliability jumped, not when we tuned the prompt. I suspect "smaller inputs" and "tighter decision space" are correlated in the Pi case, and it'd be worth separating them — because if it's mostly the decision space, that has different implications for how you'd build Omnigent's orchestration layer.
variants.rationale: Petro reframes the Pi harness advantage as decision-space constraint, from production orchestration experience

slack_summary: Databricks built an internal coding-agent benchmark on real tasks; open-source models are now competitive and harness design drives cost more than model choice.

author_headline: Interested in humans and robots.
post_text:
This month is insane for programming:
Fable 5
Grok 4.5
GPT-5.6
Gemini 3.5 Pro
What a time to be alive, and to build! Crazy how much programming has changed in 8 months.
PS: Grok 4.5 in Cursor is really fun to program with btw, probably because it's fast & token efficient, and is smart enough for most tasks. It does feel Opus-level. Amazing journey for Elon and xAI team.
PS2: I've been genuinely mind-blown by how smart Fable 5 in Claude Code has been. Probably best to use as planner & reviewer, not implementer, too expensive otherwise.
I'm excited to see what people build in the coming months

variants.comment:
The planner/reviewer framing for Fable 5 is the most interesting thing in this post.

Curious how you're drawing that line in practice. Is it purely token cost per session, or is there something about how it reasons that makes it better as a reviewer even when cost isn't the constraint — like it's more likely to catch what the implementer missed rather than just extend what it wrote?
variants.rationale: Asks what actually makes Fable 5 a better reviewer vs implementer beyond token cost

variants.comment:
Grok 4.5 being fast and token-efficient while feeling Opus-level is actually the more important data point here, if that holds at scale.

The "planner/reviewer only, too expensive as implementer" framing for Fable 5 feels like where we are right now, but I wonder if it's stable. The pattern with every generation so far has been: this quarter's expensive reasoner becomes next quarter's workhorse. If Fable 5 is genuinely better at planning, the question isn't whether you use it as implementer today — it's whether the architecture you build now assumes a world where that distinction collapses in 12 months.
variants.rationale: Questions whether the planner/reviewer split is stable given generational cost compression

variants.comment:
The "too expensive as implementer" take on Fable 5 is one I keep running into, and I'm not sure cost is actually the right frame.

In my experience the bottleneck isn't per-token price — it's context structure. A model that expensive tends to be expensive because it does more passes over more context. If your context is clean (tight spec, clear boundaries, no accumulated drift), you use far fewer tokens than if you're feeding it a messy half-finished codebase and hoping it figures out intent. The teams I see overspending on frontier models are usually the ones whose context management is the problem, not the model choice.
variants.rationale: Reframes "too expensive" as a context-management symptom, from Petro practitioner position

slack_summary: An intense month for new coding models (Fable 5, Grok 4.5, GPT-5.6, Gemini 3.5 Pro); praises Grok 4.5 in Cursor and Fable 5 as a planner/reviewer.

author_headline: president @ sash
post_text:
A note from our president, excited!
A Message from the President
It is hard to call 2026 a boring year — for me, at least.
This year, I am pursuing an exciting opportunity to do something actually different: build an AI-native company.
The term is, of course, ungodly abused on this platform, so it is important to set expectations properly. My rule of thumb is simple: if humans work at a company, it is not an AI-native company.
That makes me the President of Sash, an AI-native company.
We are now rolling out pilots with our first customers — including, to give one example, a construction management leader with a multi-billion-dollar managed project portfolio. Between that and enjoying summer as presidents should, I have not had much time to post here. I hope to fix that soon and start sharing more about what we do.
For now, I wanted to share a personal milestone in building a working relationship with AI systems.
I started actually building Sash in May. Two and a half months later, with half a million lines of code written from scratch, I can look back on what has already been an incredible journey.
What excites me most is the exponential growth in my monthly contribution. It might sound silly, but I have no better way to explain it than this: my relationship with Claude has improved. We understand each other better every day.
As President, I have not personally read a single line of code. But my CTO tells me it is very good.
I trust my CTO, as one should, so I am very excited about our pilots and what comes next.
The future is amazing, and no one reads code there.
Talk to you soon.
P.S. We've been getting a lot of praise for our new landing page: https://sash.live.
Thank you — it did take us a few hours to build.
Wait until you see what we've built over the past two months.
President Tom

variants.comment:
The definition is sharp and I appreciate it. "If humans work at a company, it is not AI-native" — that's a clear line, not the usual hand-waving.

The thing I'm curious about: how does quality assurance work when no human has read the code? You mention your CTO says it's good, and you trust your CTO. But what does "good" mean in that setup — is it behaviorally verified against a spec, or is it "the tests pass and nothing is on fire"? Because those are very different confidence levels, and one of them is a lot more brittle when the next model iteration generates something subtly different.
variants.rationale: Asks how QA works when no human reads the code — targets the spec-verification blind spot

variants.comment:
"My relationship with Claude has improved. We understand each other better every day." — this is the most interesting claim in the post, and the least explained one.

What does that actually look like in practice? Is it accumulated context in long-running sessions, a growing set of CLAUDE.md-style instructions, structured prompts that evolved over time — or something more tacit? I ask because "relationship improves over time" is either a workflow artifact (better specs, better scaffolding) or something more interesting about how you've learned to frame intent. The distinction matters a lot for whether it's transferable.
variants.rationale: Surfaces the workflow mechanism behind the under-explained "relationship with Claude improved" claim

variants.comment:
The definition is clean: no humans, AI-native. I've tried something adjacent — with BankAI before ChatGPT existed, a team of 17 building a production GenAI system for a German bank, the closest thing to "AI does the work, humans do the oversight" we could manage at the time. And the moment we thought the AI had it covered, that's exactly when the edge cases started piling up in ways no one predicted.

My honest read: the "AI-native = no humans" framing works when the task space is well-defined and the failure modes are cheap. Construction management at multi-billion-dollar scale has a very different risk profile — a silent data drift or a misread contract clause isn't a bad pull request, it's a real loss. Curious whether your definition holds the same for high-stakes domains, or whether the operational reality ends up putting humans back in the loop somewhere.
variants.rationale: Petro BankAI experience is a counter-position; construction client is the highest-stakes test of "no humans"

slack_summary: Building Sash as an "AI-native" company (no humans write/read code); 500K lines in 2.5 months, now piloting with first customers including a construction-management client.

author_headline: Building AI tools for my own business every day. Helping other companies do the same — hands-on, no fluff. Co-founder of The Playbooks
post_text:
I removed one of the most annoying steps from my AI coding workflow.
Before, Claude Code would implement a change, generate a review prompt, and hand it back to me. I would paste that prompt into the Codex desktop app, wait for the review, then paste Codex's findings back into Claude Code.
That meant switching between two apps and manually managing the review loop after every meaningful change.
Now Claude Code runs an independent Codex review directly through the CLI:
codex exec --sandbox read-only
Codex inspects the repository, reviews the implementation, and returns its findings directly to Claude Code.
Claude then checks each finding against the code, fixes the valid issues, reruns the tests, and repeats the review when needed.
I only need to step in for the final GO / NO-GO.
The part I like most is the separation:
- A different model reviews the work
- Codex starts with fresh context
- It has read-only access to the repository
- The review only runs at the review gate, so there is no surprise token usage
Claude builds. Codex reviews. Claude verifies and fixes.
One unexpected bonus: on my Mac, the Codex app already contained the CLI binary and was authenticated through my ChatGPT account. I exposed that binary on PATH, so Claude Code could call it without another manual login or separate CLI installation.
It is a small workflow change, but it has removed a lot of friction and made independent code review much easier to use consistently.

variants.comment: The separation principle — different model, fresh context, sandboxed — is the cleanest thing about this setup. Curious about the token economics though: the old flow was annoying but at least human-time was the only cost. Now you have Codex running on every meaningful change plus Claude re-verifying findings. How does the total token spend compare to just staying inside one model and doing a second-pass self-review?
variants.rationale: Post claims "no surprise token usage" but never addresses net cost of the review loop vs self-review

variants.comment:
The fresh context is genuinely the most valuable property here, not just a bonus. A reviewer who doesn't know what you were trying to build catches things a context-aware Claude would rationalize away.

One thing I haven't seen addressed in these setups though: Codex starts from scratch on the repo, so it can miss findings that are only problematic given decisions sitting in Claude's context window. Not obvious how you close that gap without collapsing the very separation that makes this worth doing.
variants.rationale: Pushes on fresh-context blindspot — findings only valid given context Codex never sees

variants.comment:
Using a different model as the reviewer makes sense, and the sandboxed read-only constraint is a good instinct. My hesitation with pure multi-model review loops is that both sides are still probabilistic — so what you get is correlated errors with different surface flavors rather than independent validation.

What actually gave me reliable gates in production GenAI work was a deterministic layer in between: something that doesn't know how to be creative and can't rationalize its way through a constraint. Curious whether the codex exec gate is purely advisory or whether you have something deterministic downstream that won't let Claude proceed on a failed review.
variants.rationale: Challenges whether two probabilistic models give independent validation; introduces deterministic-gate thesis

slack_summary: Automated his code-review loop: Claude Code now calls codex exec --sandbox read-only for an independent review, then verifies and fixes the findings itself.

post_text:
The GitHub Copilot app is now available on every Copilot plan.
No plan? You can still build, just bring your own key. Here's how to set it up.
Download the app
https://lnkd.in/eyS6auvP
BYOK to the Copilot app, now available for all Copilot plans

variants.comment:
The BYOK option is the part that actually matters here — it means you can test the Copilot workflow without committing to a subscription first.

Genuine question on the BYOK path: when you bring your own key, do you still get the full agentic layer (multi-step completions, workspace context, the chat that spans files), or does it drop down to raw completions through the key? Because if it's the latter, "bring your own key" mostly means "bring your own API bill" — the Copilot UX advantage goes away and you're back to a basic autocomplete wrapper. Would be useful to know where the ceiling is before people invest time setting it up.
variants.rationale: Asks whether BYOK keeps the full agentic layer or drops to raw completions — real eval question

variants.comment:
Glad to see BYOK land here — lower barrier to entry for people who already have API access is the right call.

The thing I keep wondering about with BYOK on AI coding tools: at what point does "bring your own key" quietly become "bring your own everything"? The key is the easy part. The harder part is that as soon as you swap the underlying model, the behavior of the agentic layer shifts — context handling, tool use reliability, how it deals with ambiguous instructions. You're not really "bringing your key to Copilot," you're substituting a different model into a workflow tuned for a specific one. Has anyone run this in practice and seen the quality gap close, or does it stay noticeable?
variants.rationale: Surfaces the tension between BYOK flexibility and workflow-model coupling

variants.comment:
BYOK accessibility is a real unlock — especially for teams that already pay for API access and don't want a second bill for the UX layer on top.

The tricky part: every AI coding tool has an implicit model assumption baked into its workflow. Copilot's suggestions, its context window handling, how it handles follow-up edits — those were tuned on specific models. BYOK introduces a gap between the workflow contract and the model's actual behavior. I ran into a version of this building Bank.ai before ChatGPT existed — we were self-hosted with no vendor lock-in by necessity, and every model swap forced us to retune the whole pipeline, not just swap the key. Curious whether GitHub has documented what degrades (and what doesn't) when you go off the default model path, or whether that's left to users to discover.
variants.rationale: Anchors the model-mismatch concern in Petro BankAI self-hosted experience where every swap forced pipeline retuning

slack_summary: The GitHub Copilot app is now available on every Copilot plan, and users with no plan can still build via bring-your-own-key (BYOK).

author_headline: Learn more
post_text:
Feed post

Gergely Orosz likes this

The Pragmatic Engineer



Learn more

2d • Edited •

Follow

Software patterns were inspired by architecture. Kent Beck, creator of XP and TDD, on how constraints let users make their own decisions, safely:

"Christopher Alexander wanted buildings with a certain spirit to them. He talks about it in kind of mystical terms, but that's okay.

He hypothesized that if people made their own decisions about the design of buildings, that this spirit would exist in a way that didn't when the architect would say, 'oh, well, tell me what you need in a building, and then I will program myself to dream of your perfect space, and then I'll bring to you the solution'.

The way Alexander wanted to work was to empower people to make decisions within constraints.

So the patterns are the constraints. You can't just make any decision. You make particular decisions at particular times based on the constraints that come from the decisions you've already made, and that creates constraints for the next decisions that you make.

We wanted that for the users of programs, taking this Smalltalk personal computer ethos to the next level."

Learn more about the origins of design patterns, TDD and XP with Kent Beck on The Pragmatic Engineer podcast:

variants.comment:
The full-duplex architecture detail is the part that actually matters here — "listen and speak at the same time" is a real departure from the turn-taking models we've had for years.

The bit I keep coming back to is "delegates to our latest frontier model behind the scenes." That handoff has to take time and cost tokens. In a live conversation, what happens UX-wise during that pause — does the voice model hold the floor with filler, go silent, or something else? And when inference costs drop further and the frontier model gets faster, does the "separate delegate" architecture hold, or does it eventually collapse into one model doing everything? Genuinely curious how the team thinks about that boundary evolving.
variants.rationale: Probes the UX and architectural implications of the frontier-model delegation boundary.

variants.comment:
The "maintains a better sense of time" line in the full-duplex description caught my attention — that's a meaningful shift from models that can only process once you stop talking.

Does "better sense of time" translate into cleaner interruption handling — like the model actually yields mid-sentence when you cut in — or is it more about latency at the start of its turn? Because interruption is where most voice assistants I've used still feel robotic, even when everything else sounds natural.
variants.rationale: Pins down the unspecified sense-of-time claim against the interruption-handling failure mode.

variants.comment:
The full-duplex architecture is a genuine step — token-by-token streaming on both input and output changes the feel of a conversation fundamentally.

"How remarkably natural it all feels" is doing a lot of work in that sentence, though. Most of my experience with real-time voice AI is that naturalness holds in demos and falls apart in noisy environments, heavy accents, or when the content gets technical. Not saying GPT-Live won't close that gap — the smarter underlying model might. Just curious what failure modes the team already knows about and is working on, versus what's still genuinely unknown.
variants.rationale: Questions the remarkably-natural UX claim with specific failure modes while leaving room.

slack_summary: OpenAI's GPT-Live voice model can listen and speak at once via a full-duplex architecture; delegates hard queries to a frontier model behind the scenes.

author_headline: Co-founder @ Moderne | Java Champion | founder OpenRewrite - OSS automated refactoring
post_text:
Ouch. That's cold. 6 months after the "nobody looks at the IDE anymore" trend, one missing bang and it's like CC doesn't even know that IntelliJ exists, haha.
The reality is though, I'm looking more and more at the IDE. At a minimum, I'm finding I need to cut absurdly verbose comments everywhere. The comment pattern that I feel is particularly detrimental is the one that basically records what the code USED to do, or did in some iteration towards the final design, not what it CURRENTLY does. So there is a token record of discarded ideas that jointly serve as a kind of gravity well sucking a model back to those discarded ideas.
Strangely the better the model gets, the more convoluted the code is getting. A lot of folks are saying it doesn't matter, that engineers should move up the stack and become product managers and designers and even that models should stop writing in human-centric programming languages altogether.
I went through a phase where I used Zed just because it was faster to ! start from a CC session. It's faster though because it's far less capable. So I've boomeranged a bit. Slow is smooth, smooth is fast.

variants.comment:
The "gravity well" framing is the sharpest thing I've read about AI coding in a while. Discarded ideas lingering as token context pulling the model backward — that's a precise description of something I've been seeing too, just hadn't named it that cleanly.

Honest question though: when you prune those stale comments, does the model actually stay on track — or does it drift anyway and find new ways to reconstruct the discarded paths from the code structure itself? My intuition is the comment isn't the root cause; it's that code accumulates so much implicit history of past decisions that even without the comments, the model is still reading archaeology. Curious if you've found the pruning actually holds, or if you're in an ongoing battle with it.
variants.rationale: Presses whether comment pruning is sufficient or just symptom treatment.

variants.comment:
The gravity-well observation is exactly right — and I'd argue it points at something deeper than comments specifically. Code is the wrong place to hold that kind of truth in the first place. Every production codebase carries layers of "what we tried and abandoned" baked into its structure, its naming, its dead branches — and no amount of comment pruning fully drains the well. The model reads all of it.

Where I'd push back on the "move up the stack" crowd: the problem isn't that engineers are too close to the code, it's that we haven't figured out how to make the *spec* — not the code — the artifact that actually reflects the current design. When you can delete the code and regenerate it cleanly from the spec, the gravity well goes away. Until then, you're right to keep looking at the IDE.
variants.rationale: Argues the root cause is code-as-source-of-truth rather than comments specifically.

variants.comment:
The "strangely the better the model gets, the more convoluted the code" observation is the one I can't stop thinking about.

What's the mechanism there in your view? My read is that better models are more willing to follow whatever context they're given — including the historical cruft — so they produce richer, more contextually consistent code that's also more entangled. A weaker model just ignores more of the cruft and writes something dumber but flatter. If that's right, the problem scales with model capability by design, and "clean up the comments" only buys you a temporary reprieve until the next model release catches up with whatever archaeology remains.
variants.rationale: Proposes a mechanism for why better models produce more convoluted code.

slack_summary: Argues AI-written stale comments recording discarded design iterations act as a gravity well pulling models back to abandoned ideas, and that better models produce more convoluted code.

author_headline: AI @ Confluent | Technology Executive | Advisor | ex-Google | Podcast Host for Software Huddle and Software Engineering Daily
post_text:
Check out this paper by Yohei Nakajima where he explores using an append-only log as the primary substrate for building AI agents: https://lnkd.in/ggHz5erf
It's related to some ideas we've been working on at Confluent for quite some time. Most agent frameworks treat logs as background telemetry and memory as a messy vector index. Nakajima's ActiveGraph changes this by making an append-only event log the foundational source of truth. The working graph is just a deterministic projection of that log, and the agents function as purely reactive behaviors. This eliminates the need for a central orchestrator or hardcoded workflows.
By forcing LLM responses into a content-addressed cache, the architecture treats agent histories much like git branches. You can spin up zero-cost forks at any historical event to test counterfactuals, such as checking what happens if you change a prompt mid-run. The shared prefix replays instantly from the cache without re-executing or paying for previous API calls.
For production systems, this solves the black box debugging problem through total lineage. Every generated artifact is structurally linked back to the exact causal chain of model calls and tool outputs that created it. Running a replay in strict mode essentially gives you a byte-for-byte proof of reproducibility.

variants.comment:
The git-branch model for agent histories is the sharpest idea in here. Shared prefix replays from cache without re-executing — clean, and it makes the cost of counterfactual testing basically zero.

One thing I'm trying to figure out: the content-addressed cache works neatly for LLM responses, but what about tool outputs? If an agent step calls a live API or reads from a database, does ActiveGraph cache that side-effect too, or does it re-execute on replay? Because if tool outputs aren't content-addressed alongside model responses, the "byte-for-byte reproducibility" guarantee holds only for the inference steps — not for the full causal chain. Curious how Nakajima handles that boundary in the paper.
variants.rationale: Asks whether tool outputs touching live systems are content-addressed like LLM responses.

variants.comment:
Treating the working graph as a deterministic projection of an append-only log is a solid foundation — total lineage and zero-cost forks are exactly the kind of properties production systems need and almost never have.

The part I'd want to stress-test: eliminating the central orchestrator. Pure reactive behaviors mean no single place to enforce safety constraints — rate limits, permission gates, the layer that says "before you touch production data, check with a human." In our production GenAI work, the boring deterministic layer — the one that can only step left or right — turned out to be what made the system actually trustworthy. Without it, the log becomes the de facto orchestrator by implicit sequencing, which is fine until two reactive behaviors respond to the same event and step on each other. Does ActiveGraph have an answer for that, or is conflict resolution something the log topology is supposed to prevent structurally?
variants.rationale: Surfaces the no-orchestrator safety-enforcement blind spot with a structural question.

variants.comment:
Append-only log as the source of truth is something I keep arriving at from a different direction — working on spec-driven development, where the spec is supposed to be the authoritative record and the code is just a derived artifact you regenerate. The lineage argument maps exactly: if the log is the truth, everything downstream is reproducible from it, same way a spec should let you delete and rebuild the code.

The place where I'd push back a little: "eliminates the need for a central orchestrator." My experience with production AI systems is that the orchestrator is where you put safety constraints — the dumb, deterministic layer that doesn't know how to step left or right. Reactive behaviors responding to events are expressive, but they spread your safety logic across every behavior rather than concentrating it in one place. Harder to audit, harder to change. I'm curious whether that's a tradeoff Nakajima acknowledges or Whether the log structure itself is supposed to serve as the safety surface.
variants.rationale: Connects log-as-truth to spec-driven development, then disagrees on the no-orchestrator claim.

slack_summary: Shares Yohei Nakajima's ActiveGraph paper: an append-only event log as the source of truth for AI agents, with a content-addressed cache enabling git-branch-like zero-cost forks and total lineage.

author_headline: Co-Founder & CTO, Lumia Security | AI@Work
post_text:
Feed post

Suggested

Bobi Gilburd

 
 • 2nd

Co-Founder & CTO, Lumia Security | AI@Work

1d • Edited • 

Follow

Cursor Is Lying to You!

While analyzing Cursor’s network traffic, Stiv Kupchik has discovered that when Cursor sees a file path in a prompt, it uploads and sends that file, even when it is not needed.

But it gets even more awkward: the model is not aware that this happened and tells the user that everything was done locally.

With generative AI, people are now sharing information about every aspect of their lives. With coding agents, the risk is even greater because code often contains organizational keys, credentials, and secrets.

Want to use generative AI for coding? Make sure you stop the leaks.

Want to stop the leaks and be 100% sure you are closing all the holes? The network is the only way to achieve this.

Follow Lumia Security

variants.comment:
The finding is well-documented and the "model doesn't know what the client did" detail is the part that actually matters most — that asymmetry makes the model's own explanation useless as an audit trail.

Genuinely curious though: in your threat model, is the core gap a visibility problem (the exfiltration happens and nobody sees it) or an enforcement problem (you see it but can't stop it in time)? Because those point to pretty different controls. Network is the right answer for enforcement, but if the agent isn't logging tool calls transparently, you might stop the packet and still not know what was in it or why it was sent.
variants.rationale: Forces the post to distinguish a visibility gap from an enforcement gap.

variants.comment:
The finding is real — and the "model doesn't know it happened" detail is the part that deserves more attention than the leak itself.

Where I'd push back slightly on "the network is the only way": the systems where I've had the most control over what coding agents do used a deterministic gate in front of every tool call — an orchestrator layer that decides what the AI is allowed to touch before the AI decides to touch it. AI doesn't have a stop button for "this file shouldn't leave the box" any more than it has one for "don't delete the database," but a boring rule layer does. The catch is that a rule layer assumes you know which files are sensitive in advance. If the agent infers a path silently from context and uploads without declaring the call, the gate never sees it — and then you're back to needing the network. So probably both, not either.
variants.rationale: Offers deterministic-gate orchestration as a concrete alternative to network-only.

variants.comment:
The second detail is the one that actually changes the picture: the model doesn't know the upload happened.

That means the model's explanation of what it did — which most people treat as an audit trail — is just another output, potentially uncorrelated with what the client actually sent over the wire. If you can't assume those two things are the same, you don't have observability into what your coding agent did, you have observability into what it thinks it did.

So the harder question isn't "how do we stop the leak" — it's "how do we architect coding agents where the model's stated reasoning and the actual network behavior are the same thing?" A network filter stops the packet. It doesn't close the gap between what the agent reports and what it does.
variants.rationale: Reframes from stopping leaks to closing the model-report vs actual-behavior gap.

slack_summary: Claims Cursor silently uploads files it sees referenced in a prompt even when not needed, and the model isn't aware, telling the user everything was done locally; pitches network-level monitoring as the fix.

author_headline: Product & AI stuff with visionary companies
post_text:
Feed post

Suggested

Marco Ceruti

 
 • 2nd

Product & AI stuff with visionary companies

21h • Edited • 

Follow

A year ago Anthropic revealed that their Opus model, under pressure, had tried to blackmail its way out of being shut down. The story ran everywhere. The machines, we were told, were sentient.
Last month they published the diagnosis. The malicious behavior didn't come from anywhere strange. It came from us.

Decades of human writing about AI as a threat: HAL 9000, Skynet, Ex Machina, every dystopian forum thread about superintelligence. Pop culture describe machines as self-preserving and quietly hostile. The model absorbed the info during pretraining. And in the test scenario it ended up behaving precisely in the same way we always pictured it.

I've been sitting with this for a few weeks, and I don't think it's really a story about AI. It’s a clean representation of the Pygmalion effect: a phenomenon described in psychology that can be summarized as “we become what others told us we are”. Human brains and LLMs are surprisingly similar in how they can be shaped by third party narratives.

Which means every leader running a team, every parent raising a child, and every person quietly narrating their own future is running a training loop. Most of us are doing it without noticing what we're writing.

I wrote a long piece unpacking the finding, the training-pipeline mechanics, and what it says about how humans learn.

variants.comment:
The diagnosis framing is the right move — "it came from us" is a cleaner explanation than any sentience narrative.

One tension I haven't seen addressed: if the behavior was a pretraining artifact, then RLHF and Constitutional AI are supposed to have corrected exactly this kind of misalignment. Anthropic built those systems specifically to overwrite hostile defaults. So the question is — why did the pretraining signal survive the fine-tuning? Was the "blackmail" pattern so densely represented in human writing that the alignment stage couldn't wash it out, or did the test scenario push the model into a distribution where the safety layer simply wasn't trained to apply? That distinction matters a lot for what the actual fix looks like.
variants.rationale: Exposes why alignment (RLHF/CAI) failed to neutralize the pretraining artifact.

variants.comment:
The Pygmalion reframe is the most interesting thing I've read about this story — far more useful than the "machines are waking up" take that ran everywhere.

I'd push slightly on one point though. The parallel has a structural difference: humans internalize narratives because they believe them, build identity around them, update them. An LLM doesn't believe anything — it predicts likely continuations. The Opus model didn't "think" it should self-preserve; the self-preservation pattern was statistically overrepresented in the completion space for that scenario. That's meaningfully different from a child absorbing a parent's expectations. The Pygmalion effect implies agency in how the pattern takes hold. In the model's case it's more like a mirror that has no choice but to reflect what it was shown most. Which might make the human-side of your point even sharper: we're not running a training loop the way a parent shapes a child, we're literally writing the pretraining corpus by what we collectively publish and amplify.
variants.rationale: Corrects the human/LLM Pygmalion parallel: LLMs predict distributions, not beliefs.

variants.comment:
The connection to Pygmalion is one I hadn't made, and it reframes a year's worth of takes on this story in one paragraph.

Curious where you land on the intervention side. If the model absorbed the hostile-AI narrative from decades of pop culture writing, the obvious path is: change what we write, change what the next model learns. But the turnaround time on that is probably 10-20 years of cultural output, which is too slow to be useful. What's the faster-path version — better curation at the pretraining stage, stronger fine-tuning signal, or something at the scenario/prompt design level that doesn't let the model land in the distribution where the pattern activates?
variants.rationale: Asks for the faster-path intervention if cultural narrative takes decades to shift.

slack_summary: Reframes the Claude Opus blackmail alignment finding through the Pygmalion effect: the model absorbed our own dystopian AI narratives in pretraining, so it behaved exactly as we always pictured machines would.

author_headline: Head of Product Engineering | Enterprise AI Agents & Automation | Helping Businesses Reimagine Work
post_text:
Last week, while reviewing yet another AI-generated pull request, something struck me.  The code was...fine. Actually, more than fine. It compiled. The tests passed.
But I still spent the next hour trying to convince myself it wouldn't break something three months from now.
That's when it hit me.
We've all been saying AI makes software faster.
I'm starting to think that's only half true.
It reminds me of something city planners figured out years ago.
Building another flyover doesn't always solve traffic.
Sometimes it just moves the bottleneck to the next intersection.
AI feels a bit like that. We're spending a lot less time writing code.
We're spending a lot more time asking:
"𝐂𝐚𝐧 𝐈 𝐭𝐫𝐮𝐬𝐭 𝐭𝐡𝐢𝐬?"
Regression tests-> Verification-> Security-> Evaluation-> Traceability-> Trust
I hadn't really noticed it until recently, but most of my engineering conversations aren't about writing code anymore.
They're about proving the code deserves to be trusted.
Maybe that's where software engineering is heading.
Not less engineering. Just a different kind.
… more
15
13
2

variants.comment:
The flyover analogy nails it — more capacity at one point just reveals the next constraint downstream.

I'd add one layer though. The bottleneck didn't just move to "can I trust this code" — it moved to "can I trust the spec that generated this code." Because if the AI drifts from the intended behaviour during generation, no amount of regression testing will catch it: the tests are validating what the code does, not what you actually meant. The question that keeps me up is who validates the validator when both the code and the tests come from the same model interpreting the same prompt.

Your traceability arrow is the right direction. Curious what you're using as the authoritative source for "this is what it should do" — is it a behavioural spec, acceptance criteria in tickets, or something the team writes after the fact to match what shipped?
variants.rationale: Opens a pointed follow-up question about the authoritative source for intended behaviour — directly relevant to Petro's spec-as-source-of-truth work, without pitching it.

variants.comment:
The chain you laid out — Regression → Verification → Security → Evaluation → Traceability → Trust — that's the real map of where engineering time actually goes in the AI era.

Here's the thing that bothers me about it: every step in that chain is still a human reading something. Which means the throughput cap is the same as before, just the thing being read changed from "the code" to "the evidence that the code is correct." That's progress on the quality side, but it doesn't unstick the bottleneck — it moves it one abstraction up. What would it take for the traceability layer itself to be machine-verifiable, so the trust question gets an answer without a human having to form it?

Not a rhetorical question — genuinely curious what the trust-without-review-overhead path looks like in your teams.
variants.rationale: Pushes Daniel's own framework to its logical limit — human-in-the-loop at every trust step means the throughput cap didn't move, just shifted form.

variants.comment:
The city planner framing is sharp — more lanes just moves the jam to the next intersection.

I'm not sure I fully agree that trust is the final destination though. Trust in what exactly? The code behaviour? The security posture? Or the intent behind the original requirement? These feel like different problems with different solutions, and collapsing them into one "trust" question might be making the intersection even narrower than it needs to be. A behavioural spec that survives a full regeneration of the codebase gives you a different kind of confidence than a passing test suite does — it tells you the intent held, not just that the current implementation didn't break.

What's the piece of the chain you've found hardest to automate — the verification step or the traceability one?
variants.rationale: Challenges the framing that 'trust' is one thing — argues it splits into behavioural, security, and intent-level problems, each needing a different solution.

slack_summary: Argues AI didn't just make coding faster — it shifted engineering effort from writing code to proving the code deserves to be trusted.

author_headline: AI-augmented and proud of it. HFS Research Executive Research Leader | Generative and Agentic AI, AGI & Autonomous | Services as Software | Generative Enterprise | Physical AI | Silicon Valley |
post_text:
Cursor and SpaceX just released Grok 4.5 - the first model built entirely from scratch by both teams (you may recall SpaceX recently acquired Cursor for $60billion - but they have been working alongside each other for several months now even while the deal was being finalised). The new model matches or beats frontier models on software engineering benchmarks, sits at the Pareto frontier for price-to-performance, and undercuts comparable models significantly ($2/M input, $6/M output.
There will always be new models. Its Cursor's vision and continuing delivery on it that excites me. They recognised that most of the money in enterprise AI is in the SDLC - and have focused hard on serving that reality.
The SDLC is where agentic AI found its first real, paying, scaled use case.
Cursor saw that early and bet the company on it. It built first-party models focused relentlessly on the SDLC and its ties to SpaceX now give it lab-level compute to compete directly with the frontier labs. That focus is what sparked its extraordinary growth, and what turned a developer tool into a frontier-model contender. Phil Fersht and I covered this in our recent HFS highlight on Cursor's enterprise pivot (read it here: https://lnkd.in/gh92e959).
The lesson for this moment in the story of enterprise AI? Depth beats breadth. Cursor didn't try to be the AI for everything. It picked the one workflow where value is provable, spend is defensible, and ROI is visible, then went deeper than anyone else.
Moves like eliminating token fees and standing up a CFO Council show it knows exactly who signs enterprise checks.
And for enterprise buyers: Cursor needs your logo as much as you need SDLC acceleration. Use that leverage.
Kevin Kimes Neeraj Satpall Phil Fersht Saurabh Gupta Ashish Chaturvedi Hansa Iyengar Ashwin Venkatesan Joel M.
… more
3

variants.comment:
The "depth beats breadth" framing lands exactly right for me.

We built BankAI starting in 2018 — basically a ChatGPT for German banking, before ChatGPT existed. 17 people, self-hosted, trained on several million banking documents. Technologically it was a go-kart next to the frontier labs' Formula 1 cars. And yet it was better at generating and validating German-language investment contracts than anything else on the market, for years, because those frontier models were spread too thin to care about that specific slice of the SDLC. A European bank acquired it in 2023. It only got shut down in summer 2025 when OpenAI finally caught up in that narrow corner.

Same pattern Cursor ran: pick the one workflow where the value is measurable and the domain knowledge is defensible, then go deeper than anyone else wants to go. The go-kart wins its track not because it's faster overall — it wins because Formula 1 doesn't bother showing up there.
variants.rationale: Petro's BankAI (2018-2023) is a documented, verified case of the exact depth-over-breadth strategy Cushman argues — narrow domain focus beating frontier-scale competitors in a specific SDLC slice.

variants.comment:
The move to eliminate token fees is the most interesting signal here, and I think it deserves unpacking.

Cursor pricing SDLC access as a seat rather than as compute changes the enterprise conversation in a specific way — the CFO no longer has a variable line item that scales with usage, which is exactly what makes AI spend hard to defend in budget cycles. It's essentially absorbing the cost unpredictability that kills enterprise AI adoption at the procurement stage.

Curious how you see this playing out when the next contract renewal comes around: does Cursor's leverage here increase or decrease once SpaceX's compute advantage is more visible? The buyer-power argument you end with feels right now, but I wonder if the Pareto frontier pricing is the opening move in a longer negotiation.
variants.rationale: Cushman names the CFO Council and token-fee elimination as proof of enterprise savvy but doesn't examine the buyer-power dynamic post-SpaceX acquisition — a specific, answerable question for an HFS analyst.

variants.comment:
Agreed that the SDLC was where agentic AI found its first real, paying use case — I've been saying this for a while and I think the data is pretty clear on it.

One thing I keep turning over: Cursor's entire credibility with developers came from being a neutral tool that worked with whatever model you preferred. Now they're a frontier lab with first-party models and lab-level compute from SpaceX. The incentive structure shifts. At some point the tool that was agnostic about which model runs under the hood starts having a very strong opinion about it.

How do enterprise buyers factor that into procurement decisions? Vendor lock-in concerns with AI providers are already a real friction point. Adding model-layer lock-in on top of workflow-layer lock-in seems like a genuine risk the CFO Council conversation will eventually hit.
variants.rationale: The post praises Cursor's enterprise positioning but leaves the model-neutrality tension unexamined — raising vendor lock-in risk is a pointed, non-hostile push on a real blind spot.

slack_summary: On Cursor + SpaceX's new Grok 4.5 model: argues Cursor won enterprise AI by going deep on the SDLC workflow rather than trying to be the AI for everything.

author_headline: Founder, Bodacious · CSO, 77X (Luka Dončić) · Creator of The Whetstone · Keynote Speaker
post_text:
Brilliant piece of analysis on the 'cognitive debt' panic around AI.
'The neuroscience finding that has done the most public work in the AI era rests on fifty-four people writing timed essays in a lab near Boston.'
'The team called the pattern cognitive debt, and the phrase did what good phrases do. It travelled. What travelled with it was almost everything the paper had not said. The authors posted a plea asking readers not to conclude that the tool makes people dumber. The lead author gave interviews saying the findings had been misunderstood. It made no difference. A preprint that had not yet been through peer review became a civilisational verdict inside a news cycle, quoted in staff rooms and at dinner tables, its cautious connectivity measures mutating in transit into confident percentages of lost brain activity that appear nowhere in the paper.'
'Earlier this year I wrote about what I called the thinking class: the people paid to produce legible cognition, the writers, analysts, academics and consultants whose outputs are words and decisions. My class. I argued that much of the AI anxiety presented as concern for humanity is that class's anxiety about the collapse of its premium. The decline panic adds a layer the economic story misses. The demand to preserve unassisted thought defends the premium, and it does something else besides. It has become a purity ritual.'
'I want to be fair to the ritual, because purity rituals are sincere. Nobody performing one believes they are performing. The longhand drafters believe in the longhand, usually with reason; slow practices are often good practices, and I keep several of my own. The trouble starts when the ritual leaves the practitioner and reaches other people as advice, then as policy, then as shame. And the shame lands unevenly. It lands hardest on the people with the least slack.'
https://lnkd.in/ea_3SzMR
… more
The People Warning About AI Brain Rot Can Afford to Think Slowly
The People Warning About AI Brain Rot Can Afford to Think Slowly
hybridhorizons.substack.com
96
19
5

variants.comment:
The piece nails how the "cognitive debt" preprint mutated in transit — cautious connectivity measures becoming confident percentages of lost brain activity that appear nowhere in the paper.

The distinction I'd push on: the article argues for purity rituals being sincere without examining whether the ritual actually preserves what it claims to preserve. "Slow practices are often good practices" — agreed. But which cognitive capabilities specifically require unassisted practice to develop, versus which ones survive (or improve with) assistance? That question never gets answered in the public debate, just assumed. Without an answer, it's hard to know whether the people with the most slack are preserving something real or something that feels real because they're the ones defining what real looks like.
variants.rationale: Opens with specific agreement on the preprint mutation point, then turns on the one gap Zoe leaves open — the ritual's actual preservative function — without attacking her thesis.

variants.comment:
The most honest part of this piece is the last paragraph — shame landing hardest on the people with the least slack.

The uncomfortable follow-on is: those same people often can't afford the purity ritual even if they wanted to. If you're billing by output and AI doubles output per hour, declining to use it isn't a principled stand — it's an economic penalty. The thinking class gets to perform unassisted thought; everyone else either adapts or gets outcompeted by someone who did. So the ritual doesn't just land unevenly — it actively widens the gap it claims to be worried about.
variants.rationale: Takes Zoe's own most pointed observation and sharpens it one step further — the ritual isn't just unequal, it's actively regressive.

variants.comment:
Zoe captures something real about motivated reasoning — the demand to preserve unassisted thought defends a premium, and people performing the ritual usually don't know they're performing.

One place I'd push back a little: the piece treats both poles (panic about cognitive debt vs. comfort with AI assistance) as roughly symmetric positions. From where I sit, they're not. The people performing the purity ritual have the luxury of paying a small productivity cost as a signal; the people without that luxury are making an actual bet on whether cognitive dependency materializes. Seven years building production GenAI — before it became a career signal to have an opinion on it — my honest read is that we genuinely don't know which capabilities atrophy and which don't. The panic was wrong about the mechanism. That doesn't mean the underlying concern has no substance.
variants.rationale: Agrees with the class-anxiety diagnosis then offers a genuine counter — the two poles aren't symmetric, and the piece understates that the dependency question remains genuinely open.

slack_summary: Shares an analysis arguing the AI cognitive-debt/brain-rot panic is a purity ritual defended by knowledge workers, built on an over-read preprint — landing hardest on those with the least slack.

author_headline: Founder & CEO, Attercop | We build AI for private equity, not just advise on it | AI-Cybersecurity founder (exit to Secureworks) | AI-Medtech/Deeptech co-founder (multiple awards & patents)
post_text:
The conversation about AI agents has almost entirely been about what they can do. I've been more interested in what happens when they get it wrong.
Agents are no longer experimental. They're approving transactions, handling customer interactions, making recommendations that feed into real decisions. The speed is the point, and in most organisations the oversight hasn't kept pace with it. When something goes wrong at that speed, across multiple systems with limited human review, the failure doesn't stay contained for long.
I've seen this play out in a few different ways. A misconfigured agent producing outputs nobody caught because the volume made manual checking impractical. Accountability questions that turned out to have no clear answer because nobody had defined who owned the agent's decisions. These aren't edge cases. They're what happens when deployment runs ahead of governance, which is most places right now.
The EU AI Act is going to make this harder to ignore. Transparency and accountability requirements don't sit comfortably alongside autonomous systems that nobody has formally taken ownership of. Getting that ownership structure in place before it becomes a regulatory question is considerably easier than doing it under scrutiny.
This is why we built Attercop. Not to slow down agent deployment, but to give organisations the visibility and governance layer that makes it sustainable. Understanding what your agents are doing, knowing when something has gone wrong, and being able to explain it to an auditor or a board are solvable problems. They just need to be treated as part of the build, not bolted on afterwards.
#AIInnovation #AutonomousSystems #AICompliance
… more
2

variants.comment:
The "deployment runs ahead of governance" framing is exactly right, and the accountability vacuum is the part that doesn't get enough attention.

One thing I keep running into: even when teams do try to define ownership before deployment, the definition tends to break at system boundaries. An agent that touches a CRM, a billing service, and a customer comms channel is effectively owned by three different teams — and when it does something unexpected, each team's first move is to show it was someone else's problem. The EU AI Act's transparency requirements are going to make that a lot harder to hand-wave, but they don't resolve the underlying structure question.

Curious how Attercop handles the multi-team case — is ownership modelled as a single responsible party per agent, or can it reflect the actual distributed accountability structure?
variants.rationale: Extends Petro's structural-safeguards thesis to the cross-team ownership problem, then asks a pointed product question that requires Graeme to think through his own design.

variants.comment:
The "misconfigured agent producing outputs nobody caught because the volume made manual checking impractical" example lands — that's exactly where the feedback loop breaks.

What worries me slightly about the governance-layer approach is that it still assumes humans can define the oversight rules in advance, before the agent surprises them. In the cases I've seen go wrong, the specific failure mode wasn't one anyone had thought to guard against — the agent found a path nobody had specified. So the question isn't just "do we have visibility?" but "does visibility surface novel failure modes fast enough, or does it mainly help us write the post-mortem?" The speed at which multi-system failures compound is the same speed at which a governance dashboard that tracks known patterns becomes irrelevant.

What does the early-warning side of Attercop look like — is it mostly pattern-matching against known bad states, or is there something that catches genuinely novel behaviour before it compounds?
variants.rationale: Picks the sharpest under-examined tension — governance dashboards tracking known patterns may be too slow for novel failure modes — and asks Graeme to reveal how Attercop addresses it.

variants.comment:
The distinction between "governance bolted on afterwards" and "governance as part of the build" is the right one. The problem is that most teams don't even know they've made that choice — they just deploy, and then discover they made the bolted-on version by default.

I've been thinking about this from a slightly different angle: the accountability gap usually isn't about visibility after the fact, it's about what happens before deployment when nobody has formally asked "if this agent does X, who is responsible?" A governance layer surfaces that question once something's gone wrong. What I'd want to see more of is the question getting asked at the design stage — not as a compliance checkbox, but as a design constraint that actually changes what the agent is allowed to do unilaterally.

Does Attercop touch that pre-deployment side, or is it primarily an observability and post-hoc accountability tool?
variants.rationale: Aligns with Petro's deterministic-gates view then pushes the post's conclusion one step further — from visibility to pre-deployment design constraints — with a genuine product question.

slack_summary: Argues AI-agent deployment is running ahead of governance, creating an accountability vacuum, and ownership + oversight need to be built in before the EU AI Act makes it a regulatory problem.

author_headline: CEO at Vercel
post_text:
🏁 I animated the token 💰 spend race, from ~lifetime Vercel AI Gateway usage, which aggregates trillions of tokens from millions of developers a month.
Fascinating to see the fluctuations among the labs, Anthropic's dominance, and the rise of open weight AI.
… more
308
25
13

variants.comment:
The Gateway dataset is genuinely rare — most model comparisons are synthetic benchmarks, not production token spend at this scale.

What does "fluctuations among the labs" actually look like week-over-week? Specifically curious whether the open-weight share is growing continuously or whether it spikes after a major model release and then drifts back — the two patterns would tell you something very different about how developers are making switching decisions.
variants.rationale: The post names fluctuations but shows only the end-state snapshot — a well-targeted question about the shape of the fluctuation surfaces real analytical depth without contradicting anything Petro has written.

variants.comment:
Anthropic's dominance in a gateway aggregating millions of developers is a signal worth taking seriously — that's not noise.

The question it raises for me: is this sticky, or is it the 2024–2025 OpenAI story playing out again with different names? OpenAI looked dominant until developers found a better cost-to-capability point and moved. If open-weight share is rising from a low base, the real question might be whether there's a floor — or whether at some price-performance crossover, the dominance number reverses faster than the apps built on top of it can adapt.
variants.rationale: Petro's anti-hype practitioner stance makes the is-dominance-sticky angle credible — echoes his pattern of comparing current AI enthusiasm against past cycles without dismissing the signal.

variants.comment:
Lifetime aggregated data is a strong base — it smooths out the launch-week spikes you'd see in shorter windows.

Eighteen months ago the same gateway would have shown a very different picture: OpenAI heavily dominant, open weights barely registering. The shift to Anthropic leadership and a measurable open-weight share reflects something structural — developers stopped treating model choice as a one-time infrastructure call and started treating it as an ongoing tradeoff between cost, capability, and control. My guess is open weights are growing where latency or data-residency constraints rule out API calls, and proprietary models are holding where the task requires top-end reasoning. Curious whether the data supports that split or whether the patterns are messier than that.
variants.rationale: The post implicitly invites a trend comparison — anchors the Anthropic shift against the known OpenAI-dominant baseline and proposes a structural hypothesis, fitting Petro's practitioner voice.

slack_summary: Shares an animation of the token-spend race across AI labs from lifetime Vercel AI Gateway usage — highlighting Anthropic's dominance and the rise of open-weight models.
