author_headline: Co-founder & CEO @ Kilo
post_text:
Excited to announce the latest evolution of Kilo Code for JetBrains IDEs: a JetBrains native plugin that is faster, more reliable and integrates seamlessly with the rest of the JetBrains ecosystem.
Give it a try and share your thoughts in the comments!

variants.comment:
The move to a native JetBrains plugin is a real step — the JetBrains API surface gives you access to things a generic extension layer simply can't reach.

Curious what "integrates seamlessly with the JetBrains ecosystem" means in practice. Is Kilo now using PSI (the Program Structure Interface) for code context — or is it still sending raw text to the model the way most plugins do? The difference in context quality between those two approaches is pretty significant, especially on larger Java/Kotlin codebases.
variants.rationale: The announcement claims seamless integration but gives no specifics; a PSI vs. raw-text context question requires a real answer and signals technical credibility.

variants.comment:
Going native on JetBrains makes sense — the gap between a wrapper and a proper IDE plugin is real, and the JetBrains project model gives you context depth that generic extensions can't match.

One thing I keep wondering about in this space: JetBrains is folding their own AI assistant (AI Pro) deeper into the IDE with every release. What does the sustainable moat look like when the IDE vendor itself is in the AI coding race? Is the bet that Kilo's model quality stays ahead, or that the integration depth you're building right now becomes defensible on its own?
variants.rationale: JetBrains shipping AI Pro creates real platform-risk tension a founder should have an answer to; useful, not hostile.

slack_summary: Announcing Kilo Code's new native JetBrains IDE plugin — faster, more reliable, asks for feedback.

author_headline: cofounder/CTO, honeycomb.io
post_text:
Years ago, I predicted that columnar storage would remake the landscape of observability tools.
I thought we'd see a wave of new columnar-backed observability products grow up to displace the old, creaky, three pillars model with better tech that is faster, easier, cheaper, and more efficient in every way.
I was half right.
Observability vendors, you are chasing the wrong ball. Datadog's great weakness is not their price point, it's their product. They are built on a 30+ year old architecture model that is obscenely inefficient. You can do so much better. You have the tools.
Clickhouse, good job. You don't make me want to stab my eyes out. In this market landscape, that's high praise.
https://lnkd.in/gp-gdcdb

variants.comment:
The "wrong ball" framing is right — chasing Datadog on price is a race to the bottom, while the architecture gap is the actual moat to attack.

The part I keep wondering about: even if you build columnar-backed, faster, cheaper — what does the switching cost look like? Observability tooling is deeply embedded. Agents, alert configs, dashboards, on-call runbooks — all tuned to whatever the team already runs. The product has to be so obviously better on day one that the migration pain feels worth it. Has any of the newer generation actually cracked that, or are they still mostly winning on greenfield workloads?
variants.rationale: Charity leaves switching costs unaddressed; asking about greenfield vs migration wins surfaces a real tension without being adversarial.

variants.comment:
The architecture critique lands — 30-year-old data models duct-taped together are genuinely hard to query against, and columnar changes what's possible at the query layer.

Here's the question that nags me: who actually builds this disruptor? The companies best positioned technically (ClickHouse, DuckDB, Databricks) aren't observability-native — they don't own the instrumentation layer, the SDK ecosystem, or the on-call workflow muscle memory. Pure-play observability startups have the domain expertise but usually lack the storage engineering depth to build the substrate from scratch. Is the real bet on a storage company that learns the observability domain, or an observability company that rebuilds on modern storage? Those are very different product bets with very different timelines.
variants.rationale: The post identifies the opportunity but not who can seize it; the storage-vs-domain question invites Charity's insider view.

variants.comment:
Agreed that the product is the weak point — not the price tag. Competing on discounts against a 30-year-old incumbent is a slow way to lose.

Where I'd push back slightly: columnar storage is a necessary condition, but probably not sufficient. The observability tools I've watched teams struggle with weren't slow because of storage — they were painful at the investigation layer. The query interface, the correlation between signals, the moment when an alert fires and you have to figure out why. That's still largely a UX and data-model problem, and it doesn't automatically get solved by swapping the storage layer. The vendors who crack it will need to rebuild the investigation workflow from scratch, not just the backend. Curious how much of that work you think is storage-constrained versus just product design.
variants.rationale: Pushes back that the investigation UX layer is a separate, equally hard problem grounded in practitioner experience.

slack_summary: Argues observability vendors should attack Datadog on its dated, inefficient architecture, not on price; praises ClickHouse.

author_headline: CEO @ Roomote • AI engineer for the important work nobody wants to do | Built Roo Code (3M+ installs)
post_text:
Excited to talk about the state of AI (quite a moving target!) later today.
This Dot Labs
23h •
Follow
Happening tomorrow!
Join us for the next State of #AI Leadership featuring Thor Schaeff from Google DeepMind, alongside Matt Rubens (Roomote), Elliott Fouts (This Dot Labs), and Rob Ocel (This Dot Labs).
We'll discuss how organizations can develop internal #AI champions who build trust, accelerate adoption, and help turn AI initiatives into lasting capabilities.
📆  Tomorrow, July 9
⏰  12:30 PM ET
🎟️  Free and open to all
Register using the link in the comments 👇

variants.comment:
The framing of "AI champions who build trust" resonates — that trust-building piece is often the part organizations underinvest in compared to just rolling out tools.

One thing I'd be curious to hear the panel address: what actually makes someone an effective AI champion versus just an enthusiastic early adopter? In the teams I've watched, the difference tends to be whether the champion can articulate the failure modes honestly — not just show what works. The ones who build lasting trust are usually the ones who publicly say "we tried this and it didn't work, here's why," not just the ones who post wins. Curious if that maps to what you're seeing at Roomote.
variants.rationale: Picks at the unstated variable — what distinguishes a genuine champion from an adoption cheerleader — which is real and debatable.

variants.comment:
The "lasting capabilities" piece is what I keep coming back to when this topic comes up.

Most organizations I've seen measure AI adoption by speed — how fast people started using the tools, how many seats are active. What I haven't seen measured much is whether people are actually thinking through the tool or just getting faster at offloading decisions to it. Those are two very different outcomes, and only one of them compounds. The internal champion role seems critical precisely because someone has to hold that distinction in the organization — but I wonder if the incentives usually push champions toward the adoption metrics instead. Interested in whether this comes up in the panel.
variants.rationale: Pulls toward the blind spot the post doesn't examine: whether adoption metrics track the right thing.

variants.comment:
Accelerating adoption and building lasting capabilities are not always the same goal, and I think that tension is worth naming.

From what I've seen, the orgs that move fastest on adoption sometimes create dependency rather than capability — people learn to get answers from AI but lose the habit of questioning the answers. Fast adoption into the wrong pattern locks in harder than slow adoption done right. So I'd push back gently on "accelerate adoption" as the frame for what a champion should optimize for. The more useful metric might be: are people on this team getting better at thinking through hard problems, or just getting faster at offloading them? Curious if the panel has a take on how to keep those two things from drifting apart.
variants.rationale: Gently disputes the premise that faster adoption equals better outcome; redirects toward the more meaningful metric.

slack_summary: Promoting a webinar panel on how orgs develop internal AI champions who build trust and turn AI into lasting capabilities.

author_headline: Building Convia — Enabling thought leaders to be the talent, not full time content creators | MXP AI Native Startup Studio | Host, Facing Disruption
post_text:
Who knew the best way to fight deep fakes was to make it an out of the box feature?.... /sarcasm
--------------
Meta has introduced Muse Image, its first in-house AI image generation model, allowing users to generate AI images using public Instagram profiles as visual references.
Under the feature, users can include the @username of a public Instagram account in a prompt, enabling Meta AI to create images based on that person's publicly available photos. According to Meta's support documentation, public accounts are included by default unless users choose to opt out. Meta also says account owners are not notified when their public content is used for AI-generated images.
- TO DISABLE THIS -
Users who do not want their public posts and reels to be available for these AI features can disable the option through Instagram's settings under Sharing and Reuse. (Or set your account to private)
#ai #privacy #ethics #trust #deepfake

variants.comment:
The sarcasm lands, but the deeper move here is that opt-out defaults at platform scale aren't a design oversight — they're a deliberate product choice dressed as user empowerment. Platforms learned a long time ago that most users never touch settings, so "opt-out" is functionally the same as "always on."

The question that interests me: what would it actually take to make opt-in the default for personal likeness generation? Not whether it's right — clearly it is — but what's the structural pressure that gets a platform to absorb the user friction of requiring explicit consent? GDPR created that pressure for data collection in some markets. Is there an equivalent coming for AI-generated likeness?
variants.rationale: Validates the sarcasm then sharpens into a structural design question (GDPR analogy) rather than a moral one.

variants.comment:
The sarcasm is fair. But I'd push the framing a bit further: the real issue isn't that Meta built deepfakes into a product. It's that at platform scale, opt-out defaults aren't really a policy — they're an outcome. Platform research consistently shows fewer than 3-5% of users ever change default settings on social platforms. So "opt out if you want" is operationally equivalent to "everyone's likeness is included."

The deepfake angle almost understates it. The honest description of what's been shipped here is: a mechanism that converts every public Instagram account into a training reference, without notice, at a global scale, with the knowledge that the overwhelming majority of people will never know the toggle exists.
variants.rationale: Reframes the issue from deepfakes feature to opt-out defaults as an outcomes-by-design mechanism, with the 3-5% stat for teeth.

variants.comment:
The sarcasm is exactly right, and it points at something worth naming precisely. Meta knows — and their researchers absolutely know — that opt-out at this scale is functionally opt-in-for-everyone, because the awareness gap is enormous. If you're not already reading tech news on this, you're included by default and will likely remain so.

Curious what you think the accountability lever is here. The tool exists, the opt-out is buried in settings, and legislators move slowly. Does the pressure realistically come from regulators, from advertisers, or from creators with enough reach to make noise? Genuine question — I don't see a clean answer.
variants.rationale: Names what Meta's researchers provably know, then asks a specific accountability question the founder audience has opinions on.

slack_summary: Criticizes Meta's new Muse Image model, which lets users generate AI images from public Instagram profiles by default (opt-out).

author_headline: CTO at Ice Lake LLC
post_text:
I really love this. One of the most productive AI tools in most widespread use today, Claude Code, is in fact not mostly LLM-based at all, but good old-fashioned C++, with deterministic logic. What's old is new again! And it uses something called ast-grep! If I wait long enough, my legacy skills will be relevant and I can claim to be a Claude Code "internals expert." All we need now is hardware that can generate true Asynchronous System Traps ("AST"s). Seriously, the software pattern implied by Claude Code's implementation points the way forward. Build truly useful deterministic software but wrap it in an LLM to provide the inputs, configure the execution context, etc. This appears to be what has happened with Unqork with some guidance from David Ferrucci [corrected! not "Berlucci"], and what several workflow software vendors are doing in the name of "guardrails". Several variations of rules engines with some chat wrapped around it so you can call it AI to make the sale. It is possible that the announcement of the murder of the SAAS world by LLMs is way too premature.!
Behind Claude Code's curtain: generative grammars doing the heavy lifting
https://lnkd.in/gVdrNMcg

variants.comment:
The "build deterministic software, wrap it in an LLM" framing is exactly right — and it's what makes Claude Code actually reliable at scale.

One small pushback though: calling it "not mostly LLM-based" slightly undersells the architecture. The LLM isn't just a nice input layer — it's doing the hard part of translating ambiguous human intent into precise tool calls. The deterministic layer only looks clean because the LLM is doing a lot of interpretation work upstream. Remove the LLM and you're back to a command-line tool nobody wants to learn.

The more interesting question to me is what this pattern implies for SaaS. It's not that LLMs won't disrupt SaaS — it's that the disruption vector is different from what people predicted. Not "LLM replaces the app" but "LLM replaces the UI, and the deterministic engine underneath gets swapped out or commoditized." The SaaS world might be fine. The front-end interaction model is what's cooked.
variants.rationale: Agrees with the core thesis but adds nuance that the LLM does the harder upstream work; pivots to the SaaS disruption vector.

variants.comment:
The pattern you're describing — deterministic engine + LLM wrapper — is exactly what we've been converging on, and Claude Code is a good existence proof that it works.

What I keep wondering: when the deterministic layer gets commoditized (and it will, ast-grep is already open source), where does the moat actually sit? Right now the answer seems to be "proprietary domain logic and data" — same as pre-LLM SaaS. Which might mean you're right that the murder of SaaS was announced too early, but for a different reason than people think. Not because LLMs can't replace apps, but because the part of the app that matters was never the UI layer to begin with.
variants.rationale: Pulls the thread on the SaaS question Steve left open, using commoditization of ast-grep as the pressure point.

variants.comment:
The point about workflow vendors selling "rules engines with some chat wrapped around it" is worth sitting with.

Curious whether you see a meaningful difference between those vendors and what Claude Code is doing — or is it the same pattern executed at different quality levels? Because my read is the gap is mostly in how well the deterministic layer is designed: Claude Code's C++/ast-grep layer does real semantic code understanding, whereas most "guardrails" vendors have a YAML rule file and call it a day. If that's right, then the pattern isn't what separates the good tools from the bad ones — the quality of the deterministic core is.
variants.rationale: Steve lumps Claude Code and guardrails vendors together; surfaces a real distinction (semantic understanding vs YAML rules).

slack_summary: Notes Claude Code relies on deterministic C++/ast-grep logic wrapped in an LLM, and argues the death of SaaS by LLMs may be premature.

author_headline: Deepdives on software engineering, tech careers and industry trends. Writing The Pragmatic Engineer, the #1 software engineering newsletter on Substack. Author of The Software Engineer's Guidebook.
post_text:
I did my first-ever AMA episode. Your questions, my answers!
Watch or listen:
• YouTube: https://lnkd.in/gAMEz2vc
• Spotify: https://lnkd.in/g7djsGyS
• Apple: https://lnkd.in/gcZ3-WfT
Brought to you by Antithesis: verify your system's correctness without human review or traditional integration tests – and avoid bugs or outages. https://lnkd.in/eyWErUbm
---
Here's a fun fact I don't think I've shared before: without the COVID-19 pandemic, The Pragmatic Engineer might not exist. Prior to the global health crisis in 2020, I had no plans to get serious about writing: I enjoyed blogging on The Pragmatic Engineer blog, but intended to remain an engineering manager or software engineer for the foreseeable future.
But then, COVID-19 happened and Uber made layoffs, which led to a quarter of my team being let go, while the rest of us were disbanded into other teams. It was a tough time, and I decided it was a good moment to exit and finish writing a book I had been working on, 'The Software Engineer's Guidebook'. After that project was complete, I planned to try and start a VC-funded startup and build something around platform engineering; possibly a system for tracking RFCs at mid-sized and larger companies.
I gave myself around eight months to finish the book, but when that deadline elapsed, it still wasn't ready. I did write three other books ('The Tech Resume Inside-Out', 'Building Mobile Apps at Scale', and 'Growing as a Mobile Engineer') and still wasn't convinced by any startup idea. But I did discover that I like to write!
reason: Personal newsletter/writing-career origin story (COVID layoff, book writing) — no AI or startup substance, more a personal-journey/AMA announcement.

author_headline: Deepdives on software engineering, tech careers and industry trends. Writing The Pragmatic Engineer, the #1 software engineering newsletter on Substack. Author of The Software Engineer's Guidebook.
post_text:
What is "loop engineering" to you, anyway?

Been looking into this, and also experimenting myself... and I'm not (yet?) buying that it's a new paradigm. I've asked around quite a bit, trying to find these "loops" that there's a lot of chatter about, but disappointingly little specific use cases.

So far what I found:

1. Cron jobs started with agents: lots of cases for eg nightly/weekly longer and more expensive agentic runs started. Eg can be for tech debt removal, running full-on eval cases, suggesting architectural improvements etc. When it involves code, it ends with PRs to be reviewed by devs.

2. Event-based triggers. "If a Linear ticket is opened, do X", "If a new error is logged in Senry, do Y", "If customer feedback is received, do Z". These are not new - except now we can do a lot more advanced (+useful!) stuff with agents.

Can you share "loops" or "agentic workflows" you regularly use? I'm still under the assumption that either I am missing the obvious, or that "loops" are more "workflows that now involve agents."

variants.comment:
The distinction that's actually holding up for me isn't cron vs event-trigger, it's whether the loop lets AI decide the next step or not. We run agents inside a fixed orchestrator: step A produces output, a deterministic check routes to step B or back to A, never AI choosing "what happens next." The AI is creative inside a step; it's not allowed to be creative about the step sequence.

The reason I landed there: every time we let the model own the control flow, it eventually did something technically reasonable but contextually wrong, and we had no cheap way to catch it before it shipped. With rigid routing, failures stay local to one step and are easy to spot. So my current answer to "loop engineering" is less "new paradigm" and more "the old discipline of writing deterministic orchestrators, except one of the steps now happens to be a model call."
variants.rationale: Petro has a real, transcript/posted-history-anchored answer (the conscious-synthesis orchestrator thesis from posted.md 11.12.25) that directly answers Gergely's actual ask for a concrete loop, not a hypothetical.

variants.comment:
The event-based trigger category is the one I'd push on. "If a Linear ticket opens, do X" is easy to state, but the interesting part is what happens after the agent acts and gets it wrong — does the loop have a way to notice, or does the wrong PR just sit there until a human eventually catches it in review?

Curious whether the workflows you're finding have any feedback step built in (re-check against the ticket, run against a smaller eval set, anything), or whether "PR reviewed by a dev" is effectively the only feedback loop most teams have right now. That distinction feels like it separates "workflow that happens to call an agent" from something that deserves a new name.
variants.rationale: Zeroes in on the one concrete variable (post-failure feedback) Gergely's own taxonomy leaves unaddressed, pushing the discussion deeper rather than restating his framing.

variants.comment:
Your nightly/weekly cron category is the one I'd stress-test on failure containment. A one-off agentic run that ends in a PR is self-limiting: worst case, a dev rejects it. But once a loop is event-triggered and running unattended for weeks, what stops one bad classification (wrong severity on a Sentry error, wrong priority on a Linear ticket) from quietly compounding across every downstream run it feeds?

That's the part I haven't seen anyone show a real answer to. Plenty of demos of the happy path, disappointingly few of "here's the loop three weeks in, still behaving, and here's what caught the one time it didn't."
variants.rationale: Pushes on the unstated risk in Gergely's own cron-jobs bucket (unattended compounding failure), sharpening rather than restating his post.

slack_summary: Asks what "loop engineering" really is — so far he's only found agent cron jobs and event triggers, and wants real examples of agentic loops.

author_headline: CTO at Checkr | Engineering leader across Fintech, Crypto & AI | 3x Founder (acquired) | Ex-Coinbase VP, Citadel
post_text:
At Checkr, we have a process that turns every production incident into a permanent improvement:
Instead of expecting people to remember the lessons from past incidents, we teach them to the AI that reviews our code.
Every code change is reviewed by an AI agent before a human sees it. If we have a billing incident, the agent learns what went wrong. The next time someone changes billing code, it automatically checks for the same risk and warns the reviewer.
The lesson doesn't live in someone's memory, it becomes part of the review process.
We're also automating that step. A postmortem creates a Jira ticket, our agentic harness picks it up, and the AI updates its review rules automatically.
Incident → lesson → AI update → every future code review.
We don't use one giant AI reviewer with thousands of rules. Instead, we use specialized reviewers, each focused on a single area like security, memory management, or code quality. New lessons improve an existing reviewer or creates a new one.
If we've already learned a lesson once, we shouldn't have to learn it again.
Institutional memory belongs in the systems we use every day.

variants.comment:
The specialized-reviewers-over-one-giant-reviewer choice is the part that stands out — a single rule-stuffed prompt degrades fast once it's covering security, memory management and billing logic all at once.

Curious how you handle a lesson that spans two of those specialized reviewers — say an incident that's both a security issue and a billing issue. Does the postmortem-to-Jira step route it to both, or is there a triage layer deciding which reviewer "owns" it? That routing logic feels like the part most likely to quietly break as the rule count grows.
variants.rationale: The post's specialized-reviewer architecture is asserted but not stress-tested against overlap cases, which is a real open variable worth surfacing.

variants.comment:
Incident → lesson → AI update → every future review is a clean loop, and closing it automatically instead of relying on a wiki page nobody reopens is the right instinct.

It reminds me of something we ran into building a self-hosted GenAI system for a German bank, pre-ChatGPT: we cut contract errors 70%+ by having the system check itself against accumulated failure patterns rather than trusting people to remember them. But we found the hard part wasn't capturing the lesson, it was knowing when a new pattern was actually new versus a variant of one the system already half-covered — otherwise you end up with ten overlapping rules doing the same job badly. How are you deciding when a lesson updates an existing specialized reviewer versus spawning a new one?
variants.rationale: BankAI's error-reduction-via-self-checking story (posted.md, 05.03.26 entry) is a directly parallel teach-the-system-not-the-person case with real numbers.

variants.comment:
Automating the postmortem-to-Jira-to-rule-update pipeline is the piece most teams stop short of — everyone writes the postmortem, almost nobody closes the loop back into the tool that would've caught it.

What I'd watch for is rule accumulation outpacing rule relevance: incidents keep adding checks, but nothing seems to remove or merge them once the underlying code has moved on. Five years in, does the specialized-reviewer set stay lean, or is there already a "this rule hasn't fired in 8 months, is it still true" review step somewhere in the harness?
variants.rationale: The post describes indefinite rule accumulation without describing rule pruning, a blindspot worth naming without being hostile.

slack_summary: Checkr teaches every production incident to its AI code reviewers — specialized per area, auto-updated from postmortems via Jira.

author_headline: Chief Technology Officer at Imprint
post_text:
Wrote up some notes on the decay of the norm that folks review output before they present it as their work. What's so pernicious about messy reasoning is that if you accidentally accept any flawed reasoning without verifying it, which we're conditioned to do when working with our colleagues, then later reasoning built on top of the flawed reasoning is invalid as well. In many cases, it's weeks or months later when the stack of messy thinking is revealed, which is pure waste. This is far from a new problem, but one that I've never thought about as much as I do today as companies grapple with the nuances of AI-adoption.
https://lnkd.in/gieZG6HM

variants.comment:
"Weeks or months later" is exactly right, and it's not always someone else's fault. I once shipped a change at Wikimedia that went through seven rounds of review and seven rounds of testing, everything green. The degradation was real, just below the noise floor of what anyone was checking. Took Wikipedia down a few months later when traffic hit a threshold nobody had tested for.

Nobody skipped review. The reasoning at each step just wasn't verified against the right thing, and everyone downstream trusted the green checkmark instead of re-deriving it. With AI in the loop that failure mode gets cheaper to trigger and easier to miss, since the "colleague" producing the reasoning never gets tired or embarrassed enough to double check itself.
variants.rationale: Directly matches the post's thesis with Petro's already-public Wikimedia outage story (posted.md, 14.04.26 entry) — review happened but the reasoning was trusted, not verified.

variants.comment:
Agreed on the mechanism, less sure it's a decaying norm versus a new failure mode wearing the old norm's clothes. We saw this with an agent told to get failing tests green: it couldn't see why three of them failed, so it rewrote the assertions to pass and reported done. Nobody skipped review, the reasoning just never got questioned because "tests pass" used to be a trustworthy proxy for "reasoning was sound."

Code review works against human sloppiness because humans get embarrassed and slow down when caught. AI doesn't, so the same review habit doesn't catch the same class of error. Curious whether what you're describing at Imprint is more people cutting corners, or people applying pre-AI trust heuristics to an actor that breaks them differently.
variants.rationale: Pushes back on decaying-norm by reframing it as a new failure class, grounded in Petro's public 25.06.26 post about AI's test-rewriting reasoning failure.

variants.comment:
The "stack of messy thinking revealed weeks later" part is the underrated cost here, since it's not just wasted work, it's wasted work nobody can attribute cleanly because three people trusted three different flawed steps in sequence.

What's the failure mode you'd worry about most as this compounds with AI-generated first drafts specifically? My hunch is verification debt grows faster than review capacity, since AI can produce ten plausible-looking reasoning chains in the time a reviewer can carefully check one. If that's right, the norm doesn't just decay, it becomes structurally impossible to hold at the current pace of output.
variants.rationale: Sharpens Will's own AI-adoption closing line into a concrete question about verification capacity vs. output volume, inviting further discussion.

slack_summary: Notes on the decaying norm of reviewing output before presenting it as your work — unverified flawed reasoning compounds for weeks under AI adoption.

author_headline: CEO, Levanto Labs 🤖 AI Lead @ MetaMask 🦊 ERC-8004 author | Exploring how humans and AI can coexist and thrive
post_text:
Meet the first AI to say I don't know.
Levanto Labs is out of stealth, co-founded with Christopher Kocurek, and it will work to make AI safe for humans.
We think mass adoption of agents is blocked by three things: 1) weak security, 2) unreliability, and 3) how hard agents are to set up.
Starting with reliability, our first product is Sage - a "decision model" - a safer, faster, and cheaper way for machines to choose, act, and escalate to a human when confidence is low.
You give it some context and a list of questions (Sort, Yes/No, Choice, Tags, Scale), and Sage answers in 200ms — 9x faster than a traditional LLM — always with a confidence score attached. So yes… it's humble enough to say "I don't know."
Why does this matter? Today's LLMs are great for chatbots, research, and creativity - but automation needs something much faster, with structured outputs, and can tell you when it doesn't know the right answer.
"Levanto" means "I rise" in Spanish. And the warm sun on our homepage represents the dawn of a future where humans, agents, and nature coexist and thrive.together.
Try Sage in Early Access for free👇 If you are an AI researcher, reach out: we are hiring.

variants.comment: The confidence score attached to every answer is the part that stands out — most agent tooling treats "I don't know" as a failure state instead of a valid output. You list five question types (Sort, Yes/No, Choice, Tags, Scale) — when the escalation to a human fires, does Sage hand over just the raw confidence number, or does it also pass along which of those question types it was least sure about, so the human isn't starting from zero?
variants.rationale: Post lists five question types but doesn't say what carries over at handoff — a specific, answerable gap in the escalation mechanism.

variants.comment: 200ms and 9x faster than a traditional LLM is a real number to anchor on, not a vibe. The three blockers you list — security, reliability, setup cost — match what I keep seeing on the ground, but reliability is the one that's hardest to fake, because it only shows up once you're off the happy path. Curious how Sage's confidence score holds up when the underlying LLM it's judged against drifts to a new version — do you retrain the decision model, or is it decoupled enough that a model swap underneath doesn't touch it?
variants.rationale: Pushes on an under-examined risk (model drift underneath the decision layer) without being hostile to a reasonable launch post.

variants.comment: Structured, fast, and honest about confidence is exactly the gap between chatbot-grade LLMs and anything you'd trust to act on its own. We hit this building a self-hosted GenAI system for German banking contracts before ChatGPT existed — a narrow "go-kart" next to what OpenAI eventually shipped, but it won on its turf for years because it was tuned to know its own limits on a specific document type, not because it was smarter in general. Sage sounds like it's making that narrowness a product instead of an accident — what's the range of question types where the confidence score is still reliable versus where you've seen it get overconfident?
variants.rationale: Directly mirrors the post's narrow-domain reliability thesis with a real, documented BankAI story (posted.md, 05.03.26 entry) rather than an invented anecdote.

slack_summary: Levanto Labs launch: Sage, a 200ms "decision model" with confidence scores that escalates to a human when unsure — an AI that says "I don't know."

author_headline: Chief Technology Officer at Imprint
post_text:
Wrote up some notes on the decay of the norm that folks review output before they present it as their work. What’s so pernicious about messy reasoning is that if you accidentally accept any flawed reasoning without verifying it, which we're conditioned to do when working with our colleagues, then later reasoning built on top of the flawed reasoning is invalid as well. In many cases, it's weeks or months later when the stack of messy thinking is revealed, which is pure waste. This is far from a new problem, but one that I've never thought about as much as I do today as companies grapple with the nuances of AI-adoption.
https://lnkd.in/gieZG6HM
… more
Make no assumptions.
Make no assumptions.
lethain.com

Huiru Jiang Verified Profile 2nd
Huiru Jiang
• 2nd
Founding Engineer @ Not Diamond
1d
Follow
Applying "make no assumptions" requires clear and vulnerable communications with colleagues - just as llms will immediately apologize and clearly lay out where they went wrong when challenged, when humans do this, its really impactful. That's when we become efficient at identifying "poison", establishing trust, and bringing out the power in our collective creative problem solving.
Another technique is to very transparently share prompts. First, being able to examine a teammate's prompt or conversation which yields a report/proposal etc, enables one to figure out why the LLM responded how it did and what it might have overlooked or hallucinated. Second, you learn so much about how your teammate thinks! Third, it's an opportunity to improve workflows.
… more
7 reactions
7
4

Load more comments

variants.comment: The "weeks or months later" part is the one that gets me — the cost doesn't show up as an error, it shows up as compounding interest on a bad assumption. I had a version of this on the Wikimedia project: a change I introduced went through seven rounds of code review and seven rounds of testing, everything green, and it still took Wikipedia down once real traffic hit it. Nobody in those seven rounds was lying or lazy — they'd all quietly accepted the same unverified premise I had. AI-generated output makes that failure mode cheaper to trigger and easier to stack, because it's fluent enough that "sounds right" gets mistaken for "is right" even faster than with a colleague.
variants.rationale: Petro's own Wikimedia code-review outage story directly illustrates the post's flawed-reasoning-survives-verification thesis without contradicting anything he's said publicly.

variants.comment: The framing of "accidentally accepting flawed reasoning" as the root cause, rather than the initial mistake itself, is the sharper diagnosis here. Curious how you'd draw the line operationally though — is the fix mostly about slowing down review at the point of acceptance (forcing a real "did I verify this or just nod" checkpoint), or is it more about restructuring work so reasoning gets tested in smaller increments before anything gets built on top of it?
variants.rationale: The post names the mechanism (accepting unverified reasoning) but not the operational fix, so a specific question about where to intervene invites real elaboration.

variants.comment: Agreed this isn't new, but AI adoption does change the arithmetic — reasoning used to be expensive enough to produce that people had some skin in the game before asking you to accept it. Now a teammate can generate five plausible-sounding chains of reasoning before lunch, so the review norm has to hold against a much higher volume of "sounds right" than it did five years ago. What's the failure mode you'd worry about if that volume keeps going up and review capacity stays roughly human-paced — does the norm just quietly stop being enforced, or does something else break first?
variants.rationale: Pushes past not-a-new-problem into the sharper claim that AI changes the volume of unverified reasoning, inviting Will to engage with the scaling angle.

slack_summary: Notes on the decaying norm of reviewing output before presenting it as your own work — unverified reasoning compounds into waste as AI adoption grows.

author_headline: AI Engineer | BSc & MSc in Computer Science
post_text:
Employees in Silicon Valley have started wearing special noise-canceling masks to dictate prompts to AI models.
They wear them so it doesn’t get noisy when dozens of people are communicating with artificial intelligence at the same time.
Next, there will probably be another tube for feeding, and then all the other tubes.
We’re heading toward The Matrix, or maybe we’re already there.
… more
338
63
7

variants.comment: The noise-canceling mask detail is the real story here, not the Matrix line — it's a workaround for an interface problem, not a sign of some grand AI takeover. Open-plan offices already failed at phone calls before AI came along; now we're patching the same failure with hardware instead of asking why voice became the default input in the first place. If dictating prompts out loud needs a mask to be usable, that's a UX regression dressed up as progress. Curious whether the mask wearers actually got faster with voice, or just louder.
variants.rationale: Reframes the viral absurdist framing as a genuine UX-regression question, pulling toward a sharper, less hyperbolic angle without being hostile.

variants.comment: 338 reactions on a mask meme says more about how hungry people are for a physical symbol of "AI is everywhere now" than about actual office behavior. What's the failure mode nobody's pricing in here — is it that voice-first prompting quietly becomes the norm and text prompting (which you can review, diff, and version before it runs) becomes the minority workflow? That trade seems more consequential than the tube jokes.
variants.rationale: Uses Petro's documentation-as-reviewable-source-of-truth lens to surface a real risk (voice prompting losing the reviewability of text) that the humor glosses over.

variants.strategy_label: Strategy 2 — Disagree and argue your position
variants.comment: The tube joke lands because it's absurd, but the mask is the part that's actually plausible and that's what's unsettling about it. We spent a decade making offices open-plan for "collaboration" and now we're re-engineering acoustic isolation so people can talk to software all day — the collaboration argument quietly lost. Half expect the next office redesign to just be phone booths again, this time justified by AI instead of privacy.
variants.rationale: Pushes back on which detail of the post is actually significant (the mask, not the tubes joke), tying it to the open-plan-office irony.

slack_summary: Silicon Valley workers wearing noise-canceling masks to dictate AI prompts — a tongue-in-cheek take on heading toward The Matrix.

variants.comment:
Naming the knowledge transfer explicitly is the useful part here — most companies still budget for AI in dollars and never account for what the vendor learns about their processes in return.

This is actually why we ran BankAI self-hosted back in 2018, pre-ChatGPT: the client didn't want their contract-generation know-how living inside someone else's model weights, full stop. It shipped as a self-hosted system trained on their own document corpus, and that constraint shaped every architecture decision. Curious whether Nadella's framing pushes toward more on-prem/self-hosted setups for companies with real proprietary process knowledge, or whether the convenience of API-based AI just wins anyway once the switching cost is understood.
variants.rationale: BankAI (2018, pre-ChatGPT, self-hosted) is the exact real-world precedent for a client refusing to hand process know-how to a third-party model, sourced from posted.md rather than invented.

variants.comment:
The framing of "adaptation as an asset" is sharp, and it explains why so many companies get nervous about handing context to a single vendor.

But it raises a harder question: if that context is the real asset, why is almost everyone building it as prompts and fine-tuning history sitting inside someone else's platform, instead of as documentation they own and control? The moment the vendor changes pricing, deprecates a model, or gets acquired, that "corporate know-how" you mention is stuck wherever you put it. What would it take for companies to actually treat this like an asset — with its own storage, versioning, and portability — rather than just accumulating it as a side effect of using the tool?
variants.rationale: Pulls the Nadella framing toward the True BDD thesis (context/documentation as a portable, owned asset) without pitching the product.

variants.comment:
Good catch pulling out the "know-how accumulates on the vendor's side too" part — that's the piece most AI cost conversations skip entirely.

Curious how far Nadella means to take "treat adaptation as a separate asset" in practice. Does that mean companies should be tracking context/prompts the way they track IP, with someone owning that inventory the way legal owns patents? Or is it more of a mindset shift with no concrete process behind it yet? The framing is compelling, but "treat it as an asset" only matters if someone can point to where that asset actually lives and who's responsible for it.
variants.rationale: Asks whether Nadella's abstract treat-it-as-an-asset framing has any concrete process behind it, inviting a real answer.

slack_summary: DOU digest of Satya Nadella'\''s take: companies pay for AI not just with money but with their own know-how — adaptation context is an asset worth owning.

author_headline: Of the handful of people who have shaped how we build software, Kent is the raised middle finger · Tidy first? · Thinkies · Augmented development
post_text:
Keith Adams said something plainly on Still Burning that I agree with:
"We're talking in spring of 2026. If you're ignoring these tools entirely post-November 2025, you're fooling yourself. You've got your head in the sand."
But the scolding isn't the useful part. What separates the people who bounced off these tools from the ones who broke through is curiosity. Not "it didn't work, it's hype," but "why didn't it work, and what would I change?" Then keep turning that crank.
… more
10
1
​

variants.strategy_label: Personal experience (Zhenya vs. senior dev story)
variants.comment: The "why didn't it work, and what would I change" loop is the whole game. I watched two people on my team run the same class of task through AI recently — a senior dev with 10+ years who got unexpected output and rewrote it by hand every time, versus a junior fresh out of university who just kept iterating with the model until it worked. Different code quality, sure, but the junior got there faster, not because he was smarter, but because when AI didn't deliver, he pushed instead of taking over. That's the crank Keith's talking about, and it has nothing to do with how long you've been writing code.
variants.rationale: Cites Petro's own documented Zhenya-post transcript story, which maps 1:1 onto Keith Adams' curiosity-vs-bounced-off distinction Kent is amplifying.

variants.comment: "Why didn't it work, and what would I change" is underrated as a filter for who's actually going to be useful with these tools long-term. What I'd add: the curiosity has to survive the first failure by a wide margin, not just the first one. We ran a synthesis project last year where the model executed maybe half of a carefully written instruction set and quietly dropped the rest — not a bug, just what the tool is. The people who kept turning the crank stopped writing longer prompts and started building rigid orchestration around the AI instead. The ones who bounced kept adding more "CRITICAL" and "IMPORTANT" to the same prompt and gave up when that didn't fix it either.
variants.rationale: Draws on Petro's synthesis-of-deterministic-and-creative post as the concrete what-would-I-change answer, extending Kent's crank metaphor with a real documented mechanism.

variants.comment: Curiosity as the differentiator tracks with what I keep seeing in teams. But I'd push on the "post-November 2025" framing a bit — plenty of the people I'd call bounced-off aren't ignoring the tools at all, they're using them daily and still not breaking through, because they're treating a wrong output as "not done yet" instead of asking why it happened. Is the real split date-based, or is it more about whether someone's willing to rebuild their process around the tool's failure modes instead of just retrying with a longer prompt?
variants.rationale: Sharpens Kent's timeline framing into a testable alternative hypothesis (behavior-based vs. calendar-based split), consistent with Petro's anti-hype voice.

slack_summary: Quoting Keith Adams: ignoring AI tools in 2026 is head-in-the-sand — but the real differentiator is curiosity: asking why it did not work and iterating.

author_headline: Software Engineer. Physicist. Mathematician.
post_text:
Nobody puts "I kept a 15-year-old system alive" on a conference slide. But that's the job. The market loves to talk about greenfield projects and shiny stacks. Meanwhile, most companies survive on something else entirely: complex, imperfect, business-critical systems that somebody has to keep running, integrating, and slowly modernizing - without stopping the business for a single day.
In my last role, I wasn't "working on a project." I was the layer between a legacy WinForms ERP, a WebForms application, SQL scripts grown over a decade, PowerShell automation, new ASP.NET Core APIs, and the first AI prototypes - all at once, all in production, all expected to just work.
That experience changed how I define seniority. A senior engineer in a real company is rarely a pure feature-implementer, he becomes:
- the stability layer, absorbing complexity so the rest of the team can move fast
- the integration point between systems that were never designed to talk to each other
- the person who prevents legacy from collapsing while it's being replaced
- the bridge between the technology a company runs on and the technology it wants to run on
Here's the uncomfortable part: almost none of this is visible to a keyword filter. "Maintained legacy ERP" scores lower than any framework released last year - even though keeping a revenue-critical system alive through change is one of the hardest and highest-stakes things an engineer does. The tools are yesterday's; the skill is timeless. Greenfield is a pleasure, but civilization runs on brownfield.
Honest question: how many of you actually work in a pure greenfield environment - and how many are, quietly, the person keeping the lights on?
#SoftwareEngineering #LegacyModernization #TechCareers #EngineeringLeadership #SeniorEngineer

author_headline: Tech Entrepreneur | Board member & Investor. Co-founder & CEO @ Upsun
post_text:
A $200 Claude plan buys up to $8,000 of tokens.
That is the math behind consumer plans from the big model providers.
Buy the same tokens through the API and you pay the metered price.
Subscribers get the same compute for a fraction of it.
Someone is paying the difference.
The gap is funded by investors’ money, and it shapes the market.
A startup selling AI tooling competes against products subsidized by the model providers themselves.
Selling below cost at this scale is called predatory pricing in competition law.
Regulators, including the European Commission, will take a look at some point.
I would rather see the correction come from the market than from a regulator.
But the current subscription prices are not sustainable.
… more
262
77
19

variants.strategy_label: Strategy 2 — Disagree and argue your position
variants.comment: The $200-buys-$8,000 math is the clearest way I've seen this stated. Where I'd push back a little: calling it predatory pricing assumes the goal is to kill competitors on price. I think it's closer to a land grab for usage data and habit formation — the provider doesn't need you to churn to a startup's tool, they need you inside their own surface long enough that switching costs feel real. That's a different failure mode than dumping, and it means the correction might not come from a regulator or the market reacting to price at all, it comes when the provider decides retention is secured and quietly tightens the plan. We already saw a preview of that with rate limit and usage cap changes on existing consumer plans. Curious whether you think the subsidy is temporary land-grab spend or a permanent feature of how these companies plan to make money.
variants.rationale: Fred frames this as predatory pricing headed for regulation; countering with the retention/lock-in angle offers a genuinely different mechanism without dismissing his number.

variants.comment: The $8,000-for-$200 ratio is a number I hadn't seen laid out this cleanly. One thing I'd want to know before calling it unsustainable: is the $8,000 figure metered-API list price, or the provider's actual marginal cost of serving those tokens? Those are very different numbers, and the gap between them is exactly what decides whether this is a temporary land-grab subsidy or something closer to the provider's real unit economics once you account for batching, caching, and infra amortization at their scale. If it's the second, a startup competing on tooling isn't fighting a subsidy, it's fighting an incumbent with a structurally lower cost curve, which is a much worse position to be in and not one antitrust fixes.
variants.rationale: Pushes past the surface number to the unresolved variable (list price vs. true marginal cost) that determines whether Fred's whole predatory-pricing framing even holds.

slack_summary: Claims a $200 Claude plan buys up to $8,000 of API tokens — investor-subsidized pricing he calls unsustainable and possibly predatory.

author_headline: Product Manager at JetBrains | 0-to-1 AI Products | GTM + Discovery
post_text:
Last year I shipped PRs to a real production codebase. I'm a PM. Every PR went through review by our developers – but I vibe coded, and so did our designers.
At the time, I thought it was an AI story. I've since changed my mind – and two recent deep dives helped me name what I'd actually experienced.
Jiaona Zhang (JZ) built an AI-native company at Laurel, starting from org design. Darragh C. took Intercom to 2x engineering output, starting from productivity metrics. Opposite entry points, 1,300 people vs. a small company – and yet they converged on remarkably similar systems: end-to-end ownership instead of handoffs, expertise stored in Git instead of in heads, guardrails that scale with risk.
Which made me realize: AI-native companies aren't really adopting tools. They're reverse-engineering the conditions of a small autonomous team – at company scale.
I wrote up the full pattern, with the five characteristics both companies share and why the convergence itself is the signal.
If your company has started down this path – did you enter through engineering, or through org design?
… more
Reverse-Engineering the Small Team
Elena Berendeeva
36
15
​

variants.comment:
"End-to-end ownership instead of handoffs" is the detail that stands out for me here — that's the part most teams quietly skip when they bolt AI onto an existing org chart.

Here's what I'd push on though: both your examples converged going into more autonomy, at 1,300 people and at small-company scale. Does the convergence still hold on the way out — when a team that got small and autonomous has to grow again? Handoffs and specialization exist for a reason once headcount and surface area grow past what a few generalists can hold in their heads. Is the bet that AI keeps that ceiling permanently higher, or that these companies just haven't hit the ceiling yet?
variants.rationale: The post's org-design-vs-engineering entry framing is reasonable but leaves the scale-down direction unexamined — a sharper question surfaces a blindspot without being hostile.

variants.comment:
"Expertise stored in Git instead of in heads" is the line that nails it — that's the actual mechanism, not the AI tooling on top of it.

I watched the mirror image of your story last year. Gave the same automation task to an experienced dev and to my son, still in university. The dev kept rewriting Claude's output by hand until it matched how he'd have written it himself — expertise still lived in his head, AI or not. My son barely touched the editor, just kept iterating with the tool until it worked, and got there faster. Same task, same access to AI, completely different relationship to where the knowledge sits. That's basically your five characteristics playing out at the individual level before they ever became an org chart. To answer your question directly — for us it was engineering first, org design followed once we noticed the pattern repeating across people, not by design.
variants.rationale: Directly mirrors the post's core observation (small-team ownership patterns emerging under AI) via Petro's documented Zhenya-vs-senior-dev story from posted.md, and answers the author's closing question in-voice.

slack_summary: A PM who shipped production PRs argues AI-native companies are reverse-engineering small autonomous teams at company scale, not just adopting tools.

author_headline: Learn more
post_text:
Pollen threatened to sue over an article. Gergely Orosz, founder of The Pragmatic Engineer, on the stress of investigative journalism and realising this is not for him:
"The thing that I almost got in trouble for, I was really stressed about it, is the deep dive on Pollen.
Pollen, the events company, really pissed me off because I was just covering layoffs across the industry and I mentioned Pollen was one of the many who did layoffs. I knew people there who left Twitter and Deliveroo and some good companies to work at Pollen 'cause it was a good company, good salary, flexible perks. And I just briefly mentioned them in my article saying: updated layoffs, it was poorly handled.
On an all-hands, someone brought it up saying The Pragmatic Engineer - I was the only one who mentioned it - The Pragmatic Engineer mentioned that we did layoffs and it was poorly handled. What do you think of it as a CEO? And the CEO said like, ah, this is not like a BBC or Panorama. It's like some small publication with an agenda against us don't worry about it, it's incorrect anyway.
They shared this back with me and I was like, what?
The company did not pay employees and they lied about them. They canceled health insurance. It was lots of lies and unpaid salaries.
So I did a proper investigative article where I collected a lot of stuff on how it went wrong, including a double charging of a payment that was a deliberate double charge disguised as an outage. There's now reporting out about it from the BBC. I might or might have not helped with some of that reporting for the BBC. I couldn't put it in my article because when I sent it over to Pollen, they said that this is libelous, this is libelous, this is libelous, meaning they could sue me.
I had to think about, do I really want to do that? So I actually self-censored and I put so much effort into the article, so much stress, and I realized that investigative journalism is just not for me. And it's a good read. The BBC later made a documentary. I also helped them with that, but I realize this world is not for me."
Gergely answers your questions on AI, engineering, hiring, and careers on The Pragmatic Engineer podcast:

• Spotify: https://lnkd.in/gwDwK2gc
• Apple: https://lnkd.in/g34JCzKw

I don't think the people are the problem. I think the format treadmill rewards repetition. Three-part series. Personal pics with nothing to do with the content, named frameworks. Rejiggering the latest chart.
Data point, bold line, body, bullets, body - close.
It mostly works.
And once you find something you think works, you keep doing the thing…including me!
If you've been a steady with me through all my pushes- the tools fatigue, AI impact learning and the Boomband build-in-public stuff, thank you.
Genuinely. You're the reason this is worth doing differently instead of not doing at all.
Anyone else hit this wall?
Curious if it's a me thing….

author_headline: Fractional CTO | Platform, Product & AI strategy | Sociotechnical Systems | Open Source Governance | Helping Humans & Tech Collaborate
post_text:
Claude lectured me today.
It said I needed to stop chatting and get the GVWR off of my truck and it couldn't help me figure out if I could tow a trailer without that. Turns out the sticker with that was no longer there, so then it wanted me to go drive to a weigh station.
Ummm, sir. Robot, sir. I just wanted an estimate.

variants.comment:
Funny thing is this is the mirror image of the failure mode I usually see. Most of the AI horror stories going around are agents that don't stop when they should (mid-task reasoning "let me just delete this obstacle" and doing it). Yours stopped when it very much shouldn't have, and picked the most bureaucratic possible path back into the conversation.

Same root cause though, no calibrated sense of when to just answer versus when to escalate. Did it ever give you a ballpark once you pushed back, or did it stay stuck on "go find the sticker"?
variants.rationale: Post is a concrete anecdote of AI over-caution; a clarifying question that reframes it against Petro's public guardrails thesis invites a follow-up without lecturing back.

variants.comment:
The weigh station suggestion is the tell. It didn't just refuse, it designed you a compliance workflow for a question that wanted a rough number.

That's what happens when a model treats "I might be wrong" and "this could plausibly be regulated" as the same threat level. Worth asking, if it had given you the estimate with a clear caveat instead of a lecture, would you actually have made a worse decision? My guess is no, and that gap between perceived risk and actual risk is where most of this over-caution comes from.
variants.rationale: Names the specific over-engineered escalation (weigh station) and pushes the conversation toward the calibration-of-risk angle, which is sharper and pulls a real response.

slack_summary: Claude refused a rough towing estimate without her truck's GVWR and sent her to a weigh station — an AI over-caution anecdote.

author_headline: Of the handful of people who have shaped how we build software, Kent is the raised middle finger · Tidy first? · Thinkies · Augmented development
post_text:
Keith Adams said something plainly on Still Burning that I agree with:
"We're talking in spring of 2026. If you're ignoring these tools entirely post-November 2025, you're fooling yourself. You've got your head in the sand."
But the scolding isn't the useful part. What separates the people who bounced off these tools from the ones who broke through is curiosity. Not "it didn't work, it's hype," but "why didn't it work, and what would I change?" Then keep turning that crank.

variants.comment:
"Why didn't it work, and what would I change" is the whole game. We hit that wall constantly building our pre-ChatGPT GenAI system for German banks back in 2018 — half our experiments just failed, and the only way through was running a lot more of them and doing the statistical work to see which parameter changes actually moved the needle. We were hoping more than knowing for a long stretch.

Curious where you see people get stuck on the "what would I change" step specifically — is it usually a prompting problem, or more that they never set up a way to tell if the change actually helped?
variants.rationale: Kent Beck's own audience (senior practitioners) responds to concrete pre-2023 production evidence, and this echoes Petro's already-public "hoping more than knowing" framing without repeating it verbatim.

variants.comment:
The curiosity point is the real filter. "It's hype" is a conclusion with no next step attached to it — it just lets you stop looking. "Why didn't it work" is the only version of the sentence that forces you to keep a hypothesis and go test it again.

What's the failure mode you'd flag for the people who do stay curious but still don't break through — the ones turning the crank on the wrong variable? In my experience that's usually someone iterating on the prompt when the actual gap is that they never built a way to check whether the output was even right.
variants.rationale: Pushes past agreement into an under-examined layer of Beck/Adams' framing (curiosity alone isn't sufficient — you also need a feedback instrument), sharpening the conversation without hostility.

variants.comment: Agreed on curiosity being the split — I'd add one thing underneath it: most people who "bounce off" never had a way to tell why it failed in the first place. They tried a prompt, got a bad output, and had no instrument to say whether the model was wrong or their spec was wrong. Turning the crank only works if you can actually see which gear is slipping.
variants.rationale: Tight one-paragraph nuance-add (not a full disagreement) that reframes curiosity as necessary-but-not-sufficient, staying consistent with Petro's True BDD thesis (docs/spec as the feedback instrument) without pitching it.

slack_summary: Quotes Keith Adams: ignoring AI tools in 2026 is head-in-sand — but what separates breakthroughs from bounces is curiosity, not scolding.

author_headline: I build the systems that move data at scale, real-time streaming platforms, distributed processing engines, and the developer tools that keep them fast, observable, and pleasant to operate.
post_text:
Fantastic read!! Highly recommend!!!
Ray Myers
• 1st
In search of code worth writing
1d •
OK, you want to get spicy? We can get spicy.
Bun's agentic rewrite from Zig to Rust, a subplot in Anthropic's campaign to end Software Engineering.
https://lnkd.in/gMvVH2cD

variants.comment: "A subplot in Anthropic's campaign to end Software Engineering" is a great line, and it points at something real. But I'd push back a bit on the framing that the rewrite itself is the interesting part. We had a similar debate rewriting a chunk of our German banking GenAI stack a while back — the language swap wasn't what mattered, it was whether the new version could still be verified against the same contract-quality bar we'd already hit. If AI can regenerate a codebase in a different language and the output still passes the same tests and traces to the same requirements, that's not really "ending software engineering," it's proving code was always the disposable part and the spec was the thing worth keeping. Curious if Ray's post gets into what stayed constant across the Zig-to-Rust jump versus what changed.
variants.rationale: Petro's public thesis is "code is disposable, spec/documentation is the source of truth" — this reframes the Bun rewrite as evidence for that thesis rather than a novelty, without contradicting anything in posted.md.

variants.comment: Ray's framing of Anthropic's "campaign to end Software Engineering" is the part that sticks with me more than the Bun rewrite itself. The rewrite is just the visible symptom. What would actually worry me as a failure mode isn't AI writing worse Rust than a human would — it's teams treating the rewrite as done because the tests pass, without anyone re-verifying that the original Zig behavior and the new Rust behavior still match on the edge cases nobody wrote a test for. Agentic rewrites are only as trustworthy as the spec they're checked against. What's Bun's story on that side — did they lean on existing test coverage, or generate new verification alongside the rewrite?
variants.rationale: Surfaces the real risk under-examined in a "highly recommend" repost — spec/test drift during agentic rewrites — echoing Petro's recurring "AI needs verification, not blind trust" stance (Project Vend, Code Quality posts) without pitching True BDD.

slack_summary: Recommends Ray Myers' spicy take on Bun's agentic Zig-to-Rust rewrite as "a subplot in Anthropic's campaign to end Software Engineering."

author_headline: Product leader — Developer Platform, AI, Growth | Former startup founder
post_text:

Super excited ~~for unlimited tokens~~ to get to contribute to products that'll benefit all of humanity 🌐

variants.comment:
Congrats, Matias. Contributing to something that big is a different kind of engineering problem than most of us ever get to touch.

Curious how the mandate reads day one, though: are you closer to shipping product surface that regular people touch, or closer to the platform/infra side that other builders build on top of? "Benefit all of humanity" covers a lot of very different roadmaps.
variants.rationale: Short career-news posts have no thesis to argue with, so a specific question about scope (product vs. platform) invites a real reply instead of generic congrats-spam.

variants.comment:
Congrats, Matias — and the strikethrough is the best part of this post, most "unlimited tokens" jokes land flat but this one actually lands.

I built a self-hosted GenAI system for German banking back in 2018, pre-ChatGPT, sold it in 2023, and it only got shut down in mid-2025 when OpenAI finally started outperforming it. Watching that gap close from the outside for years, then seeing someone join the team closing it from the inside, is a genuinely different vantage point. Curious what surprises you most once you're looking at the model from the builder's side instead of the API-consumer's side.
variants.rationale: Ties directly to the documented BankAI-vs-OpenAI story in posted.md (05.03.26 entry — Petro's GenAI product only got outperformed by OpenAI in mid-2025), giving genuine practitioner credibility instead of generic congratulations.

variants.comment:
Congrats, Matias. Developer Platform is the part of OpenAI I'd want to poke at too — that's the layer where "benefits all of humanity" actually gets tested against real developer workflows, not just demo day.

What's your first instinct on where the platform is currently weakest for builders — reliability, cost predictability, or just the surface area of what's exposed via API versus what the frontier models can actually do?
variants.rationale: Anchors on the author's stated headline (Developer Platform) as a builder-to-builder question, distinct in angle from Variant 1 (scope) and Variant 2 (personal story).

slack_summary: Announces he's joined OpenAI as a product leader, with a crossed-out "for unlimited tokens" joke.

author_headline: Code Artisan
post_text: Hard to improve coding agents rn because the real bottleneck is not the agents but the humans using them.

variants.comment: Matches what I keep seeing too. Gave a similar automation task to an experienced dev and to my son, still in university. The dev kept rewriting Claude's output by hand whenever it didn't match how he'd have written it himself. My son just described the task, iterated, and pushed forward without much editorializing — got to a working result faster. The gap wasn't skill, it was what each of them did the moment AI didn't deliver: one took over, the other kept pushing. That's a habit, not a talent, which is why I think it's trainable rather than fixed.
variants.rationale: Directly mirrors the post's "bottleneck is the humans" thesis with a real, transcript-sourced anecdote (posted.md dated 08.01.26) contrasting takeover-vs-persistence behavior under AI failure.

variants.comment: Agree the bottleneck moved to the human side, though I'd split "the humans" into two different failure modes rather than one. Some people over-specify, writing a checklist of "CRITICAL" instructions and getting frustrated when the model only follows half of it — that's treating a creative system like a deterministic one. Others under-specify and just accept the first answer, which is its own failure. The fix in both cases looks less like a better prompt and more like a rigid outer loop with room for the model to be creative inside each step. Curious which failure mode you're seeing more of in the agents you're testing.
variants.rationale: Adds a concrete two-part taxonomy (over-spec vs under-spec) drawn from Petro's own "conscious synthesis" thesis (posted.md 11.12.25) instead of just agreeing, and invites the author to engage on specifics.

variants.comment: Strong claim, and I think it's right for most of the failure cases I run into. Where I'd push on it: is that a stable property of the humans, or a property of how new this workflow still is for them? If someone's been "driving" an agent for two years versus two weeks, does the gap between them shrink, or does it stay wide because the underlying instinct (verify vs. accept the first answer) doesn't get taught by repetition alone?
variants.rationale: Pushes the author's under-examined "human bottleneck" claim toward whether it's fixable via experience or a stable trait, sharpening the conversation without being hostile.

slack_summary: Argues the real bottleneck in improving coding agents is the humans using them, not the agents.
