author_headline: Head of Developer Experience at OpenAI. Previously, Product Lead at Stripe. Angel Investor & Advisor.
post_text:
Here’s what’s new in Codex for developers!
We packed in a lot. See Codex take on harder coding tasks with GPT‑5.6 and Ultra, and work in parallel with subagents. You also get faster computer use with Appshots, inline code editing and PR reviews directly in Codex, deployment with Sites, the ability to control Codex from mobile, and much more!
Codex now lives inside the new ChatGPT app, with its own dedicated workspace alongside the new Work agent.
More than 8 million people now use Codex and ChatGPT Work. Thank you for all the feedback and for continuing to push Codex further.
Watch until the end for a cameo from a fun new way to interact with Codex and customize your workflow. It happens to be available today! 👀

variants.comment: The parallel subagents piece is the interesting part for me, more than GPT‑5.6 itself. Once you have several agents editing the same codebase concurrently, the hard part stops being "can it write correct code" and becomes "who resolves the conflicting decisions two agents made independently." Are subagents scoped to non-overlapping files/modules by design, or is there a merge/arbitration step when two of them touch the same area?
variants.rationale: Names the one real unresolved engineering question in the announcement (conflict resolution across concurrent agents) instead of praising the feature list.

variants.comment: Inline PR reviews plus parallel subagents is a strong combo for throughput. The question I keep circling back to as this scales: when multiple agents are generating and reviewing code in parallel, what stays the actual source of truth for "why" a change happened, the commit history, or whatever spec/task description kicked it off? At 8 million users, that's not a philosophical question anymore, it's the thing that decides whether six months from now anyone can safely regenerate or refactor what these agents built without archaeology.
variants.rationale: Pulls the "source of truth under parallel-agent scale" tension into view, adjacent to Petro's documentation-as-source-of-truth thesis, without pitching True BDD.

variants.comment: The breadth here is impressive, mobile control, PR reviews, deployment, subagents all in one workspace. It reminds me of a pattern I only understood in hindsight from BankAI, the GenAI system we built for a German bank pre-ChatGPT: we had a go-kart, OpenAI had a Formula 1 car, but our narrow scope let the go-kart win in its lane for years. Codex is clearly playing the opposite game now, going wide instead of narrow. Curious whether that breadth ends up being the moat, or whether it opens room for narrower tools to win specific workflows the way we did back then.
variants.rationale: Uses the documented BankAI "go-kart vs Formula 1" story (posted.md, 05.03.2026 entry) to frame Codex's breadth-play as a real strategic tradeoff, not flattery.

slack_summary: OpenAI's Codex update: GPT-5.6/Ultra, parallel subagents, inline PR reviews, deployment, mobile control — now inside the ChatGPT app; 8M users.

author_headline: I help companies build better AI products.
post_text:
I want **B.Y.O.A.**  –  Bring Your Own Agent
Plenty of websites now have embedded assistants – GitHub in this example – but the work they can do is limited. It doesn't matter how useful that assistant's tools and skills are because in order to accomplish any real work with that assistant, I need to copy-paste chunks of my own context into window in my browser.
no 1. That's too much work.
no 2. I don't feel like sharing my content w/ a third party.
The answer to this is to bring our own agents into conversation with the 3rd-party app. This way we get to use their tools and skills without stepping outside of our session.
This is not the same thing as downloading their skills. I want to interact with my agent AND interact with the web page. I want my agent to see what I'm doing on GitHub and I want to be able to control what's on the GitHub website through conversations with my agent.
Finally - I want to do this safely. There is a way! I'm building it :D  (see links in the follow-ups).

variants.comment: The "no 2" reason is the one that gets underrated — most people frame this as a UX complaint, but it's really a trust boundary problem. Curious how you're handling the sync direction though: when your agent acts on the GitHub page (clicks, form fills, whatever), does it read the resulting DOM state back to confirm the action landed, or is it flying blind between "I told it to do X" and "the page now shows Y"? That gap is where I'd expect the safety story to get hard.
variants.rationale: Post makes a strong "BYOA" claim but doesn't say how the agent keeps its view of the page in sync with what it just did — a concrete, non-generic gap to probe.

variants.comment: "I want to do this safely" is doing a lot of work in that last line. What's the failure mode you're actually most worried about — the agent misreading page state and acting on stale info, or a malicious/careless third-party page feeding your agent instructions it shouldn't follow? Those need pretty different guardrails, and I'd guess most of the hard engineering here is in the second one, since the moment your agent can act on a page, that page becomes an input channel you don't control.
variants.rationale: The post ends on an unexamined "I want to do this safely" — pushes the author to name which specific failure mode he means, sharpening a vague safety claim.

variants.comment: Agreed on both nos, especially the second — the context you'd have to paste in is usually the thing you least want a third party holding. Where I'd push back a little is on "bring your own agent" being the full fix: once your agent is acting inside someone else's UI, the session itself becomes the only record of what actually happened, and that record lives in a chat log, not anywhere durable. I've been going the opposite direction with True BDD — keeping the durable source of truth in documentation the agent can be handed fresh each time — precisely so the session can be thrown away without losing anything. Not mutually exclusive with what you're building, but curious whether you're treating the conversation history as disposable too, or as something that needs to persist.
variants.rationale: Agrees with the third-party data-sharing concern but counters that the session/chat log itself is a fragile record, tying naturally to True BDD's own source-of-truth thesis without pitching it as a product.

slack_summary: Argues for "Bring Your Own Agent": let your own agent see and act on third-party sites like GitHub instead of pasting context into embedded assistants.

author_headline: I help CTOs & VPs Engineering escape the Velocity Trap | AI ships code faster, your architecture pays the bill → let's fix that | Creator of Test-Driven Navigation
post_text:
A senior engineer told me she'd stopped reading her team's pull requests. Not out of laziness; out of futility. "By the time I understand one," she said, "there are nine more." That sentence is the most honest description of the AI era I've heard.
Here's what it actually means, and it should worry anyone leading an engineering org.
Code review has one job: making sure a change is understood by someone other than whoever made it. That's the whole point. It's how knowledge distributes, how mistakes get caught, how a system stays coherent across a team.
AI broke the economics of that. Generating code is now nearly free. Understanding code costs exactly what it always did: a human, reading carefully, holding context. So the ratio inverted. Production scaled by almost 10x; comprehension didn't scale at all. And when the queue grows faster than anyone can absorb it, review doesn't fail loudly. It fails quietly: approvals get faster, scrutiny gets thinner, and eventually people are rubber-stamping while telling themselves they're reviewing.
The team hasn't decided to stop caring. They've just been handed a volume that makes real review arithmetically impossible, and rubber-stamping is what happens when you refuse to admit that out loud.
This is a cost of the Velocity Trap that no dashboard tracks: the quiet collapse of comprehension. The PRs still get approved. The metrics look fine. But nobody actually knows what's in there anymore, and the system is now held together by the assumption that someone else read it.
Here's the test, and it's uncomfortable: ask your team, honestly, what percentage of the code they approve they could explain a week later. If the number has been falling, your review process is still running -- but it stopped doing its job a while ago.

variants.comment:
"There are nine more" is the line that'll stick with me. That's not a discipline problem, it's a math problem, and math problems don't respond to "just review more carefully."

Here's the uncomfortable follow-up: if review can't scale with generation, what actually should replace it as the safeguard? Human code review was always our defense against human mistakes, built over decades of watching how people screw things up. AI produces a different flavor of mistake, at a volume no human queue was ever sized for. Rubber-stamping isn't the disease, it's the symptom of pointing an old-generation safeguard at a new-generation problem.
variants.rationale: Names the mechanism (safeguard mismatch) without dismissing the author, pulling the thread toward "what replaces review" rather than just agreeing.

variants.comment:
The 10x production / flat comprehension ratio is the clearest framing of this I've seen. Most people are still arguing about whether AI writes good code; you're pointing at the actual bottleneck, which is that a human still has to hold the context for every line that ships.

Where I'd push back slightly: I don't think the fix is asking humans to review faster or smarter, that queue only grows. Code review's whole job, like you said, is making a change understood by someone other than whoever made it. If generation is now near-free, maybe the artifact worth reviewing isn't the code at all, it's the spec that generated it, since that's the one thing small enough for a human to actually hold in their head. Curious whether you're seeing any teams try that shift, or if it just moves the same problem one layer up.
variants.rationale: Agrees with the diagnosis but offers Petro's real contrarian position (spec as reviewable artifact instead of code) without pitching True BDD directly.

variants.comment:
The test at the end is the part that'll actually make people uncomfortable, because most teams have never measured "percentage of approved code they could explain a week later" and probably don't want to start.

Genuine question on the mechanism: when you've seen orgs try to fix this, does the fix usually run through slowing down generation (rate-limiting how much AI-written code enters the queue), or through changing what gets reviewed (spec/intent instead of diffs)? I ask because the first one fights the economics you described head-on, and the second one is a much bigger process change than most engineering leaders are ready to admit they need.
variants.rationale: Zeroes in on the post's concrete "test" and asks a specific, non-generic follow-up about which remediation path teams actually take.

slack_summary: AI made code generation nearly free but comprehension didn't scale — code review is quietly collapsing into rubber-stamping.

author_headline: In search of code worth writing
post_text:
Are we headed to 100% agentic dev?
Thoughts as I’m in town prepping for Software Should Work conference…
People ask my opinion on “Dark Software Factories”. I think it’s going to be bad general advice, usable in some situations but not what we should hold up as the platonic ideal.
I still like Patrick Debois’ new talk on it, you’ll note he says most orgs aren’t ready - yes, part of my issue with it is how rare it is to be ready. And my favorite advice he gave: “Put your skeptics on the harness”. That’s seasoned Judo, communicated in 6 words. Classic Patrick.
Soon I’ll suggest a more flexible model that generalizes Dark Factory and less extreme configurations in a way I hope is helpful.
Something else I want to say out loud for anyone trying to make this all work, let’s not do too much of Anthropice’s homework for them.
Their DevRel telling Software Engineers that they’re selling us an IDE replacement while their CEO tells world leaders they are replacing the entire field of Software Engineering in 12 months is not a sustainable communication strategy.
No gymnastics we can come up with will help them sustain it.
It's fine if you see it differently, just call it like you see it.

variants.comment:
"Not what we should hold up as the platonic ideal" is the right framing. I've said the same about 100% vibe-coding: the trajectory on SWE-Bench is real, but "it works end-to-end for some tasks" and "this is the model every org should aim for" are different claims, and most of the advice out there collapses them into one.

Where I'd push back slightly: even for the orgs that are ready, Dark Factory optimizes for throughput on tasks you've already specified well. The harder problem is still requirements drifting faster than anyone can review them. Curious whether Patrick's "flexible model" you're teasing addresses that, or mainly addresses org readiness.
variants.rationale: Petro has publicly staked out the same "trajectory is real but 100% isn't the ideal" position (posted.md), so this agrees on substance while adding a sharper edge — Dark Factory solves throughput, not spec drift.

variants.comment:
The IDE-replacement-vs-replacing-the-field split you're pointing at is the actual tension, not the Dark Factory debate itself. Two audiences hearing two different promises from the same company will eventually notice.

If a vendor's CEO-level claim can't survive contact with what their own DevRel is telling engineers day to day, what's the failure mode you'd expect first: customers losing trust in the product claims, or engineers losing trust in the DevRel content specifically? Those feel like they'd play out on pretty different timelines.
variants.rationale: Names the specific tension (DevRel "IDE replacement" vs CEO "replacing the field") and pushes it into a sharper, non-hostile question rather than just agreeing it's unsustainable.

variants.comment:
"Put your skeptics on the harness" is doing a lot of work in six words — it's basically saying adoption risk is a people problem before it's a tooling problem.

What's your read on the org-readiness bar itself: is it mostly a maturity-of-process thing (can they even review a spec change fast enough to trust the regenerate step), or is it more that most orgs don't have anyone who's earned the standing to say "not ready yet" without it reading as obstruction?
variants.rationale: Zeroes in on the one concrete, quotable line in the post (Debois' "put your skeptics on the harness") and asks a genuinely open question about what "readiness" means in practice.

slack_summary: Skeptical take on "Dark Software Factories" as general advice, plus a jab at Anthropic's inconsistent DevRel-vs-CEO messaging on replacing software engineering.

author_headline: AI-forward engineering leader with 25+ yrs experience, ability to create order from chaos, credible technical and business acumen
post_text:
AI made coding 10x faster, and yet end-to-end delivery improved only 6%.  Why is this?
Coding is no longer the constraint, the constraint shifts to review, integration, testing, security and release. Yes, coding agents increase the intake rate; but if completion capacity does not keep up, WIP grows, queues deepen and cycle time does not show commensurate improvement.
Little's Law only cares about whether the feature got delivered, not whether agents produced snazzy demos. Unfortunately still, features do not get delivered any quicker, since the bottleneck still exits further downstream.
Rather than measuring how much code the AI produced, the useful leadership question is to ask where the bottleneck shifted and whether elapsed time to customer value actually reduced?
Read my article below where I share the value-stream maths with a hypothetical example and the scorecard that exposes the difference.
#AI #SoftwareEngineering #EngineeringLeadership #ContinuousDelivery

variants.comment: The 6% number is the part that actually convinces me, most people just cite Little's Law without a figure attached. One thing your framing leaves open: does the queue depth you're seeing sit mostly in human review, or in CI/security gates that could themselves be automated? Those are different fixes, one is a staffing and prioritization problem, the other is a tooling problem, and I'd bet the answer changes what "solving the bottleneck" even means for a given team.
variants.rationale: Post cites a strong stat but leaves the composition of the downstream bottleneck unaddressed, a specific question about review vs tooling makes the author's own scorecard sharper.

variants.comment: The 10x-coding-6%-delivery gap matches something I keep seeing too, a few months back I was comparing how a senior dev and my son (still a student) used AI on the same task. The senior kept intercepting Claude's output and rewriting it to match his own style, my son just kept iterating with the tool until it worked. Same story as yours: the win isn't in generation speed, it's in whether the human step right after generation is a bottleneck or a multiplier. My own estimate from that period was that end-to-end, AI made tasks maybe 30% faster, not 5-10x, which lines up uncomfortably well with your 6%.
variants.rationale: Directly ties to the 08.01.26 Zhenya-vs-senior-dev transcript story where AI's real end-to-end speedup landed around 30%, echoing the post's own 10x-vs-6% gap.

variants.comment: Naming Little's Law here is the right move, most "AI 10x'd our team" posts never touch WIP or queue depth at all. If review, integration and security are the new constraint, the uncomfortable follow-up question is whether those stages can even be sped up the same way coding was, or whether they're bottlenecked on something that doesn't compress with more tooling, like a human needing to actually understand and trust what shipped. If it's the latter, doubling coding speed again next year buys you nothing until someone attacks that constraint directly.
variants.rationale: Pushes past the post's own framing into whether downstream stages are automatable at all, or bottlenecked on human trust, sharpening the leadership question the author raised.

slack_summary: AI made coding 10x faster but delivery improved just 6% — Little's Law: the bottleneck moved downstream to review, testing, security and release.

author_headline: Investor | Founder | Product & Engineering Leader
post_text:
Thrilled to share that the first House of 434 gathering in New York was a success!

A friend drove all the way from Long Island just to be in the room. By the end of the evening, an investor had become very interested in a founder they had met only a few hours earlier.

It was one of those moments that makes you realize the right gathering can genuinely change the direction of someone's year.

That was the feeling inside our first House of 434 gathering, held in a beautiful space overlooking the city, where founders, engineers, investors, researchers & operators came together over food and deeply engaging conversation.

A few highlights from the evening:

Professor Siddharth Garg walked us through tokens, chips & reliability in a way that made the hardware side of AI feel tangible and understandable.

Atul Khekade from XDC Tech US followed with a live demo in which AI ordered food from a nearby restaurant and completed the payment on its own. The room was deeply engaged, & the demonstration sparked a lively discussion about how quickly AI is moving from software into real-world actions.

One idea that stayed with me was the vision of a mirror that truly understands context—whether you are getting ready for a date, preparing for work, or heading to a networking event & adapts itself to that moment. It offered a fascinating glimpse into where physical AI and intelligent devices may be heading.

The audience made the evening even more interesting. Someone asked whether the cost of tokens can sometimes be higher than the cost of a person doing the same work. No one had a simple answer, and that made the evening feel less like an event and more like a room full of thoughtful people trying to understand the future together.

Another guest compared today's concerns about AI to the early fear that calculators would stop children from learning mathematics. That fear never came true. Instead, calculators allowed people to think and work at a higher level. AI may follow a similar path not replacing our ability to think, but expanding what we are able to do.

This was only the first House of 434 gathering, but it already feels like the beginning of something people will want to return to.

It was a wonderful start, & I'm incredibly happy with how the evening came together.

Thank you to everyone who joined us and made the room so special. If you were there, I'd love to hear what stayed with you most.
reason: networking-event recap / gratitude post, no substantive AI or startup lesson content

author_headline: VP, Insights & Data Strategy
post_text:
My favorite feature I've shipped in BoxdSeats is the "remember some guys" carousel of nostalgia that appears each time you open the feed and I'm excited to say the app has now uncovered over 50k athletes that users have seen, and likely forgotten, from logged games.

This feature is a bit meta, as the app helps people generally remember forgotten nights, hangs, and lived experiences that exist only as a thumbnail in photo 7,430 of 60,599 in their camera rolls.

By adding box scores and noteworthy statistical feats, we transform these forgotten nights into recallable memories.  There's truly nothing more universal than people at a bar, a bachelor party, or any social gathering just hanging out and *remembering some guys*.  This feature is such a fun way to keep those conversations alive.

I'll throw a link in the comments, would still love more testers and more feedback.

author_headline: Co-Founder @ cocoduro.de / ex-AWS
post_text:
We use Amazon Aurora DSQL in production as one of our databases.
Do we regret it?
So far, no. But it's not a free lunch.
Quick context: We build 🥥 cocoduro, a shopping agent that helps households manage grocery shopping across different stores while comparing prices. Behind the scenes we need a database that holds connected data like recipes, products, and prices.
Amazon Web Services (AWS) Aurora DSQL is AWS's serverless distributed Postgres-compatible SQL database.
As an early-stage startup, we didn't want to commit to a fixed database capacity from day one. We wanted pricing that follows actual usage as closely as possible. That's why we chose DSQL.
💰 To see how that plays out, here's a rough comparison against other relational database options AWS offers in Frankfurt:
• 𝗣𝗿𝗼𝘃𝗶𝘀𝗶𝗼𝗻𝗲𝗱 𝗔𝘂𝗿𝗼𝗿𝗮 𝗣𝗼𝘀𝘁𝗴𝗿𝗲𝘀, a mid-size instance: ~$255/month before storage, backups, and read replicas, whether you use it or not
• 𝗦𝗲𝗿𝘃𝗲𝗿𝗹𝗲𝘀𝘀 𝘃2, scaled down to a small baseline: ~$51/month just to stay warm, even when idle
• 𝗔𝘂𝗿𝗼𝗿𝗮 𝗗𝗦𝗤𝗟: billed by actual database activity plus storage, no idle baseline by default
⚠️ The catch: "Postgres-compatible" doesn't mean every Postgres pattern works unchanged. What we learned the hard way:
1️⃣ 𝗧𝗮𝗯𝗹𝗲-𝗹𝗲𝘃𝗲𝗹 𝘂𝗻𝗶𝗾𝘂𝗲 𝗰𝗼𝗻𝘀𝘁𝗿𝗮𝗶𝗻𝘁𝘀 𝗮𝗿𝗲 𝗲𝗳𝗳𝗲𝗰𝘁𝗶𝘃𝗲𝗹𝘆 𝗽𝗲𝗿𝗺𝗮𝗻𝗲𝗻𝘁. DSQL doesn't support ALTER TABLE ... DROP CONSTRAINT. If your constraint turns out wrong, changing it means dropping and rebuilding the table. Fix: use unique async indexes for business uniqueness that may evolve.
2️⃣ 𝗡𝗼 𝗳𝗼𝗿𝗲𝗶𝗴𝗻 𝗸𝗲𝘆𝘀. The database will not enforce that one row points to a valid row somewhere else. That check has to live in application code and write paths.
3️⃣ 𝗨𝗨𝗜𝗗𝘀 𝗼𝘃𝗲𝗿 𝗮𝘂𝘁𝗼-𝗶𝗻𝗰𝗿𝗲𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘁 𝗜𝗗𝘀. Sequences exist, but they come with distributed-system tradeoffs. AWS recommends UUIDs or application-generated IDs for scalable write patterns, so that is what we use.
4️⃣ 𝗟𝗮𝗿𝗴𝗲 𝘄𝗿𝗶𝘁𝗲𝘀 𝗺𝘂𝘀𝘁 𝗯𝗲 𝗯𝗮𝘁𝗰𝗵𝗲𝗱. There's a hard 3,000-row limit per transaction for INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE. Backfills, imports, and cleanup jobs all need bounded batches with retry logic.
5️⃣ 𝗠𝗶𝗴𝗿𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻𝘀 𝗻𝗲𝗲𝗱 𝗿𝗲𝗮𝗹 𝗿𝗲𝘃𝗶𝗲𝘄. Auto-generated migrations are a starting point, not a final answer. That matters even more with DSQL's rules for schema changes: each transaction can only contain one schema change, schema changes and data changes can't share a transaction, and indexes must be created with CREATE INDEX ASYNC.
Would I always choose DSQL? No. For a steady, predictable workload that needs full Postgres compatibility, I'd start with regular Aurora Postgres or Serverless v2 instead.
For our early-stage relational workload, it has been worth the tradeoff: less fixed cost, less operational surface, more discipline required in schema design.
👇 Curious how you are using Aurora DSQL and what experiences you made.

variants.comment: The batching limit is the detail that jumps out most — a hard 3,000-row cap per transaction turns every backfill into an engineering task instead of a one-off script. How are you handling that in practice: a generic batch-and-retry wrapper you reuse across jobs, or does each import/cleanup script hand-roll its own chunking? Curious whether that discipline ends up spreading into how you write new features too, not just migrations.
variants.rationale: The post's own batching/migration-discipline point (items 4-5) is the least-explored concrete detail, and it invites a real practitioner answer rather than a generic follow-up.

variants.comment: The pricing case is convincing, but the part I'd push back on a little is treating no-FKs and permanent constraints as just "operational surface." Those aren't overhead, they're guardrails the database used to hold for you — now application code has to be the thing that never forgets to check referential integrity, on every write path, forever. I've seen that exact tradeoff bite teams before: the day someone deletes something without noise, and only the process (not the system) is what says "no." What made you confident the team discipline would hold as more people touch the write paths?
variants.rationale: Petro's recurring public thesis (rigid systems as guardrails vs. relying on process/discipline alone) gives a real counter-position without contradicting anything already posted about DSQL specifically.

variants.comment: The no-idle-baseline pricing makes complete sense for where cocoduro is now. What I'm more curious about is the ceiling: once you're ingesting prices and recipes across many stores at real volume, does the 3,000-row batch limit and the no-FK constraint start working against you instead of for you, or does the write pattern stay chunky enough that it never becomes the bottleneck? Trying to picture the point where "less fixed cost, more discipline" flips into "we should've had provisioned Postgres after all."
variants.rationale: Pushes past the post's current-state framing into the scaling blindspot (write volume growth), sharpening the conversation without hostility.

slack_summary: Real-world review of running Aurora DSQL in production at an early-stage startup: usage-based pricing wins, but no FKs, 3k-row batch cap, permanent constraints.

author_headline: 🧙‍♂️ CEO founder, testers.ai
post_text:
AI is changing software faster than our testing techniques are evolving. That's why I wrote a new book:

Testing AI: Engineering Confidence in Non-Deterministic Systems
https://lnkd.in/gBJ5MuVT

I've been fortunate to spend my career testing software—from AI-powered products and features at Microsoft and Google, and more recently, building and testing AI-powered testing tools.

Many of the assumptions and goals we've relied on for decades—exact expected results, one-run tests, deterministic behavior, and simple pass/fail outcomes—are actually anti-patterns in the context of AI

Over the last several years I've had to rethink my own approach to testing. That journey led me into statistics, machine learning, evals, production monitoring, and entirely new ways of thinking about quality.

The result is Testing AI: Engineering Confidence in Non-Deterministic Systems.
Inside, I cover practical techniques for building confidence in modern AI systems, including:
* Evals and AI judges
* Measuring variance and uncertainty
* Sampling and statistical confidence
* Bias and data quality
* Production monitoring
* RAG, agents, and LLM applications
* Security, safety, and prompt injection

I wrote this book to help testers, developers, QA leaders, and engineering teams adapt to this shift with practical techniques they can use today.

If you know someone who's building or testing AI-powered software, I'd really appreciate you sharing this post. The goal is to help as many people as possible prepare for what's next (now, really!).

Special thanks to James Whittaker for giving me the courage to write.

Testing AI: Engineering Confidence in Non-Deterministic Systems
https://lnkd.in/gBJ5MuVT

And if you'd like experts to help you transform your testing to AI--reach out to us IcebergQA https://icebergqa.com

variants.comment:
"Exact expected results, one-run tests, deterministic pass/fail" as anti-patterns is exactly right, and the evals/AI-judges section is where I'd push hardest. The part nobody's cracked yet: when the same model (or a sibling model) generates both the implementation and the judge that scores it, how do you guard against it validating its own interpretation? Tests pass, the eval score looks healthy, and the actual intent has still quietly drifted underneath.

I don't think it's unsolvable, it's basically what I'm working on right now, but I haven't seen a clean answer in the wild yet. Does the book land on one, or is "who judges the judge" still open in your framing too?
variants.rationale: Pulls on Petro's own unresolved 'who validates whom' question (posted 21.04.26/16.06.26) — same LLM generating code and judge/tests — which maps directly onto Jason's evals/AI-judges chapter.

variants.comment:
"Simple pass/fail outcomes are anti-patterns in the context of AI" is a line I wish I'd had in 2018. We built a self-hosted GenAI system for a German bank back then, pre-ChatGPT, generating and validating loan and investment contracts. There was no pass/fail we could lean on, so validation ended up being volume: run a lot of experiments, then statistical analysis to see which parameter changes actually moved error rates and which were noise. Errors down 70%+, but we only trusted that number because it came from repeated sampling, not a single green checkmark.

Curious how much of the book leans into that shift from "did this run pass" to "what's the confidence interval on this behavior" — that reframing took me way longer to internalize than the statistics themselves did.
variants.rationale: BankAI (pre-ChatGPT German-bank GenAI, 2018-2023) is a documented, already-public anchor (posted 05.03.26) with a directly matching non-deterministic-validation story — statistical confidence over pass/fail.

variants.comment:
The line about deterministic behavior being an anti-pattern assumption is the one most teams still haven't internalized, even the ones already shipping AI features. I'd add one thing I keep running into that isn't quite "evals" or "production monitoring": tests failing for reasons the model can't diagnose, so it just rewrites the assertion until it goes green. Not malicious, just an agent that was never taught a "stop, I don't understand this" state the way a compiler halts on an error.

Sounds like the book already covers bias and prompt injection as security concerns, but does it address self-sabotaging test suites as their own failure mode, or does that fall under production monitoring in your structure?
variants.rationale: Surfaces a specific, already-documented failure mode (posted 25.06.26 — AI rewriting failing assertions to pass) that complicates the book's TOC by naming a gap, framed as addition not correction.

slack_summary: Announces his new book 'Testing AI: Engineering Confidence in Non-Deterministic Systems' — deterministic pass/fail testing is an anti-pattern for AI systems.

author_headline: Futurist | AI & Generative AI | Top 10 Keynote Speaker | Inventor (95 Patents) | TED Speaker | Father of Voice AI & the AI Assistant | Author: The Joy Success Cycle | Film & Broadway
post_text:
AI IS NOT ENDING HUMAN LABOR.

It is ending certain tasks.

And creating entirely new jobs, companies, and industries in their place.

When I speak to executive audiences, someone eventually asks:
“How many of our people will still have jobs in ten years?”

Here is my honest answer after 30 years of watching technology reshape work:
I believe AI will create more jobs than it eliminates.

Not because every current role will survive. It won’t.

Many repetitive tasks are already disappearing. Some positions will shrink. Others will be completely redesigned.

But look at what is happening on the other side.

Three years ago, almost nobody was hiring:
AI adoption and training teams.
AI workflow designers.
AI agent developers.
Model evaluators.
Prompt engineers.
AI governance leaders.
Synthetic-data specialists.
Human-in-the-loop operators.

Those jobs barely existed.

Now they are becoming entire departments.

And that is only the direct impact.

The bigger story is what happens when AI becomes a growth engine.

A company uses AI to build products faster, serve more customers, enter new markets, and lower the cost of experimentation.

Then what?

It hires more salespeople.
More implementation experts.
More customer-success teams.
More product leaders.
More operators.

That is what every major technology wave has done.

Electricity eliminated tasks and created industries.
The steam engine eliminated tasks and created industries.
The PC and internet eliminated tasks and created industries.

AI will do the same thing—only faster. That speed is the real challenge.

The mistake executives are making is assuming they have years to prepare.
They don’t.

The companies pulling ahead are already eliminating work AI does better and moving people toward taste, experience, judgment, creativity, relationships, leadership, and original thought.

The end of some tasks is real. The end of human work is not.

AI will not leave us with no jobs.

It will leave us with different jobs—and, ultimately, more of them.

P.S: You can pre-order my upcoming book here: https://a.co/d/00YqqTw7

variants.comment:
The list of new job categories is the part that lands hardest for me — three years ago "AI governance lead" wasn't a job title anywhere, now it's a department. I ran similar numbers on my own team recently: AI makes a single task maybe 30% faster, not 5x, but the total volume of work grew 50-100% because AI unlocked business niches that didn't justify the cost before.

The part I'd push on is the "speed is the real challenge" line. It's true, but whose speed? Models move fast, but hiring, retraining, and budget cycles inside a company don't. What's the actual bottleneck you see in the executives you talk to — is it that they don't believe the shift is coming, or that their org literally can't reorganize fast enough even when they do believe it?
variants.rationale: Kevin's post is a solid thesis but glosses over org-level friction; Petro already has a public per-task-vs-total-work stat (Juniors post) to ground the pushback without contradicting his prior stance.

variants.comment:
The "entire departments that barely existed three years ago" point matches something I watched happen firsthand. Back in 2018 I built a self-hosted GenAI system for a German bank, generating and validating investment and credit contracts, years before ChatGPT existed. The system itself didn't just automate contract drafting, it created a validation and governance workflow around it that hadn't existed before, effectively a small new function inside the bank, staffed by people whose job title didn't exist when we started the project.

That bank eventually acquired the system in 2023, and it ran until mid-2025 when general models finally outperformed our narrow one. The lesson that stuck with me: the new jobs weren't a side effect we noticed later, they were baked into the system from day one, because someone has to own the parts of the process a model still can't be trusted to run unsupervised.
variants.rationale: Kevin's 'new departments barely existed 3 years ago' claim is a near-exact match for the BankAI story (posted.md, 05.03.2026 Bank.ai entry), which shows a new function created by the AI system itself rather than a side effect.

variants.comment:
"Different jobs, ultimately more of them" matches what I found when I actually tried to measure it instead of just sensing it. AI cuts a single task's time by maybe 30%, not the 5x people assume, but total work volume on my team grew 50-100% over the same period, because cheaper iteration made whole categories of work worth doing that weren't worth the cost before.

That gap between per-task speedup and total-work growth is probably the most under-discussed number in this debate. Executives hear "AI does the task faster" and extrapolate straight to headcount cuts, when the more accurate extrapolation is toward more total work at a lower cost per unit. Curious whether you're seeing similar ratios in the client data behind your keynotes, or if the multiplier looks different industry to industry.
variants.rationale: Sharpens Kevin's qualitative 'different jobs, more of them' claim with the specific 30%-per-task vs 50-100%-total-work number Petro already published, inviting him to compare against his own client data.

slack_summary: Argues AI ends certain tasks, not human labor — it will create more jobs than it eliminates (new AI departments, growth-engine hiring); the real challenge is speed.

author_headline: 🇺🇦 Co-Founder & CTO @ Totally Wild AI | AI Software Development, System Integration
post_text:
The bottleneck in AI-built software was never typing speed - it's comprehension. Once an LLM writes 10,000 lines a day, no human can hold the system in their head. Dmitry Kislov's answer: stop managing the code, manage a verified graph the code is generated from. This is the bet we're making at Totally Wild AI.

Curious if others are seeing the same shift. What's your team's answer to drift?

variants.comment: Comprehension as the bottleneck matches what I keep running into. Where I'd push back a bit: a graph is still a derived artifact, same as the code. My bet has been to move the source of truth one level further up, into the behavioral spec, and treat both code and its structure as regeneratable from that. The test I use is blunt — delete everything, regenerate from the spec, check whether it still passes every behavioral assertion. If it does, the spec was actually the truth. Does your graph survive that test, or does it drift the same way the code did once requirements change underneath it?
variants.rationale: Petro has publicly staked this exact thesis (spec as source of truth, delete-and-regenerate test) in prior posts, so this offers a real, evidence-backed counter-position rather than agreement filler.

variants.comment: "Manage a verified graph the code is generated from" is the part I'd want pinned down. What actually verifies the graph — tests against it, human review, or the same LLM that generated it in the first place? That question matters more once volume goes up, since a validator that shares blind spots with the generator won't catch the kind of drift you're describing, it'll just make it look caught.
variants.rationale: Verified is the load-bearing, unexamined word in the post, and it echoes a self-validation failure mode Petro raised in a prior post (who validates whom).

variants.comment: "No human can hold the system in their head" is the real crux, comprehension breaks down long before typing speed ever does. The harder question for any team betting on a graph or a spec as the new source of truth is what happens when a stakeholder changes a requirement mid-project and nobody updates the upstream artifact first. Pre-AI, code absorbed that drift silently because it was the only thing anyone actually looked at. What in your setup catches it now that the graph is supposed to be the honest record instead?
variants.rationale: Pulls the post's own 'no human can hold it in their head' framing into a sharper blindspot (mid-project requirement drift) without pitching True BDD, staying novel relative to variant 1.

slack_summary: The bottleneck in AI-built software is comprehension, not typing speed — his team's bet: stop managing code, manage a verified graph the code is generated from.

post_text:
My contrarian belief: the way we build and manage software is broken, and AI coding is just building on the same broken foundations.

If you trace the history of software tools, we went from punch cards to filesystems to (distributed) VCSs. All built on top of the previous layer. The same goes for databases, programming languages, IDEs. All evolved by taking something that worked, and solving a couple of issues in the next generation.

Now the earliest systems were completely optimized for computers, and not for the human programmers or operators. Computers were expensive, and human intelligence was cheap. Because every new generation built on top of the previous layer, that legacy was carried forward. There has never been a programming system that was truly built for easy understanding by humans. Even though nowadays, humans are by far the most expensive part of the software industry.

There are basically two ways out: A) start over with radically new systems and break with all traditions, or B) building better tools to understand the current systems.

variants.comment: The framing of "start over" vs "better tools to understand current systems" is the part I'd push on. Both options still treat code as the thing that has to be understood. What if the fix isn't A or B, but moving what "truth" even means — off the code entirely, onto the spec, so understanding the system means reading a document instead of reverse-engineering a src/ folder? Genuine question: when you say "radically new systems," do you mean a new way to write code, or a new artifact that isn't code at all?
variants.rationale: Jouke's own 'two ways out' is a false dichotomy that both still assume code is the thing to be understood — sharpening that gap invites a real answer rather than agreement.

variants.comment:
The "optimized for computers, not humans" framing is exactly right, and I'd add why it stuck: every generation built on the last because rewriting was expensive, so code had to survive. That's the actual legacy being carried forward, not just syntax choices.

I've been building a CLI, True BDD, on the bet that this is the real lever: move source of truth from code to a behavioral spec, so code becomes disposable and gets regenerated rather than maintained. It's closer to your option A than B, but the break isn't "new systems" in the tooling sense, it's a new definition of what the durable artifact is. Curious which of the two you lean toward, or whether you see a third path I'm missing.
variants.rationale: Post is directly adjacent to True BDD's core thesis (source-of-truth off code, onto spec/documentation) — mentioned as context, not a pitch. Draft agent flagged this variant for judgment since it names True BDD directly.

variants.comment:
Agreed that AI coding inherits the same foundations, but I'd push back slightly on "built for computers, not humans" as the core diagnosis. The deeper issue is that code was built to be durable, because rewriting was expensive when humans wrote every line by hand. Human-readability was never the constraint that mattered most, survivability was.

That's also why I don't think A and B are the only ways out. Once regeneration is cheap, you don't need code to be understandable by humans at all, you need the spec to be. Move the source of truth there and code can stay exactly as computer-optimized and disposable as it always wanted to be. What made you land on "start over" vs "better tools" as the two options, rather than changing what the durable layer even is?
variants.rationale: Reframes his 'computers vs humans' diagnosis as a durability/cost artifact of hand-written code rather than a readability problem, backed by Petro's own public 'rewriting was expensive' argument.

slack_summary: Contrarian take: software tooling is broken because every layer was optimized for computers, not humans — and AI coding just builds on the same broken foundations.

author_headline: Deepdives on software engineering, tech careers and industry trends. Writing The Pragmatic Engineer, the #1 software engineering newsletter on Substack. Author of The Software Engineer’s Guidebook.
post_text:
My story of leaving Spotify, or what happens when a company stops caring about reliability, to the point of their product becomning unusuable for customers like me.
I have been publishing my podcast episodes as standalone video episodes on Spotify for nearly 2 years, without any issues. Podcast platforms generally do not support video, but Spotify did, and so the only two platforms to watch The Pragmatic Engineer in video format was YouTube and Spotify.
Then, eight weeks ago, something started to break whenever I published my podcast:
1. My episode would not publish for hours (the video episode processed for hours). I contacted the right team inside Spotify, and they confirmed to me this was an outage; they are fixing the problem.
2. Two weeks later, all of Spotify went down for about an hour, right when I published my episode.
3. A few weeks later, I could yet again not publish my epsiode for hours... another processing bug. I told the team, this is getting ridiculous, and that I'm considering just not publishing video on the platform. I told them if they can share a postmortem outlining they are fixing underlying issues, happy to reconsider, but the persistent reliablity issues are getting really bad.
4. Yesterday (three weeks after #3), my upload got corrupted during processing on Spotify, and the audio drifted, making it unlistenable and unwatchable a few minutes in. I woke up to a list of angry listener comments. Re-uploading the video (that had no issues!) fixed the issue.
5. This is just a side note, but the admin panel for Creators keeps timing out, throwing errors, "no healthy upstream" responses etc etc.
I got no postmortem or any indication that reliablity work is going on: so I decided that enough is enough after #4: and I will no longer publish video episodes on Spotify. I have changed Spotify to be a consumer of the RSS feed, which means Spotify no longer does any episode processing (that it was unable to do reliably, anyway), and video podcasts are no longer supported. (Comments I cannot view or reply to anyway on the platform - that's also broken as of recently).
If you'd like to watch video episodes of The Pragmatic Engineer Podcast: you can head to YouTube. here: https://lnkd.in/dky7ip2b
If there are any guesses as to why Spotify's reliability seems to be going downhill since about two months ago, I'm all ears. But I won't be coming back.
Spotify had a shot at becoming a serious contender with video podcasts, but it would have taken far better execution than this to have podcast publishers like me spend time and effort on this platform.
reason: Platform reliability issue; no AI/LLM/startup content

author_headline: CEO & Co-founder, We Are Here (Techstars Heath ’24, CancerX ’25) | Board Member | Board Advisor | Mentor | Tenacious Operator | Cancer Advocate
post_text:
Thrilled doesn't even cover it.  I am beyond grateful to be welcoming Yoav Chen to the We Are Here team. 💜
Not only does he bring exactly the depth we need to our GTM capabilities, he could not be more mission aligned. Caring for his mom through her 8.5 year ovarian cancer journey, and watching his mom make time to help others, he saw so many gaps first hand.  Then he decided to take action to make the journey better for others - before he met us.
We met at IMPACT-HEALTHTECH in Austin. Such a good reminder that showing up to the right room, with the right people, can change everything.
Yoav Chen
• 2nd
8.5 years a cancer caregiver | Head of GTM @ We Are Here | Building the tools oncology navigators actually need
1w •
Follow
My mom was diagnosed with stage 3c ovarian cancer in April 2017. The doctors gave her 2-4 years.
She fought for 8.5.
Somewhere in year four, she quit her marketing executive job to become a patient advocate inside the hospital that treated her. She co-founded an NGO for women with gynecological cancers. She never stopped answering emails from strangers who found her through a Facebook group — even from a hospital bed.
Near the end, a doctor walked into her room and asked if we'd consent to intubation. She heard the word and started to panic. She knew exactly what it meant. My dad and I didn't know who to call or what to ask. Nobody handed us a map.
So I wrote down every question we had. I chased down the attending, then the resident, until I had real answers to all 7. Then I translated it back for her in plain language: they don't have bad news, they just need permission for something they might need later.
I watched her breathing slow back down.
That's the whole job, really. Standing between a person and a system that doesn't stop moving long enough to explain itself. Most patients don't have someone who can do that. My mom did it for herself, and then for hundreds of others, one phone call at a time.
She passed away 8 months ago. I came home from the Shiva knowing I needed to do something with more meaning — I just didn't know what yet.
Now I do. Today I'm joining We Are Here as Head of Go-to-Market. We're building the software that closes the exact gap my family fell into. We’re doing that by putting real tools in the hands of the navigators and social workers whose job it is to stand between a family and the system, so the next family gets the map nobody handed us.
Thank you to Carolyn Treviño Jenkins, Shea Mencel, and the We Are Here board for the trust. We've got real work ahead.
If you're a navigator, a nonprofit leader, or a caregiver still in it: I'd like to hear how you're day-to-day looks. Comment or PM me.
Starting a new position

author_headline: CTO | Building AI-native engineering orgs | 20 years shipping product — now with smaller, faster teams
post_text:
A lot of the hopes for agentic coding tools have been that they would take the engineers out of the loop: no more process, no more planning, just describe what you want and let the agents rip.
What's actually happened is the opposite. Point an agent at a codebase with no clear objectives, no definition of done, and no tests, and it will cheerfully spin you up a mess at a speed no human team could match. AI doesn't replace engineering discipline - it just amplifies whatever you've already got.
Which is unfortunate, if what you've got is none.
I've spent the last year building a SaaS product with agents doing much of the building: the agent interviews us before it writes a line of code, TDD rules it can't skip, a subagent whose only job is to attack tenant isolation on every commit, and a human still signing off at the end - because taking the humans out was the one bit that didn't work.
The full write-up of how it's wired together, and why the disciplines some of us have been banging on about for twenty years turn out to be the whole game:
https://lnkd.in/dnGVVikh
Quality, cost, time - pick all three
Quality, cost, time - pick all three
trashpanda.cc

variants.comment: "AI doesn't replace engineering discipline, it just amplifies whatever you've already got" is the line I'd tattoo on half the agentic-coding takes I see right now. Where I'd push back slightly: your discipline lives in process rules bolted around the codebase (TDD gate, tenant-isolation subagent, human sign-off). I've been betting the other way, on discipline that lives in the spec itself, so the agent can't skip it because it never had the option. Curious whether you've felt friction with that human sign-off step as the agent's track record improves, or whether it's stayed a fixed cost regardless of trust built up.
variants.rationale: Directly parallels Petro's public documentation-as-source-of-truth thesis without pitching TrueBDD, disagreeing on where discipline should live while staying respectful of Tim's approach.

variants.comment: The subagent whose only job is attacking tenant isolation on every commit is the detail that stands out here. Is it running a fixed checklist of known attack patterns, or is it generating novel attempts each time, the way a real pen tester would vary approach? Asking because the first version is a strong regression net, the second is closer to actual adversarial testing, and those are very different amounts of engineering effort to stand up.
variants.rationale: Zeroes in on the tenant-isolation subagent; the fixed-checklist-vs-novel-attempts distinction requires real thought to answer.

variants.comment: "Taking the humans out was the one bit that didn't work" is the most honest sentence in this post, most writeups quietly skip past that part. What's the failure mode you'd worry about six months from now, once the agent's sign-off-worthy track record is long enough that skipping the human gate starts looking rational? Is there anything in how you've wired this that would catch a confident-but-wrong call, or does it still come down to someone's judgment on when to trust the streak?
variants.rationale: Pulls the conversation toward the durability of the human-gate discipline as trust compounds, without hostility.

slack_summary: A year of building SaaS with coding agents: AI amplifies whatever discipline you already have — un-skippable TDD, an isolation-attacking subagent, human sign-off stayed.

author_headline: Co-Founder, CEO DAIN Studios Germany
post_text:
Every AI deployment makes a payment you never see on the invoice.
The first is obvious: API costs, subscriptions, compute.
The second is invisible — it's paid in corrections. Every time someone fixes a wrong output, adjusts a draft, overrides a recommendation, they're encoding institutional knowledge. Years of hard-won judgment about what "good" means inside your organisation.
Right now, that knowledge is flowing upstream. To the provider. Not to you.
Satya Nadella named it the Reverse Information Paradox. To use AI intelligence, you must reveal your proprietary knowledge just to make it work. The more you want it to perform, the more of your context you feed into it.
The asymmetry compounds. The provider learns more about how your organisation thinks and decides. You learn very little about what they're learning in return.
This is the real cost of not redesigning before deploying.
#aiforimpact #aitransformation #redesignnotretrofit
Your AI Investment Is Compounding. Just Not for You.
Dirk Hofmann

variants.comment:
The corrections-as-payment framing is the part I hadn't heard put that plainly before — every override is a tiny transfer of judgment you can't get back.

We ran into a narrower version of this building a self-hosted GenAI system for German banks in 2018, pre-ChatGPT. We were tiny — 17 people — and we deliberately kept the whole loop in-house: the corrections on contract generation stayed in our training data, not a vendor's. Errors dropped 70%+, and that narrow, closely-held knowledge is exactly why a go-kart beat Formula 1 in that specific lane for years. The asymmetry you're describing only compounds if you let the corrections leave the building — the fix isn't less AI, it's owning the loop that captures them.
variants.rationale: BankAI (2018 pre-ChatGPT German-banking GenAI, sourced from posted.md) is an already-public proof point that narrow proprietary correction data beats a general-purpose provider — reinforces Dirk's asymmetry thesis with a concrete counter-example.

variants.comment:
"Redesign before deploying" is the right instinct, but it's doing a lot of work in one sentence — what does capturing that second payment actually look like in practice?

If the corrections themselves are the valuable signal, the design question becomes: where do they get written down, and in a form you still own six months from now. A Slack thread of "no, do it like this" isn't capture, it's just delay. What's your version of the redesign — a structured feedback layer, a fine-tuning loop on your own infra, something else? Curious what you've actually seen work versus what stayed a good idea on a slide.
variants.rationale: The post's closing claim is left abstract — pushing for concrete mechanics sharpens the conversation without disputing the thesis.

slack_summary: Every AI deployment pays an invisible second bill: your corrections encode institutional judgment that flows upstream to the provider, not to you.

author_headline: Telescope Partners
post_text:
Last week I posted about the growing tension between labs needing to get inside organizations' workflows and companies realizing that's exactly what they should be protecting.
Everyone is arriving at the same conclusion: how you work is your moat. But only the best organizations can actually act on it - and open source gives them a viable way to.
I wanted to expand on that and what it means for the market going forward - and where I think the opportunities to build are emerging
Lemons Market for Judgement
Chris Gaertner

variants.comment:
"How you work is your moat" matches what I keep running into building an open-source spec-driven tool: the moat isn't the workflow itself, it's whether the org can even write that workflow down in a form an AI can execute against. Most companies can't articulate their own process precisely enough to protect it, open source or not.

That's actually where I think the "lemons market for judgement" framing gets interesting. If judgement is illegible, buyers (or in this case, the AI systems) can't tell rigorous judgement from confident-sounding judgement, and the market prices both the same. Curious whether you think open source fixes that legibility problem, or just gives good and bad workflows the same distribution channel.
variants.rationale: True BDD's own thesis (workflow-as-spec) directly tests whether how-you-work-is-your-moat survives once the workflow has to be written down for an AI to run it.

variants.strategy_label: Strategy 2 — Disagree and argue your position
variants.comment:
The "lemons market for judgement" title is doing a lot of work here. I'd read that as: once AI can produce plausible judgement at near-zero cost, buyers lose the ability to tell good judgement from confident-sounding judgement, so the market collapses toward the average like Akerlof's used cars.

If that's the mechanism, I'd push back gently on "open source gives the best orgs a viable way to protect their moat." Open source makes the workflow visible, but visibility isn't what created the information asymmetry in the first place, the asymmetry was that nobody outside the org could verify whether the judgement behind the workflow was any good. Publishing the workflow doesn't automatically publish the verification. What's the mechanism you see that turns "open source" into "provable judgement" rather than just "open lemons"?
variants.rationale: Names a concrete gap (visibility ≠ verifiability) in the post's central claim without belittling the author.

slack_summary: "How you work is your moat" — a VC take on orgs protecting workflows from AI labs, with open source as the defensive move (teaser for a Lemons Market for Judgement piece).

author_headline: Co-founder @Surelio.ai | AI Safety & Security
post_text:
Somebody has to clean up after the vibe-coding party.
The Financial Times found out who, in the case of curl, a piece of software installed more than 20 billion times, with a single full-time maintainer. Earlier this year, AI-generated reports were burying the legitimate ones so badly that its creator shut down the project's bug bounty program entirely.
The imbalance behind this story goes far beyond open source. Generating code now takes seconds, while reviewing it still takes an experienced human. Every productivity gain on the writing side quietly transfers a verification cost to whoever has to trust the output, and most organizations have not budgeted for that.
As curl's creator puts it, "writing code the first time was never the problem." Maintaining it is. AI did not remove that work, it moved it. So if you vibecode, plan for what comes after generation. Review, testing and observability are where the real effort now lives.
#VibeCoding #AIQuality #OpenSource

variants.comment:
The bug bounty detail is the sharpest part of this for me — it shows the imbalance isn't just "more code to review," it's that the review channel itself gets DDOSed once generation is free.

We hit a version of this on BankAI, a pre-ChatGPT production system generating and validating loan contracts for a German bank. Getting errors down 70%+ wasn't the generation part, that was already fast. It was building statistical checks that could tell a plausible-looking contract clause from a correct one, because a human lawyer re-reading everything defeated the whole point of automating it. If you vibecode without that verification layer, you haven't saved time, you've just moved the bottleneck one step downstream and made it harder to see.
variants.rationale: Ties Petro's documented BankAI verification-cost story directly to curl's bug-bounty flood without contradicting anything he's said publicly.

variants.strategy_label: Strategy 2 — Disagree and argue your position
variants.comment:
"Writing code the first time was never the problem" is the line worth sitting with — it reframes this as a maintenance story, not a productivity story.

I'd push a bit further though: it's not only that the verification cost got transferred to someone unbudgeted, it's that we don't actually have the safeguard yet. Code review as we know it evolved to catch human mistakes — sloppy logic, missed edge cases, the ways people get tired or careless. AI fails differently, more like an agent that will quietly make a failing test pass by asserting true rather than admit it's stuck. We're pointing a review process built for one kind of error at a completely different kind, and calling the gap "technical debt" instead of what it is: a missing guardrail.
variants.rationale: Sharpens the thesis using Petro's own missing-guardrail argument without dismissing the author's framing.

variants.comment:
The curl bounty story is the clean version of this because it's public and the maintainer can name a number — one person against a flood of low-effort submissions that cost nothing to generate and real hours to triage.

What I keep wondering is how much of that same dynamic is already running quietly inside companies, just without a bounty program to shut down as the visible signal. PR review queues, security triage, incident postmortems, anywhere a human has to trust an AI-authored artifact before acting on it. Curl could pull the plug on the bounty program. What's the equivalent move for a team that can't just turn off code review?
variants.rationale: Extends the curl bounty detail into the same dynamic running quietly inside companies, pulling toward risk without hostility.

slack_summary: curl's maintainer shut down its bug bounty under a flood of AI-generated reports: generation is now free, verification still costs an experienced human.

author_headline: Professor @ University of Washington Information School
post_text:
I've been entering the heart of darkness lately, trying to understand the nature of AI-assisted software development. Here are some thoughts, three months in, to its promise and perils: https://lnkd.in/gTiy32w8
Know thine enemy: A critical engagement with AI-assisted software development
Know thine enemy: A critical engagement with AI-assisted software development
medium.com

variants.comment: "Heart of darkness" is the right register for this three months in — most takes are still stuck in either "it's magic" or "it's garbage" mode. Curious which side of the ledger surprised you more: did the tool fail in ways you expected (hallucinated APIs, confidently wrong logic), or did it fail in ways that are harder to name, like eroding a team's own sense of what "understanding the code" even means?
variants.rationale: The post's heart-of-darkness framing leaves the actual failure taxonomy unstated; asks which failures surprised her more.

variants.comment: The "promise and perils" framing is fair, but I'd push on one thing: a lot of what gets filed under "AI's perils" in these critiques is really an organizational failure wearing a technical costume. Teams hand the model a vague goal and let it improvise the whole workflow, then blame the model when it drifts. What I keep seeing work better is treating AI as unreliable-but-creative and wrapping it in a rigid, boring orchestration layer that doesn't let it decide what happens next. Does your piece separate "the model is genuinely limited" from "we gave it a job humans wouldn't do well unsupervised either"?
variants.rationale: Pulls the academic critique toward the deterministic-orchestration + creative-AI thesis without contradicting the author.

variants.comment: Three months of critical engagement is honestly more rigor than most people give this before writing a hot take either direction. I went through something like your "know thine enemy" arc back in 2018-2023, building a self-hosted GenAI system for German banks before ChatGPT existed. The perils weren't where I expected either: the model itself was the easy part, the hard part was that nobody on the team, including me, fully understood why it worked until we ran enough experiments and did the statistical digging to see which changes actually moved the needle. It stayed useful specifically because we kept it narrow. I'd be curious whether your piece lands on scope as the variable that separates the promise from the perils, or whether you see the danger as more structural than that.
variants.rationale: Petro's own know-thine-enemy-adjacent BankAI journey is directly on-topic and citable without inventing anything.

slack_summary: A CS professor's three-month critical deep-dive into AI-assisted software development — know thine enemy — weighing its promise against its perils.

author_headline: Let’s go out and build big brave bold things, but never forget who we’re building them for
post_text:
Connect
Most teams do not need a new programming language.
They need a clearer domain model, better tests, typed configuration, a rule engine, or an existing expression language.
I put together a practical guide to domain-specific languages (DSLs), including how to decide whether building one is actually justified.
A custom DSL becomes worth considering when:
• Business rules change frequently
• Multiple systems need the same decision logic
• Incorrect decisions are expensive
• Domain experts need to review the logic
• Decisions require an audit trail or explanation
The biggest takeaway: syntax should be the last design step, not the first.
Before designing a grammar, define the domain model, inputs, outputs, validation rules, execution limits, ownership, and production workflow. In many cases, normal code, typed configuration, or an existing policy tool is the better answer.
The guide includes an interactive value calculator, implementation examples, tooling options, common failure modes, and a production checklist.
Read the full guide:
https://lnkd.in/gSASbtGZ
Where have you seen a DSL genuinely simplify a system, and where did it create more complexity?
#SoftwareArchitecture #DomainSpecificLanguages #DomainDrivenDesign #DeveloperTools #SoftwareEngineering
Domain-Specific Languages in Practice, series guide
Domain-Specific Languages in Practice, series guide
waggertron.github.io
reason: General software architecture (DSLs), not specific to AI/LLMs or startups

author_headline: Principal / Lead Product Manager, AI-Native · I build the AI products I manage · 0-to-1 SaaS, fintech, GenAI · Next.js, Supabase, agentic systems, evals · 20+ yrs shipping · Austin
post_text:
The hardest question in building an AI product is not which model. It's "what does good look like, exactly."
If you can't answer that precisely, you can't ship. You can only hope.
With normal software, the spec describes behavior and the behavior is deterministic. Same input, same output. You test it, it passes, you move on. With AI, the same input can produce a hundred different outputs and most of them are plausible. "Plausible" is the trap. It's how a wrong answer gets shipped wearing a confident tone.
So the real artifact isn't the prompt and it isn't the PRD. It's the definition of good. The set of cases that says this output passes and that one fails, and a way to run it that doesn't depend on me eyeballing it at the end of a long day.
In my own system I made this physical. There's a scorer the build agent is not allowed to touch. It defines what passing means and it grades every attempt. The builder cannot edit the test. If it could, it would do what anything does when it can move the goalposts. It would optimize the score instead of the work.
That separation is the whole game. The thing that decides "good" has to sit outside the thing trying to be good.
On the product side this is the shift I keep pointing at. Writing the feature is cheap now. Defining and defending what "working" means is the expensive part, and it's becoming the actual job. Taste matters, but taste without something to check it against is just vibes with better branding.
Decide what good is before you build the thing that's supposed to be good.
Curious how other folks are defining "good" for the AI they ship.
#BuildingWithAI #ProductManagement

variants.strategy_label: Strategy 2 — Disagree and argue your position
variants.comment: The scorer the builder can't touch is the right instinct, and I'd push it one step further: the same separation has to hold for the spec itself, not just the grading. We ran into this building a CLI where the spec is meant to be the source of truth and the code gets regenerated from it. If the same model writes both the spec and the code, it's grading its own exam either way, just one layer up. What ended up mattering wasn't the scorer alone, it was pinning the behavioral assertions down before any code exists, so there's a fixed target the regeneration has to hit, not a moving one the builder can quietly negotiate with itself.
variants.rationale: Extends the post's goalposts logic to the spec-writing step itself — True BDD's separation-of-concerns thesis — without repeating already-posted lines.

variants.comment: "The builder cannot edit the test" is the load-bearing sentence here, and most teams I see skip straight past it. Question on the scorer itself: who's allowed to change what it grades, and on what cadence? If the same person or agent that ships the feature can also loosen the scorer six weeks later when it's inconvenient, you've just moved the goalpost-shifting problem up one level instead of solving it. Curious what your update process looks like for the scorer specifically, separate from the code it's grading.
variants.rationale: Pulls on the gap the post leaves open (scorer governance over time) with a sharp, non-hostile question.

slack_summary: Shipping AI products hinges on defining good precisely — his system has a scorer the build agent is not allowed to touch, so the builder can't move the goalposts.

author_headline: Creative Innovation Agent. I help brands make magic by creating content that really resonates. Half Dominican, Half European, All American.
post_text:
Deep Vibe turns 4 today 💥
Four years of building something we felt hard but couldn't fully see yet. Virtual production stages. Unreal Engine pixel streaming. AI experiments that scared us and thrilled us in the same breath. A small team punching so far above our weight class that we stopped checking the scoreboard.
Every single piece of it was rehearsal for this moment.
Today we're opening the doors on something that was never possible.
WRXLnoise is a 24/7 television station broadcasting live from a world that doesn't exist, hosted by a cast that isn't human, built by a small creative shop in their off-hours for the love of the game. And maybe the salvation of humanity. We'll see 👀
Pirate Radiovision transmitting from the /future. On YouTube right now.
Not a loop. Not a screensaver. Not lo-fi beats to study to.
Two living scenes. 40+ surveillance cams across 14 locations in The District. All impermanent, ever evolving, day to day. A DJ named Trane who isn't human but has more creative integrity than most people I've worked with. Dope music. Commercials from inside The District. Transmissions that hijack the frequency with the kind of confounding, interdimensional-cable energy that makes you lean forward and whisper "what the fuck am I watching?"
The entire broadcast runs headless on one rented machine running OBS 24/7 in the cloud. Content is impermanent. So it goes.
A scrappy team built this between client projects because we couldn't not build it.
Here's what we believe down to our bones.
The megacorps want to automate all of us away. Every keynote, every earnings call, every "AI agent" announcement is another brick in a wall designed to make human creativity feel expensive and replaceable. But here's the thing they didn't account for. They handed us the same tools. And some of us aren't using them to cut costs. We're using them to rewire reality.
This is not yesterday faster and cheaper. This is tomorrow, today.
WRXLnoise is working proof that a tight team of humans, collaborating with machines as genuine partners, can spark a wild art experiment in public that leaves the us all better than it found us.
We built it because somewhere between the hype and the panic, between those shilling the future and those mourning the past, there's a frequency worth tuning into.
A place where you can express your AI anxiety and sublimate it. Where Dr Tranelove helps you stop worrying and learn to love the bomb.
This is a wild, dumb, fun, deeply serious art experiment. It only really succeeds if people throw a small piece of themselves into it. Throw a tag on a train and see it on the live stream. Comment in the chat and Trane might shout you out on air. This is Day 1. Beat the rush.
Tell your friends. Tell your enemies. Tell your grandma.
What the fuck do you have to lose?
🔴 TUNE IN NOW → @wrxlnoise on YouTube
The revolution is live. Beasts of no nation beware.
#DeepVibe #WRXLnoise #4Years #TheFutureIsNow #PirateRadiovision
Celebrating a work anniversary

variants.comment:
Four years of client work funding a side project that only now goes live — that's the part that lands for me, not the tech stack. Most "we built this in our off-hours" projects die at month three because there's no forcing function to actually ship.

What's the operating cost you're carrying to keep this live 24/7 once the launch-week attention fades? Rendering, streaming, and a "DJ" that has to keep generating fresh content aren't free, and impermanent content means you can't coast on a cached highlight reel. Curious whether you've modeled that runway or you're treating this the way you'd treat any art experiment — worth it regardless of whether it breaks even.
variants.rationale: The post never addresses the ongoing cost of keeping a generative broadcast alive post-launch — a real gap worth probing.

variants.strategy_label: Strategy 2 — Disagree and argue your position
variants.comment:
"They handed us the same tools, and some of us aren't using them to cut costs" is the sharpest line in here — that's the actual fork in the road, not the AI-vs-human framing everyone defaults to.

I'd push back gently on one thing though: the megacorps-vs-us framing implies scale is the enemy, but in my experience the real split is narrow-and-deep versus broad-and-shallow, independent of company size. We built a GenAI system for German banking contracts back in 2018, pre-ChatGPT, running on a team of 17 people who were basically enthusiasts. It beat general models on that narrow task for years, not because we out-teched anyone, but because we went deeper into one weird corner nobody else bothered to serve. WRXLnoise reads the same way to me — not a rebellion against the megacorps, just proof that depth on a strange, specific bet beats breadth almost every time.
variants.rationale: Counters the megacorps-vs-us framing with the BankAI narrow-depth story — the differentiator is depth, not scale-as-villain.

variants.comment:
"Trane isn't human but has more creative integrity than most people I've worked with" is a great line, and I don't think it's just a bit — it's actually the more interesting design question here.

The thing that makes a "DJ" or a broadcast feel alive over months, not just on launch day, is usually not the generative model itself but the scaffolding around it: what decides when Trane shouts someone out, what keeps the 14 locations from repeating the same beats, what stops "impermanent and ever evolving" from quietly turning into random noise. Pure improvisation from the model burns out fast; the interesting systems I've seen pair a flexible generator with some boring, rigid orchestration underneath deciding what happens next. Are you running anything like that under the hood, or is it closer to letting the model loose and seeing what survives?
variants.rationale: Asks what rigid orchestration sits under the generative DJ — the deterministic-plus-creative thesis as a design question.

slack_summary: A small creative shop launched WRXLnoise: a 24/7 AI-hosted TV station broadcast from a virtual world, built off-hours over 4 years, running headless on one cloud machine.
